questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a 45-pack-year smoking history and longstanding hypertension presents for a routine physical examination. On palpation, a pulsatile, nontender mass is noted in the periumbilical region. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a fusiform dilation of the infrarenal aorta measuring 4.2 cm in diameter. Which of the following is the most significant modifiable risk factor contributing to the development of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypercholesterolemia causing accelerated atherosclerotic plaque deposition in the aortic wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cigarette smoking causing direct proteolytic degradation of the aortic medial elastic fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic hypertension causing chronic elevation of transmural wall stress on the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sedentary lifestyle causing impaired vascular endothelial function and arterial remodeling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Excessive alcohol consumption causing chronic inflammatory changes within the aortic wall

explanation.summary: Cigarette smoking is the single most significant modifiable risk factor for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). Smoking activates matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) that degrade elastin and collagen in the aortic media, weakening the wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor for AAA development and expansion. It promotes chronic inflammation, activates MMPs (especially MMP-2 and MMP-9), and causes direct proteolytic destruction of elastic fibers in the tunica media, leading to progressive aortic wall weakening and aneurysmal dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While hypercholesterolemia contributes to atherosclerosis, which is associated with AAA, it is not the most significant modifiable risk factor. Smoking has a far stronger epidemiologic association with AAA than hypercholesterolemia alone.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypertension increases wall stress and contributes to aneurysm expansion, but it is a secondary risk factor compared to smoking in the pathogenesis of AAA. Hypertension is more strongly associated with thoracic aortic aneurysms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sedentary lifestyle is a general cardiovascular risk factor but has not been shown to be the most significant modifiable contributor to AAA formation compared to smoking.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Excessive alcohol consumption is not a well-established major risk factor for AAA. While it may contribute to hypertension, it does not directly cause the proteolytic degradation seen in aneurysm formation.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a known 5.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain radiating to the back, accompanied by hypotension and diaphoresis. His blood pressure is 78/50 mmHg, heart rate is 120 bpm, and he is pale and diaphoretic. Physical examination reveals a tender, pulsatile abdominal mass. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain a CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis to characterize the extent of rupture

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation and obtain serial abdominal ultrasounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Administer intravenous esmolol to reduce heart rate and blood pressure before intervention

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proceed to emergent open surgical repair or endovascular aneurysm repair of the aorta

choices.label: E
choices.text: Place an intra-aortic balloon pump to tamponade the site of rupture mechanically

explanation.summary: This patient presents with the classic triad of ruptured AAA: sudden severe abdominal/back pain, hypotension, and pulsatile abdominal mass. Emergent surgical intervention is the definitive management.
explanation.whyCorrect: A hemodynamically unstable patient with a known large AAA presenting with the classic rupture triad requires immediate emergent surgical repair (open or endovascular). Delay for imaging or other interventions in a hemodynamically unstable patient with a clear diagnosis significantly increases mortality.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT angiography may be appropriate in hemodynamically stable patients with suspected rupture to plan the repair, but in a patient with hypotension and a known large AAA, delaying surgery for imaging increases mortality risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While fluid resuscitation is initiated, permissive hypotension is preferred to avoid disrupting any retroperitoneal clot. Serial ultrasounds are inappropriate as the diagnosis is clinically apparent, and the patient needs emergent surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anti-impulse therapy with beta-blockers is used in aortic dissection, not in ruptured AAA. Administering an anti-hypertensive to a hypotensive patient would worsen hemodynamic instability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An intra-aortic balloon pump is used for cardiogenic shock, not for tamponading a ruptured AAA. This would not address the vascular injury.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old tall, thin man with pectus excavatum and bilateral lens subluxation is found to have an ascending aortic root diameter of 4.5 cm on echocardiography. Genetic testing confirms a mutation in the FBN1 gene. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of aortic root dilation in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excessive activation of transforming growth factor-beta signaling leading to cystic medial degeneration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque formation in the aortic root causing intimal erosion and wall weakening

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic inflammatory infiltrate destroying smooth muscle cells in the tunica media of the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deposition of amyloid fibrils within the aortic wall causing loss of structural integrity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immune complex deposition causing necrotizing vasculitis of the vasa vasorum of the aorta

explanation.summary: This patient has Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation). Defective fibrillin-1 leads to excessive TGF-beta signaling, which causes cystic medial degeneration (loss of elastic fibers and smooth muscle cells with mucoid accumulation) in the aortic wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: Marfan syndrome results from mutations in FBN1, encoding fibrillin-1, a glycoprotein essential for elastic fiber formation. Defective fibrillin-1 leads to impaired sequestration of TGF-beta in the extracellular matrix, resulting in excessive TGF-beta signaling. This drives cystic medial degeneration (also called cystic medial necrosis), characterized by fragmentation of elastic lamellae, smooth muscle cell apoptosis, and accumulation of mucoid ground substance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atherosclerosis primarily affects the infrarenal abdominal aorta and occurs in older patients with typical cardiovascular risk factors. This young patient's ascending aortic dilation is due to a connective tissue disorder, not atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic inflammatory infiltrates are characteristic of inflammatory aortic aneurysms (e.g., Takayasu arteritis, giant cell arteritis) or infectious (mycotic) aneurysms, not Marfan syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amyloid deposition can affect the heart causing restrictive cardiomyopathy but is not a recognized cause of aortic root dilation in Marfan syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immune complex-mediated necrotizing vasculitis is seen in conditions such as polyarteritis nodosa, not in Marfan syndrome, which is a structural connective tissue disorder.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man is found to have an asymptomatic infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm measuring 4.8 cm on screening ultrasound. He has well-controlled hypertension and quit smoking 10 years ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Schedule elective open surgical repair within the next two weeks to prevent rupture

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refer for endovascular aneurysm repair given the aneurysm exceeds the intervention threshold

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate surveillance with repeat imaging every 6 to 12 months and optimize cardiovascular risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescribe doxycycline to inhibit matrix metalloproteinases and slow aneurysm progression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform diagnostic aortography to evaluate for involvement of the renal and mesenteric arteries

explanation.summary: An asymptomatic AAA measuring less than 5.5 cm in men is managed with surveillance imaging and cardiovascular risk factor modification. Surgical repair is indicated when the aneurysm reaches 5.5 cm or grows more than 0.5 cm in 6 months.
explanation.whyCorrect: Current guidelines recommend surveillance for asymptomatic AAAs less than 5.5 cm in diameter in men. This includes repeat imaging (ultrasound or CT) every 6-12 months (depending on size), along with cardiovascular risk factor optimization including blood pressure control, statin therapy, and smoking cessation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Open surgical repair is not indicated for an asymptomatic AAA measuring 4.8 cm. The threshold for elective repair is generally 5.5 cm in men. Premature surgery exposes the patient to unnecessary perioperative risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The aneurysm has not exceeded the intervention threshold of 5.5 cm for men. Endovascular repair would be premature at 4.8 cm in an asymptomatic patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although doxycycline has been studied as an MMP inhibitor to slow aneurysm growth, clinical trials have not demonstrated sufficient benefit, and it is not a standard recommendation for AAA management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diagnostic aortography is invasive and not indicated for routine evaluation of an asymptomatic, small AAA. CT angiography or MR angiography is preferred for preoperative planning when repair is indicated.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with hoarseness and dysphagia. Chest X-ray reveals a widened mediastinum. CT angiography shows a saccular aneurysm of the distal aortic arch compressing the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. Which of the following findings is most likely to be present on laryngoscopy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral vocal cord paralysis with the cords fixed in the paramedian position

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right vocal cord paralysis with the cord fixed in the abducted position laterally

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left vocal cord paralysis with the cord fixed in the paramedian position

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral vocal cord nodules causing impaired vocal cord adduction bilaterally

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supraglottic edema with epiglottic swelling causing narrowing of the airway lumen

explanation.summary: The left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch. An aneurysm of the distal aortic arch can compress this nerve, causing left vocal cord paralysis (Ortner syndrome/cardiovocal syndrome), resulting in hoarseness.
explanation.whyCorrect: The left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks under the aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum before ascending to innervate the intrinsic muscles of the larynx (except the cricothyroid). Compression by a distal aortic arch aneurysm causes left vocal cord paralysis, with the cord typically fixed in the paramedian position, producing hoarseness.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bilateral vocal cord paralysis would require bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. A distal aortic arch aneurysm would only compress the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, not the right, which loops under the right subclavian artery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The right recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the right subclavian artery, not the aortic arch. It would not be affected by a distal aortic arch aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vocal cord nodules are caused by chronic voice overuse (singer's nodules) and are unrelated to nerve compression by a vascular aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Supraglottic edema with epiglottic swelling suggests epiglottitis or angioedema, which are not caused by aortic arch aneurysms.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man who underwent endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) for an infrarenal AAA 6 months ago presents for follow-up CT angiography. Imaging reveals persistent blood flow into the aneurysm sac through a patent lumbar artery, with the stent graft in good position. The aneurysm sac has increased in size by 3 mm since the last scan. Which of the following types of endoleak is most likely present?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type I endoleak due to inadequate seal at the proximal or distal graft attachment site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type II endoleak due to retrograde flow through a branch vessel into the aneurysm sac

choices.label: C
choices.text: Type III endoleak due to a structural defect or separation of graft components

choices.label: D
choices.text: Type IV endoleak due to graft porosity allowing blood to seep through the graft material

choices.label: E
choices.text: Type V endoleak (endotension) due to expansion of the sac without a demonstrable leak

explanation.summary: A type II endoleak involves retrograde flow from branch arteries (lumbar, inferior mesenteric artery) into the aneurysm sac. It is the most common type of endoleak after EVAR.
explanation.whyCorrect: The imaging shows persistent flow into the aneurysm sac via a patent lumbar artery with the stent graft in good position, which is the hallmark of a type II endoleak. Type II endoleaks involve retrograde blood flow through aortic branch vessels (lumbar arteries or inferior mesenteric artery) into the aneurysm sac. They are the most common endoleak after EVAR and require intervention if the sac is expanding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type I endoleak occurs when there is an inadequate seal between the stent graft and the aortic wall at the proximal or distal attachment sites. The question states the graft is in good position, making this unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type III endoleak results from structural failure of the graft, such as tears in the graft fabric or disconnection of modular components. The graft is described as being in good position without defects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type IV endoleak is caused by porosity of the graft material, allowing blood to seep through. This is typically seen within the first 30 days after implantation and resolves as the graft incorporates, not 6 months later.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Type V endoleak (endotension) refers to sac expansion without a demonstrable source of blood flow on imaging. In this case, the source (lumbar artery) is clearly identified.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension and bicuspid aortic valve presents with exertional dyspnea. Echocardiography reveals an ascending aortic diameter of 5.2 cm with moderate aortic regurgitation. His father died of aortic rupture at age 52. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue medical surveillance with repeat echocardiography in 12 months as the threshold is 5.5 cm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate aggressive antihypertensive therapy with a target blood pressure below 120/80 mmHg only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for prophylactic surgical repair of the ascending aorta given current size and risk factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescribe losartan to slow the rate of aortic dilation and defer surgical intervention indefinitely

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recommend genetic testing before making any surgical decisions regarding the ascending aorta

explanation.summary: In patients with bicuspid aortic valve, the threshold for prophylactic ascending aortic repair is lowered to 5.0 cm (especially with additional risk factors such as family history of aortic dissection/rupture or rapid growth rate). This patient exceeds that threshold.
explanation.whyCorrect: For patients with bicuspid aortic valve, guidelines recommend surgical repair of the ascending aorta at 5.0 cm if additional risk factors are present (family history of aortic catastrophe, rapid growth >0.5 cm/year, or coarctation). This patient has a 5.2 cm ascending aneurysm with a bicuspid valve and a strong family history of aortic rupture, meeting criteria for prophylactic repair.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The 5.5 cm threshold applies to patients with degenerative ascending aortic aneurysms without additional risk factors. In bicuspid aortic valve with a family history of aortic rupture, the surgical threshold is lowered to 5.0 cm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While blood pressure control is important, medical therapy alone is insufficient given that the patient has already exceeded the surgical threshold with significant risk factors. Surgery should not be deferred.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Losartan has been studied in Marfan syndrome for slowing aortic dilation, but it is not a substitute for surgical repair when the indication threshold has been met. Indefinite deferral is inappropriate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While genetic testing may be informative, delaying surgical decision-making is not appropriate when the patient already meets criteria for repair based on aneurysm size, bicuspid valve, and family history.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of fever, malaise, and back pain. Blood cultures grow Salmonella species. CT angiography demonstrates a saccular aneurysm of the infrarenal aorta with periaortic soft tissue enhancement and gas. Which of the following terms best describes this type of aneurysm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerotic aneurysm caused by chronic intimal plaque deposition and medial thinning

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inflammatory aneurysm characterized by thick fibrotic periaortic rind with retroperitoneal fibrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mycotic aneurysm caused by direct bacterial infection and destruction of the aortic wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pseudoaneurysm caused by disruption of all three layers of the aortic wall after trauma

choices.label: E
choices.text: Degenerative aneurysm resulting from age-related loss of medial elastin and smooth muscle

explanation.summary: A mycotic (infected) aneurysm is caused by bacterial infection of the aortic wall. Salmonella is a classic causative organism. The saccular morphology, periaortic enhancement, and gas are hallmarks of an infected aneurysm.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mycotic aneurysms (a misnomer, as they are usually bacterial, not fungal) result from direct bacterial seeding of the aortic wall via bacteremia or contiguous infection. Salmonella species are classically associated with mycotic aortic aneurysms, particularly in patients with preexisting atherosclerotic disease. Saccular morphology, periaortic inflammation, and gas on CT are characteristic findings.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atherosclerotic aneurysms are typically fusiform, develop gradually, and are not associated with fever, positive blood cultures, or periaortic gas. They result from chronic plaque deposition rather than infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inflammatory aneurysms are characterized by a thick, fibrotic periaortic rind and often present with an elevated ESR. They are not associated with bacteremia or periaortic gas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A pseudoaneurysm involves disruption of the intima and media with containment by the adventitia or surrounding tissue. It is typically caused by trauma or prior vascular intervention, not bacterial infection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Degenerative aneurysms are related to aging and loss of medial wall integrity but are not associated with infection, fever, or periaortic gas.

questions.stem: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends one-time screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm using ultrasonography for a specific population. Which of the following patients meets the criteria for this recommended screening?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 55-year-old woman who has smoked one pack of cigarettes per day for the past 30 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: A 65-year-old man who has never smoked but whose father had a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm

choices.label: C
choices.text: A 68-year-old man who has smoked at least 100 cigarettes in his lifetime and has never been screened

choices.label: D
choices.text: A 45-year-old man with Marfan syndrome and a known family history of thoracic aortic aneurysm

choices.label: E
choices.text: A 72-year-old woman with hypertension and peripheral arterial disease but no history of smoking

explanation.summary: The USPSTF recommends one-time screening for AAA with ultrasonography in men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked (defined as having smoked at least 100 cigarettes in their lifetime).
explanation.whyCorrect: The USPSTF gives a B recommendation for one-time screening for AAA with ultrasonography in men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked. 'Ever smoked' is defined as having smoked at least 100 cigarettes. This 68-year-old male smoker meets both the age and smoking criteria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The USPSTF concludes that the current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for AAA in women who have ever smoked. There is no recommendation for screening women in this age group.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While family history increases AAA risk, the USPSTF screening recommendation specifically targets men aged 65-75 who have ever smoked. For never-smokers, the recommendation is selective (C recommendation), requiring individual clinical judgment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A 45-year-old man with Marfan syndrome would need surveillance of the thoracic aorta (echocardiography), not USPSTF-recommended abdominal aortic screening, which is age-specific and smoking-related.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The USPSTF does not recommend routine AAA screening for women who have never smoked, regardless of other cardiovascular risk factors.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a 6.0-cm infrarenal AAA undergoes evaluation for endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR). CT angiography shows that the infrarenal aortic neck is only 8 mm in length with significant angulation greater than 60 degrees. Which of the following is the most appropriate surgical approach for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proceed with standard EVAR using a bifurcated stent graft with infrarenal fixation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perform open surgical repair with prosthetic graft interposition through a midline laparotomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place a covered aortic stent without fixation to allow for spontaneous seal of the aneurysm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer surgical intervention and manage with serial imaging every three months instead

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform percutaneous embolization of the aneurysm sac with thrombogenic coils

explanation.summary: EVAR requires adequate proximal neck anatomy (length greater than 10-15 mm, angulation less than 60 degrees) for secure graft fixation. This patient has unfavorable anatomy for EVAR, making open surgical repair the preferred approach.
explanation.whyCorrect: Successful EVAR requires adequate infrarenal aortic neck length (typically at least 10-15 mm) and acceptable angulation (less than 60 degrees) for a reliable seal. This patient's 8 mm neck length and greater than 60-degree angulation preclude standard EVAR. Open surgical repair with a prosthetic tube or bifurcated graft via midline laparotomy is the appropriate alternative.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Standard EVAR requires a minimum infrarenal neck length of 10-15 mm and angulation less than 60 degrees for adequate seal. This patient's hostile neck anatomy (8 mm, >60 degrees) would result in a high risk of type I endoleak.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A covered stent without fixation would not provide a durable seal and would be prone to migration and endoleak. This is not a recognized approach for AAA repair.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A 6.0-cm AAA exceeds the 5.5-cm threshold for elective repair. Deferring intervention would expose the patient to an unacceptable risk of rupture (approximately 10-20% per year for aneurysms greater than 6 cm).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Percutaneous coil embolization is used for specific vascular lesions (e.g., type II endoleaks, visceral artery aneurysms) but is not a treatment for an intact AAA requiring definitive repair.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type IV (vascular type) presents with sudden-onset flank pain and hematuria. CT angiography reveals a ruptured renal artery aneurysm. Which of the following genetic defects is responsible for the vascular fragility in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mutation in the FBN1 gene resulting in defective fibrillin-1 synthesis within elastic fibers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mutation in the COL3A1 gene resulting in defective type III procollagen production

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mutation in the TGFBR2 gene resulting in abnormal transforming growth factor-beta signaling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mutation in the ACTA2 gene resulting in defective smooth muscle alpha-actin production

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mutation in the MYH11 gene resulting in defective smooth muscle myosin heavy chain function

explanation.summary: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (type IV) is caused by mutations in COL3A1, which encodes type III procollagen, a major structural component of blood vessel walls, hollow organs, and skin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type IV (vascular type) is caused by autosomal dominant mutations in COL3A1, leading to defective type III collagen synthesis. Type III collagen is a critical structural component of arterial walls, and its deficiency leads to extreme vascular fragility, spontaneous arterial rupture, aneurysm formation, and visceral organ perforation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: FBN1 mutations cause Marfan syndrome, which is characterized by aortic root dilation, lens subluxation, and skeletal abnormalities, not the vascular fragility pattern seen in vascular EDS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TGFBR2 mutations cause Loeys-Dietz syndrome, which presents with aortic aneurysms, arterial tortuosity, hypertelorism, and bifid uvula, not classic vascular EDS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ACTA2 mutations are associated with familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection syndrome, livedo reticularis, and cerebrovascular disease, not vascular EDS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MYH11 mutations are associated with familial thoracic aortic aneurysm with patent ductus arteriosus, not Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man undergoes elective repair of a 5.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm. During surgery, the aneurysm sac is opened and a prosthetic graft is placed. Postoperatively, the patient develops bloody diarrhea and left lower quadrant abdominal pain. Colonoscopy reveals patchy mucosal ischemia of the sigmoid colon with pale mucosa and petechial hemorrhages. Which of the following arteries was most likely compromised during the repair?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superior mesenteric artery arising from the anterior surface of the aorta at the level of L1

choices.label: B
choices.text: Celiac trunk arising from the anterior surface of the aorta at the level of T12

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inferior mesenteric artery arising from the anterior surface of the aorta at the level of L3

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right colic artery arising as a branch of the superior mesenteric artery supplying the ascending colon

choices.label: E
choices.text: Middle colic artery arising as a branch of the superior mesenteric artery supplying the transverse colon

explanation.summary: Sigmoid colon ischemia is a recognized complication of AAA repair, most commonly due to ligation or exclusion of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), which supplies the left colon via the left colic and sigmoid arteries.
explanation.whyCorrect: The IMA arises from the aorta at L3 and supplies the descending and sigmoid colon. During AAA repair, the IMA origin is often incorporated within the aneurysm and may be ligated or excluded by the graft. If collateral flow from the SMA via the marginal artery of Drummond or arc of Riolan is inadequate, sigmoid colon ischemia results, presenting as bloody diarrhea and left lower quadrant pain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The SMA arises at L1, above the level of most infrarenal AAAs, and is not typically compromised during infrarenal AAA repair. SMA occlusion would cause small bowel and right colon ischemia, not isolated sigmoid ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The celiac trunk arises at T12, well above the infrarenal aorta, and is not at risk during infrarenal AAA repair. Its occlusion would cause foregut ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The right colic artery is a branch of the SMA supplying the ascending colon. It is not involved in infrarenal AAA repair and would not cause sigmoid colon ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The middle colic artery is a branch of the SMA supplying the transverse colon. Its compromise would cause transverse colon ischemia, not sigmoid ischemia.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset, severe, tearing chest pain radiating to the back. His blood pressure is 210/120 mmHg in the right arm and 170/95 mmHg in the left arm. A diastolic decrescendo murmur is heard at the left sternal border. CT angiography reveals an intimal flap originating 2 cm above the aortic valve and extending to the descending aorta. Which of the following is the correct classification of this dissection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stanford type B dissection involving only the descending aorta distal to the left subclavian

choices.label: B
choices.text: DeBakey type II dissection confined entirely to the ascending aorta without distal extension

choices.label: C
choices.text: DeBakey type I dissection originating in the ascending aorta and extending into the descending aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: DeBakey type III dissection involving only the descending aorta with retrograde extension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stanford type B dissection involving the aortic arch without ascending aortic involvement

explanation.summary: This dissection originates in the ascending aorta and extends to the descending aorta, making it a DeBakey type I (or Stanford type A) dissection. DeBakey type I involves both the ascending and descending aorta.
explanation.whyCorrect: DeBakey type I dissection originates in the ascending aorta and propagates distally to involve the aortic arch and descending aorta. This is equivalent to Stanford type A (any dissection involving the ascending aorta). The intimal tear 2 cm above the aortic valve with extension to the descending aorta fits this classification.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stanford type B dissections involve only the descending aorta distal to the left subclavian artery and do not involve the ascending aorta. This patient's dissection clearly involves the ascending aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.B: DeBakey type II dissections are confined to the ascending aorta only. This patient's dissection extends into the descending aorta, ruling out type II.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DeBakey type III dissections originate in the descending aorta distal to the left subclavian artery. This dissection originates in the ascending aorta, not the descending aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stanford type B does not include any ascending aortic involvement. Additionally, dissections involving the arch without ascending involvement are uncommon and would still be classified differently.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man presents with acute-onset tearing chest pain radiating to the interscapular region. CT angiography confirms a Stanford type A aortic dissection. While preparing for emergent surgery, the patient becomes hypotensive with muffled heart sounds and distended neck veins. Electrocardiogram shows low-voltage QRS complexes. Which of the following complications has most likely occurred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute myocardial infarction due to dissection extending into the left main coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac tamponade due to hemopericardium from rupture of the dissection into the pericardial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tension pneumothorax due to rupture of the dissection into the left pleural space

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute aortic regurgitation due to prolapse of the dissection flap into the aortic valve annulus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Massive hemothorax due to rupture of the dissection through the posterior aortic wall

explanation.summary: Beck's triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD) with low-voltage QRS complexes indicates cardiac tamponade. In type A dissection, this results from rupture of the proximal dissection into the pericardial space causing hemopericardium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension constitutes Beck's triad, pathognomonic for cardiac tamponade. Low-voltage QRS complexes on ECG further support this diagnosis. In Stanford type A dissection, the proximal false lumen can rupture through the adventitia into the pericardial space, causing acute hemopericardium and tamponade. This is the most common cause of death in type A dissection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary malperfusion from dissection can cause acute MI (most commonly involving the right coronary artery), but it would not produce Beck's triad or low-voltage QRS complexes. ST elevations would be expected on ECG.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tension pneumothorax causes hypotension and JVD but produces hyperresonance to percussion, absent breath sounds, and tracheal deviation, not muffled heart sounds and low-voltage QRS complexes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acute aortic regurgitation causes a widened pulse pressure, diastolic murmur, and pulmonary edema, but it does not produce muffled heart sounds, JVD, or low-voltage QRS complexes characteristic of tamponade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Massive hemothorax would cause hypotension and dyspnea with dullness to percussion and absent breath sounds on the affected side, not the tamponade physiology described.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with no prior medical history presents with acute severe chest pain. CT angiography reveals a Stanford type A aortic dissection. The patient has no connective tissue disease and no history of hypertension. On examination, he is 185 cm tall with an arm span of 195 cm, arachnodactyly, and positive wrist and thumb signs. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology of his aortic dissection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic undiagnosed hypertension causing accelerated medial degeneration of the aortic wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cocaine use causing acute hypertensive surge and catecholamine-mediated vasospasm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Marfan syndrome with cystic medial degeneration due to defective fibrillin-1 microfibrils

choices.label: D
choices.text: Giant cell arteritis causing granulomatous inflammation and weakening of the aortic media

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tertiary syphilis causing obliterative endarteritis of the vasa vasorum of the ascending aorta

explanation.summary: This young patient has classic features of Marfan syndrome (tall stature, arm span exceeding height, arachnodactyly, positive wrist and thumb signs). Marfan syndrome predisposes to aortic dissection through cystic medial degeneration.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient demonstrates hallmark Marfan syndrome features: disproportionately tall stature, arm span-to-height ratio >1.05, arachnodactyly, and positive wrist (Walker) and thumb (Steinberg) signs. Marfan syndrome, caused by FBN1 mutations leading to defective fibrillin-1, results in cystic medial degeneration of the aortic wall, dramatically increasing the risk of aortic dissection, particularly type A.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The question states no history of hypertension, and chronic hypertension would not explain the marfanoid habitus and skeletal findings. While hypertension is the most common risk factor overall, the clinical features point to a connective tissue disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cocaine use causes acute hypertensive crises and can precipitate aortic dissection, but it would not explain the patient's skeletal findings (arachnodactyly, increased arm span, positive wrist and thumb signs).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Giant cell arteritis typically affects patients over 50 years old, involves the temporal arteries and aorta, and presents with headache, jaw claudication, and elevated inflammatory markers, not marfanoid features.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Syphilitic aortitis involves the ascending aorta and causes aneurysm formation through vasa vasorum destruction, but it does not cause the skeletal findings seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with hypertension presents with sudden-onset severe interscapular pain. CT angiography shows a Stanford type B aortic dissection originating just distal to the left subclavian artery without evidence of malperfusion, rupture, or extension. His blood pressure is 195/110 mmHg and heart rate is 92 bpm. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent thoracic endovascular aortic repair to cover the primary entry tear site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emergent open surgical repair via left thoracotomy with graft replacement of the descending aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous labetalol to achieve heart rate below 60 bpm and systolic blood pressure of 100-120 mmHg

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous sodium nitroprusside as monotherapy to achieve rapid blood pressure reduction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral amlodipine and observation with repeat imaging in 48 hours to assess for progression

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated Stanford type B dissections are managed medically with aggressive anti-impulse therapy using IV beta-blockers (e.g., labetalol, esmolol) to reduce heart rate and blood pressure, decreasing aortic wall shear stress.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uncomplicated type B aortic dissections (no malperfusion, rupture, or rapid expansion) are managed with medical anti-impulse therapy. IV beta-blockers (labetalol or esmolol) are first-line agents, targeting heart rate <60 bpm and systolic BP 100-120 mmHg. This reduces dP/dt (the rate of rise of aortic pressure), minimizing aortic wall shear stress and preventing propagation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: TEVAR is reserved for complicated type B dissections (malperfusion, rupture, rapid expansion, refractory pain/hypertension). This uncomplicated dissection should be managed medically initially.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Open surgical repair carries significant morbidity and mortality and is reserved for complicated type B dissections when TEVAR is not feasible. Medical management is preferred for uncomplicated cases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sodium nitroprusside as monotherapy is contraindicated because it causes reflex tachycardia, which increases aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). If used, it must always be combined with a beta-blocker, which should be started first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral antihypertensives are insufficient for acute management of aortic dissection. The severe hypertension requires urgent IV anti-impulse therapy to rapidly reduce heart rate and blood pressure.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with acute-onset severe chest pain. CT angiography reveals a Stanford type A aortic dissection with extension of the dissection flap into the right coronary artery ostium. ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. Troponin is elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent percutaneous coronary intervention with stenting of the right coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer thrombolytic therapy with alteplase for acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emergent surgical repair of the ascending aorta with coronary reimplantation or bypass grafting

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel and prepare for catheterization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initiate anticoagulation with heparin and perform diagnostic coronary angiography urgently

explanation.summary: Type A aortic dissection with coronary malperfusion requires emergent surgical repair, not percutaneous intervention or thrombolysis. The coronary ischemia is due to the dissection flap, not atherosclerotic plaque.
explanation.whyCorrect: When acute MI complicates a type A aortic dissection, the cause is coronary malperfusion from the dissection flap obstructing coronary ostia, not atherosclerotic plaque rupture. Emergent surgical repair of the ascending aorta is the definitive treatment, which may include coronary reimplantation, bypass grafting, or fenestration to restore coronary flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PCI is contraindicated in the setting of type A dissection. The MI is caused by the dissection flap, not an atherosclerotic lesion. PCI would delay definitive surgery and could worsen the dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytic therapy is absolutely contraindicated in aortic dissection, as it would promote hemorrhage from the false lumen and dramatically increase the risk of fatal rupture or tamponade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dual antiplatelet therapy would increase bleeding risk and is contraindicated in a patient requiring emergent aortic surgery. The MI mechanism is dissection-related, not atherothrombotic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heparin anticoagulation increases hemorrhage risk in type A dissection. Additionally, the diagnosis has already been established by CT angiography, and the patient needs emergent surgery, not diagnostic catheterization.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents with acute severe tearing chest pain. She has no history of hypertension or connective tissue disease. CT angiography reveals a Stanford type A aortic dissection. Which of the following best explains the increased risk of aortic dissection in this clinical context?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preeclampsia-induced severe hypertension causing acute elevation of aortic wall stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hormonal-mediated changes in the aortic media including decreased collagen cross-linking and increased elastin fragmentation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gestational diabetes causing accelerated atherosclerosis and plaque erosion in the ascending aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amniotic fluid embolism causing disseminated intravascular coagulation and aortic wall hemorrhage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron-deficiency anemia of pregnancy causing chronic hypoxic injury to the aortic vasa vasorum

explanation.summary: Pregnancy, particularly the third trimester and peripartum period, increases the risk of aortic dissection due to hormonal effects on the aortic wall (estrogen and progesterone reduce collagen and elastin integrity) combined with hemodynamic stress of increased cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: During pregnancy, elevated estrogen and progesterone cause histological changes in the aortic media, including decreased collagen cross-linking, elastin fragmentation, and smooth muscle cell hypertrophy. Combined with the hemodynamic stress of increased blood volume and cardiac output (greatest in the third trimester), these changes significantly increase the risk of aortic dissection, even in women without preexisting connective tissue disorders.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The question states the patient has no history of hypertension, and while preeclampsia can contribute, the primary mechanism of pregnancy-related dissection is hormonal-mediated structural changes in the aortic wall, not simply hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Gestational diabetes does not cause accelerated atherosclerosis to a degree that would precipitate acute aortic dissection. Atherosclerosis is not a significant mechanism of dissection in young pregnant women.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amniotic fluid embolism is a catastrophic obstetric complication but presents with cardiovascular collapse and DIC during labor or shortly postpartum, not with aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron-deficiency anemia is common in pregnancy but does not cause hypoxic injury to the vasa vasorum to a degree that would precipitate aortic dissection.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a Stanford type B aortic dissection that was managed medically 3 days ago now develops severe left leg pain with absent femoral pulses bilaterally. His left leg is cool, pale, and pulseless below the inguinal ligament. CT angiography shows the dissection flap extending into both common iliac arteries with compression of the true lumen. Which of the following complications has developed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Static malperfusion due to extension of the dissection flap into the branch vessel ostia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Embolic occlusion of the iliac arteries from thrombus formation within the false lumen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dynamic malperfusion due to intermittent prolapse of the intimal flap across the vessel lumen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atherosclerotic occlusion of both common iliac arteries coinciding with the dissection event

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasospasm of the iliac arteries due to catecholamine surge from the acute pain of dissection

explanation.summary: Static malperfusion occurs when the dissection extends into branch vessels, causing fixed obstruction of the true lumen by the dissection flap. This requires intervention (fenestration or stenting) to restore flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Static malperfusion occurs when the dissection flap propagates directly into branch vessel ostia (in this case, both common iliac arteries), causing fixed compression or obliteration of the true lumen. This is distinguished from dynamic malperfusion because the obstruction is anatomically fixed by the dissection flap within the branch vessel itself, not caused by intermittent flap motion at the ostium.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While thrombus can form in the false lumen, the CT shows the dissection flap extending into both iliac arteries with true lumen compression, indicating static malperfusion rather than distal embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dynamic malperfusion occurs when the intimal flap intermittently prolapses across a branch vessel ostium without actually extending into it. The CT shows the flap extending into the iliac arteries themselves, indicating static, not dynamic, obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral simultaneous atherosclerotic occlusion of both common iliac arteries would be exceedingly unlikely as an acute coincidental event. The imaging confirms the dissection flap as the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasospasm would be transient and would not produce the fixed anatomical findings seen on CT angiography. Catecholamine-mediated vasospasm is not a recognized mechanism of malperfusion in dissection.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with severe tearing chest pain undergoes emergent CT angiography, which reveals a smooth, crescentic area of high attenuation within the aortic wall of the descending thoracic aorta without an identifiable intimal flap or false lumen flow. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer with disruption of the internal elastic lamina of the aortic wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Classic aortic dissection with a small intimal tear that is below the resolution of CT imaging

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intramural hematoma due to rupture of the vasa vasorum within the aortic media

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic pseudoaneurysm from prior traumatic injury contained by the adventitia and periaortic tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortitis with mural thickening caused by giant cell arteritis involving the thoracic aorta

explanation.summary: An intramural hematoma (IMH) presents as a crescentic area of high attenuation within the aortic wall without an identifiable intimal flap or false lumen communication. It results from spontaneous rupture of the vasa vasorum.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intramural hematoma is a variant of acute aortic syndrome characterized by hemorrhage within the aortic media from rupture of the vasa vasorum, without an intimal tear. On CT, it appears as a smooth, crescentic or circumferential area of high attenuation within the aortic wall, without the intimal flap or false lumen flow seen in classic dissection. It is managed similarly to classic dissection based on location (type A vs. type B).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Penetrating atherosclerotic ulcer appears as a focal, crater-like outpouching of the aortic lumen through an ulcerated atherosclerotic plaque, not as a smooth crescentic intramural high-attenuation area.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Classic aortic dissection by definition has an intimal flap separating a true and false lumen. The absence of any flap or false lumen flow strongly argues against this diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A pseudoaneurysm involves a focal outpouching of the aortic lumen contained by adventitia and would appear as a saccular protrusion, not an intramural crescentic hyperdensity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortitis from giant cell arteritis can cause aortic wall thickening, but it typically shows circumferential wall enhancement and would present with systemic inflammatory symptoms, not as a crescentic high-attenuation hematoma.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of Stanford type B aortic dissection treated medically 2 years ago presents for follow-up CT angiography. Imaging reveals that the false lumen has become aneurysmal, now measuring 6.5 cm in maximum diameter, with evidence of partial thrombosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue conservative medical management with blood pressure optimization and repeat imaging in 6 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate systemic anticoagulation to prevent complete thrombosis and occlusion of the false lumen

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for thoracic endovascular aortic repair to cover the primary entry tear and promote false lumen thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform catheter-directed thrombolysis to dissolve the partial thrombus and restore false lumen patency

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer high-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammatory-mediated expansion of the false lumen

explanation.summary: A chronically dissected aorta with an aneurysmal false lumen exceeding 5.5-6.0 cm warrants intervention. TEVAR to cover the primary entry tear promotes false lumen thrombosis and remodeling, reducing rupture risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Late aneurysmal degeneration of the false lumen is a common complication of medically managed type B dissections. When the false lumen becomes aneurysmal (>5.5-6.0 cm), the risk of rupture increases significantly. TEVAR to cover the primary entry tear eliminates flow into the false lumen, promoting thrombosis and aortic remodeling. This approach has lower morbidity than open repair.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A 6.5-cm aneurysmal false lumen exceeds the threshold for intervention and carries a significant rupture risk. Continued conservative management alone is inappropriate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Anticoagulation would be harmful as it could prevent beneficial thrombosis of the false lumen and promote expansion. The goal is to promote false lumen thrombosis, not prevent it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thrombolysis to restore false lumen patency would be counterproductive and dangerous. Maintaining false lumen flow increases the risk of continued expansion and rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-dose corticosteroids have no role in the management of chronic aortic dissection with aneurysmal degeneration. The expansion is hemodynamic, not inflammatory.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden severe tearing chest pain. He reports recent cocaine use approximately 30 minutes before symptom onset. His blood pressure is 230/130 mmHg bilaterally, and heart rate is 110 bpm. CT angiography confirms a Stanford type A aortic dissection. In addition to emergent surgical repair, which of the following pharmacologic agents should be avoided in the initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous esmolol administered as a continuous infusion to reduce heart rate and aortic shear stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous nicardipine administered to achieve targeted reduction of systolic blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous phentolamine administered to counteract alpha-adrenergic-mediated vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous hydralazine administered as an arteriolar vasodilator for blood pressure reduction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous clevidipine administered as an ultra-short-acting calcium channel blocker infusion

explanation.summary: Hydralazine is a direct arteriolar vasodilator that causes reflex tachycardia, increasing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). It should be avoided in aortic dissection because it worsens hemodynamic stress on the aortic wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hydralazine is a direct arteriolar vasodilator that causes reflex sympathetic activation and tachycardia, increasing the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and aortic wall shear stress. In aortic dissection, agents that increase dP/dt promote propagation of the dissection and should be avoided. Beta-blockers should always be given first before any vasodilator.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Esmolol is a first-line agent in aortic dissection. It reduces heart rate and dP/dt, decreasing aortic wall stress. In cocaine-associated dissection, beta-blockers are still used (though some advocate for initial benzodiazepines or phentolamine first).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nicardipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker used as an adjunct to beta-blockers in aortic dissection for additional blood pressure control. It does not cause significant reflex tachycardia when used with a beta-blocker.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phentolamine is an alpha-adrenergic antagonist that is specifically useful in cocaine-associated hypertensive emergencies by counteracting the alpha-mediated vasoconstriction without worsening dP/dt to the same degree as hydralazine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clevidipine is an ultra-short-acting IV calcium channel blocker with a favorable hemodynamic profile, used as an adjunct for blood pressure control in aortic dissection without significant reflex tachycardia.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with acute Stanford type A aortic dissection undergoes emergent surgical repair. Intraoperatively, the dissection is found to extend to the aortic root with disruption of the commissural support of the aortic valve. Postoperatively, the surgeon reports that a Bentall procedure was performed. Which of the following best describes this operation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replacement of the ascending aorta with a tube graft and separate aortic valve replacement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Composite graft replacement of the aortic root, ascending aorta, and aortic valve with reimplantation of the coronary arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resuspension of the native aortic valve commissures with supracoronary ascending aortic replacement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replacement of the entire aortic arch with reimplantation of the brachiocephalic vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endovascular stent grafting of the ascending aorta with transcatheter aortic valve replacement

explanation.summary: The Bentall procedure involves composite replacement of the aortic root and ascending aorta with a valved conduit (mechanical or bioprosthetic valve attached to a Dacron graft) and reimplantation of the coronary artery ostia into the graft.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Bentall procedure is a composite aortic root replacement that includes a prosthetic valve sewn into a Dacron graft, which replaces the entire aortic root and ascending aorta. The coronary arteries are reimplanted as buttons into the side of the graft. It is indicated when the aortic valve cannot be preserved (e.g., with disrupted commissures or preexisting valve disease).

whyWrongByChoice.A: A separate tube graft and valve replacement (not as a composite) describes a non-Bentall approach. The Bentall procedure specifically uses a composite valve-graft conduit as a single unit.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Resuspension of aortic valve commissures with supracoronary graft (valve-sparing repair, such as the David procedure) preserves the native aortic valve and does not involve a composite valve conduit.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Total aortic arch replacement with great vessel reimplantation is a separate procedure (arch replacement or elephant trunk) and does not describe the Bentall operation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endovascular stent grafting of the ascending aorta with TAVR is not a Bentall procedure and is not standard practice for acute type A dissection.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with hypertelorism, bifid uvula, and arterial tortuosity on CT angiography presents with an acute Stanford type A aortic dissection. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the TGFBR1 gene. Which of the following syndromes is most likely responsible for this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Marfan syndrome caused by mutations in the fibrillin-1 gene on chromosome 15

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turner syndrome caused by complete or partial monosomy of the X chromosome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loeys-Dietz syndrome caused by mutations in transforming growth factor-beta receptor genes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome caused by mutations in the type III procollagen gene

choices.label: E
choices.text: Williams syndrome caused by microdeletion of the elastin gene on chromosome 7

explanation.summary: Loeys-Dietz syndrome is characterized by the triad of hypertelorism, bifid uvula/cleft palate, and arterial aneurysms/tortuosity. It is caused by mutations in TGFBR1 or TGFBR2.
explanation.whyCorrect: Loeys-Dietz syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in TGFBR1 or TGFBR2 (transforming growth factor-beta receptor genes). The classic triad includes hypertelorism (widely spaced eyes), bifid uvula or cleft palate, and diffuse arterial aneurysms with tortuosity. Patients have aggressive aortic disease with dissections occurring at smaller aortic diameters than in Marfan syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation) presents with arachnodactyly, lens subluxation, mitral valve prolapse, and aortic root dilation, but does not feature hypertelorism or bifid uvula.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Turner syndrome (45,X) is associated with bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation of the aorta but presents with short stature, webbed neck, and shield chest, not hypertelorism and bifid uvula.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (COL3A1 mutation) causes arterial fragility and spontaneous rupture but is characterized by thin translucent skin, easy bruising, and hypermobility, not hypertelorism or bifid uvula.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Williams syndrome (elastin gene deletion) causes supravalvular aortic stenosis, elfin facies, and intellectual disability, not the features described in this patient.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with a known Stanford type B aortic dissection managed medically for 5 days develops acute-onset right flank pain, oliguria, and rising serum creatinine from 1.1 to 4.8 mg/dL. CT angiography shows the dissection flap compressing the right renal artery true lumen. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent open surgical aortic repair with renal artery bypass grafting via median sternotomy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conservative management with continued medical therapy and initiation of hemodialysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endovascular stenting of the right renal artery with or without aortic true lumen stent fenestration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right nephrectomy to remove the ischemic kidney and prevent secondary complications

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increase the dose of intravenous beta-blocker to further reduce aortic wall shear stress

explanation.summary: Renal malperfusion in type B dissection is a complication requiring intervention. Endovascular techniques (renal artery stenting, aortic fenestration, or true lumen stenting) are the preferred approach to restore branch vessel perfusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Malperfusion of a branch vessel (renal, mesenteric, or limb ischemia) is a complication of aortic dissection that converts an uncomplicated dissection to a complicated one, requiring intervention. Endovascular techniques including renal artery stenting, aortic true lumen stenting, and/or percutaneous fenestration of the dissection flap are first-line approaches to restore branch vessel flow with lower morbidity than open surgery.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Open surgical repair with renal bypass is a high-morbidity approach and is not first-line for branch vessel malperfusion. Endovascular techniques are preferred. Additionally, a median sternotomy is used for ascending aortic repair, not for renal revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Conservative management with dialysis does not address the underlying ischemia and could lead to irreversible kidney loss. The malperfusion requires intervention to restore renal perfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nephrectomy is a last resort for a nonviable kidney. The acute onset and short duration suggest the kidney may be salvageable with prompt revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increasing beta-blocker dose will not resolve the mechanical obstruction of the renal artery by the dissection flap. While medical therapy is important, malperfusion requires procedural intervention.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with a history of type B aortic dissection managed medically presents with worsening back pain despite adequate blood pressure control. He develops new-onset paraplegia. MRI of the spine shows no vertebral fracture or epidural collection. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of his neurological deficit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anterior spinal artery occlusion from dissection flap extension into intercostal or lumbar arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Posterior spinal artery thrombosis from hypercoagulable state induced by aortic dissection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Central cord syndrome from syringomyelia caused by chronic aortic dissection-related hemodynamic changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epidural hematoma from coagulopathy caused by consumption of clotting factors in the false lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vertebral artery dissection extending from the aortic dissection causing brainstem infarction

explanation.summary: Spinal cord ischemia in aortic dissection results from malperfusion of the anterior spinal artery, which is supplied by segmental intercostal and lumbar arteries arising from the aorta. The dissection flap can occlude these branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord and receives critical segmental blood supply from intercostal and lumbar arteries (including the artery of Adamkiewicz, typically arising between T9-T12). In type B dissection, the dissection flap can extend into or compress these segmental arteries, causing anterior spinal cord ischemia manifesting as paraplegia with preserved posterior column function.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Posterior spinal artery thrombosis would cause posterior column dysfunction (loss of proprioception, vibration sense) rather than paraplegia. Hypercoagulable state is not the primary mechanism of spinal cord ischemia in dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Syringomyelia is a chronic condition unrelated to aortic dissection. It presents with cape-like pain and temperature loss, not acute paraplegia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The MRI shows no epidural collection, ruling out epidural hematoma. While consumptive coagulopathy can occur in dissection, it does not explain the acute paraplegia without imaging findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vertebral artery dissection could cause posterior circulation stroke (brainstem/cerebellar symptoms), not isolated paraplegia. Additionally, extension from a thoracoabdominal dissection to the vertebral arteries is extremely uncommon.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and type 2 diabetes presents with bilateral calf pain that occurs after walking two blocks and resolves with rest. Pedal pulses are diminished bilaterally. Ankle-brachial index (ABI) is 0.65 on the right and 0.70 on the left. Which of the following is the most likely site of arterial stenosis causing his symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral common iliac arteries at their origin from the aortic bifurcation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilateral superficial femoral arteries at the level of the adductor canal (Hunter canal)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bilateral posterior tibial arteries at the level of the medial malleolus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral deep femoral arteries (profunda femoris) at their origin from the common femoral

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bilateral popliteal arteries at the level of the popliteal fossa behind the knee

explanation.summary: The superficial femoral artery (SFA) at the adductor canal (Hunter canal) is the most common site of atherosclerotic stenosis in peripheral arterial disease, causing calf claudication.
explanation.whyCorrect: The superficial femoral artery at the adductor canal is the single most common site of atherosclerotic occlusion in PAD. The artery is compressed as it passes through the adductor hiatus, and the relative immobility at this point promotes plaque formation. SFA stenosis causes calf claudication because the calf muscles (gastrocnemius, soleus) are the primary muscles affected when blood flow distal to the SFA occlusion is insufficient during exercise.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Common iliac artery stenosis (aortoiliac disease or Leriche syndrome) typically presents with buttock and thigh claudication, not isolated calf claudication. It would also affect femoral pulses, which are not described as absent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Posterior tibial artery disease at the ankle level is more commonly seen as a manifestation of diabetic small-vessel disease and typically causes foot symptoms rather than calf claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The deep femoral artery (profunda femoris) is an important collateral pathway in PAD and is less commonly affected by atherosclerosis. Its occlusion alone would not typically cause the classic calf claudication pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While popliteal artery disease can occur, it is much less common than SFA disease at the adductor canal as the primary site of atherosclerotic stenosis causing claudication.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with a 6-month history of bilateral buttock and thigh claudication with erectile dysfunction. On examination, bilateral femoral pulses are absent. Which of the following syndromes best describes this clinical presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Subclavian steal syndrome due to proximal subclavian artery stenosis with retrograde vertebral flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: May-Thurner syndrome due to compression of the left common iliac vein by the right iliac artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leriche syndrome due to aortoiliac occlusive disease at the aortic bifurcation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Popliteal entrapment syndrome due to anomalous muscular compression of the popliteal artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thoracic outlet syndrome due to compression of the subclavian artery by a cervical rib

explanation.summary: Leriche syndrome is the classic triad of buttock/thigh claudication, absent femoral pulses, and erectile dysfunction caused by aortoiliac occlusive disease at the aortic bifurcation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leriche syndrome describes the triad resulting from chronic atherosclerotic occlusion at the aortic bifurcation or common iliac arteries: (1) bilateral buttock, hip, and thigh claudication, (2) absent or diminished femoral pulses, and (3) erectile dysfunction (due to impaired blood flow through the internal iliac arteries to the pudendal arteries).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Subclavian steal syndrome involves stenosis of the proximal subclavian artery with retrograde flow in the ipsilateral vertebral artery, causing upper extremity claudication and vertebrobasilar symptoms, not lower extremity claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: May-Thurner syndrome involves compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery, predisposing to left lower extremity deep venous thrombosis, not arterial claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Popliteal entrapment syndrome occurs in young athletes due to anomalous course of the popliteal artery relative to the gastrocnemius muscle, causing calf claudication with exercise, not buttock/thigh claudication with absent femoral pulses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thoracic outlet syndrome involves compression of neurovascular structures at the thoracic outlet, causing upper extremity symptoms, not lower extremity claudication.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with diabetes and peripheral arterial disease presents with a nonhealing ulcer on the lateral aspect of his right fifth toe. The ulcer has a punched-out appearance with pale, atrophic base and minimal granulation tissue. The surrounding skin is shiny and hairless with dependent rubor. ABI is 0.35 on the right. Which of the following features most strongly distinguishes this arterial ulcer from a venous stasis ulcer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Location on the lateral aspect of the toe rather than the medial malleolar region

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of surrounding hyperpigmented skin with hemosiderin deposition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Shallow ulcer with irregular borders and a fibrinous exudative base

choices.label: D
choices.text: Associated lipodermatosclerosis with induration and fibrosis of the subcutaneous tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Painless ulcer with profuse serous drainage and associated lower extremity edema

explanation.summary: Arterial ulcers characteristically occur on the distal extremities (toes, heel, dorsum of foot) in areas of poor perfusion, while venous ulcers typically occur at the medial malleolus (gaiter area).
explanation.whyCorrect: The location of an ulcer is one of the most distinguishing features between arterial and venous ulcers. Arterial ulcers occur at the most distal, poorly perfused sites: tips of toes, between toes, lateral foot, and heel. They have punched-out borders, a pale/necrotic base, and are associated with pain. Venous ulcers characteristically occur at the medial malleolus (gaiter zone) due to venous hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemosiderin deposition is characteristic of chronic venous insufficiency, not arterial disease. It results from extravasation and breakdown of red blood cells due to venous hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Shallow ulcers with irregular borders and fibrinous exudate are more characteristic of venous ulcers. Arterial ulcers typically have well-demarcated, punched-out borders.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lipodermatosclerosis is a hallmark of chronic venous insufficiency, involving fibrosis and induration of the subcutaneous tissue due to chronic venous hypertension and inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Venous ulcers are typically less painful (or painless) with serous drainage and associated edema. Arterial ulcers are typically painful, especially at night and with elevation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with longstanding diabetes and end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis presents with bilateral calf claudication. His ABI measurements are 1.45 bilaterally. His toe pressures are 40 mmHg on the right and 38 mmHg on the left (normal >80 mmHg). Which of the following best explains the discrepancy between the ABI and toe pressure values?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Falsely elevated ABI due to medial arterial calcification (Monckeberg sclerosis) of the tibial arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Falsely low toe pressures due to Raynaud phenomenon causing digital vasospasm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Falsely elevated ABI due to acute deep venous thrombosis causing increased venous pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Falsely low toe pressures due to improper cuff placement over the digital arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Accurate ABI suggesting no significant peripheral arterial disease in this patient

explanation.summary: In patients with diabetes and ESRD, medial arterial calcification (Monckeberg sclerosis) makes tibial arteries noncompressible, producing falsely elevated ABI values (>1.3). Toe pressures and toe-brachial index are more reliable in these patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Monckeberg medial calcific sclerosis is common in patients with diabetes and chronic kidney disease. Calcium deposits in the tunica media render the tibial and peroneal arteries noncompressible, producing falsely elevated ABI readings (typically >1.3). Digital arteries are spared from this calcification, making toe pressures and toe-brachial index (TBI) more accurate assessments of distal perfusion. The low toe pressures confirm significant PAD despite the misleading ABI.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Raynaud phenomenon involves episodic digital vasospasm triggered by cold or stress and presents with color changes. It does not chronically lower toe pressures and is not the explanation in a diabetic patient with ESRD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Deep venous thrombosis affects the venous system and would not elevate arterial pressure measurements. DVT does not cause falsely elevated ABI.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While technical error can affect readings, the consistent bilaterally elevated ABI >1.3 with low toe pressures is the classic pattern of medial calcification, not a measurement artifact.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An ABI >1.3 is not normal; it indicates noncompressible calcified arteries and is actually associated with increased cardiovascular mortality. The low toe pressures confirm significant PAD.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with PAD and an ABI of 0.55 presents with rest pain in his right foot that worsens when the leg is elevated and improves when the leg is dangled over the side of the bed. According to the Fontaine classification, which stage of peripheral arterial disease does this patient have?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stage I: asymptomatic peripheral arterial disease detected only by abnormal ABI testing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stage IIa: mild claudication with pain occurring after walking more than 200 meters

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stage IIb: moderate to severe claudication with pain occurring before walking 200 meters

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stage III: ischemic rest pain occurring in the distal foot, typically at night in the supine position

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stage IV: tissue loss with ulceration or gangrene of the affected lower extremity

explanation.summary: Ischemic rest pain represents Fontaine stage III (or Rutherford category 4). It occurs when perfusion is insufficient even at rest and characteristically worsens with elevation (reduced hydrostatic pressure) and improves with dependency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fontaine stage III is defined by ischemic rest pain, which occurs when arterial perfusion pressure is insufficient to meet the metabolic demands of the foot even at rest. Rest pain characteristically affects the forefoot and toes, worsens with leg elevation (loss of gravitational assist to perfusion), and improves with leg dependency (increased hydrostatic pressure to the foot). The ABI of 0.55 is consistent with this stage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stage I represents asymptomatic PAD where the disease is detected by abnormal ABI or other objective testing but the patient has no symptoms. This patient has rest pain.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Stage IIa represents mild intermittent claudication with a pain-free walking distance greater than 200 meters. This patient has rest pain, not exercise-induced claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stage IIb represents moderate to severe claudication with a pain-free walking distance less than 200 meters. Rest pain indicates more advanced disease beyond the claudication stages.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stage IV involves tissue loss (ulceration or gangrene). While this patient may progress to stage IV, currently only rest pain is described without tissue loss.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with stable intermittent claudication affecting both calves after walking 3 blocks has an ABI of 0.72 bilaterally. She is currently taking aspirin and atorvastatin. Her claudication significantly limits her daily activities. Which of the following pharmacologic agents is most appropriate to add for symptomatic improvement of her claudication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin to prevent thrombotic progression of atherosclerotic plaque in the peripheral arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cilostazol to increase walking distance through phosphodiesterase-3 inhibition and vasodilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Clopidogrel to replace aspirin for enhanced antiplatelet effect on walking distance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pentoxifylline as first-line therapy to improve red blood cell deformability and blood viscosity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nifedipine to achieve peripheral vasodilation and improve blood flow to the ischemic muscles

explanation.summary: Cilostazol is the pharmacologic agent with the strongest evidence for improving walking distance in intermittent claudication. It is a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor with vasodilatory and antiplatelet properties.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cilostazol is a selective phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor that has been shown in clinical trials to increase maximal and pain-free walking distance by 40-60% in patients with intermittent claudication. It promotes vasodilation, inhibits platelet aggregation, and has anti-proliferative effects on smooth muscle cells. It is the first-line pharmacologic agent for symptom relief in claudication (after supervised exercise therapy).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Warfarin is not indicated for PAD claudication. Anticoagulation does not improve walking distance and adds bleeding risk without benefit in stable atherosclerotic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While clopidogrel is an alternative to aspirin for cardiovascular risk reduction in PAD, it has not been shown to improve claudication symptoms or walking distance.
