whyWrongByChoice.D: Pentoxifylline has weak evidence for improving claudication symptoms and is not considered first-line. Its mechanism involves improving red blood cell deformability, but clinical trials show marginal benefit at best.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nifedipine and other calcium channel blockers have not been shown to improve walking distance in PAD. Peripheral vasodilation without addressing the fixed stenosis does not effectively relieve claudication.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with diabetes and PAD presents with a black, mummified fourth toe on his right foot. The toe is dry, shriveled, and has a clear line of demarcation between viable and nonviable tissue. There is no surrounding erythema, warmth, or purulent drainage. The patient denies fever. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emergent surgical amputation of the fourth toe to prevent proximal spread of the gangrene

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediate below-knee amputation to ensure adequate blood supply to the amputation stump

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vascular surgery consultation for revascularization assessment while allowing auto-amputation to occur

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiate broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics and emergent surgical debridement of the toe

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apply topical nitroglycerin paste to the affected toe to promote vasodilation and tissue salvage

explanation.summary: Dry gangrene with a clear line of demarcation and no signs of infection can be managed conservatively with auto-amputation while assessing the patient's vascular status for potential revascularization to prevent further tissue loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dry gangrene is desiccated, mummified tissue with a clear demarcation line, without active infection. It does not require emergent amputation. The priority is to assess the patient's vascular status for potential revascularization (angiography, endovascular or surgical intervention) to improve perfusion and prevent further tissue loss. Auto-amputation (spontaneous separation of the necrotic tissue) can be allowed to occur in the interim.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Emergent surgical amputation is not required for dry gangrene without infection. The clear demarcation line indicates the process is stable, and revascularization assessment should precede definitive amputation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Below-knee amputation is a drastic intervention for a single toe with dry gangrene. Maximizing limb salvage through revascularization assessment is the appropriate initial approach.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Broad-spectrum antibiotics and surgical debridement are indicated for wet gangrene with active infection (erythema, warmth, purulent drainage, fever). This patient has dry gangrene without signs of infection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Topical nitroglycerin is not an effective treatment for gangrene. The tissue is already necrotic and beyond salvage; the focus should be on revascularization to prevent further tissue loss.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset severe pain, pallor, and pulselessness in her right leg. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and is not on anticoagulation. The right leg is cold and pale from the mid-thigh distally, with absent popliteal and pedal pulses. Sensation is diminished in the foot. Which of the following clinical findings, if present, would indicate irreversible limb ischemia and preclude attempted revascularization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Absent pedal pulses on Doppler examination with a measurable ankle pressure of 30 mmHg

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sensory deficit over the dorsum of the foot with inability to feel light touch

choices.label: C
choices.text: Muscle rigor with fixed mottling of the skin that does not blanch with pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moderate pain in the calf muscles exacerbated by passive dorsiflexion of the ankle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Capillary refill time of 5 seconds with faint audible signals on arterial Doppler

explanation.summary: Muscle rigor (hardening) and fixed, nonblanching mottling indicate irreversible ischemia (Rutherford class III). Revascularization in this setting risks reperfusion injury, compartment syndrome, hyperkalemia, myoglobinuria, and death.
explanation.whyCorrect: Muscle rigor (stiffness/hardening of the calf or thigh muscles) and fixed mottling that does not blanch with pressure are signs of irreversible tissue ischemia, indicating that the limb is nonviable (Rutherford class III acute limb ischemia). At this stage, the tissue has undergone necrosis, and revascularization would release toxic metabolites (potassium, myoglobin, lactate) into the systemic circulation, risking fatal reperfusion syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Absent pedal pulses with a measurable ankle pressure of 30 mmHg indicate severe ischemia but the limb may still be viable. This corresponds to Rutherford class IIb (threatened but potentially salvageable with urgent intervention).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sensory deficit over the dorsum of the foot indicates threatened ischemia (Rutherford class IIa/IIb) but does not indicate irreversibility. Sensory loss can recover with prompt revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pain on passive stretch (passive dorsiflexion) suggests impending or early compartment syndrome and indicates viable but threatened muscle tissue that may be salvageable with revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prolonged capillary refill with faint Doppler signals indicates severely compromised but potentially viable tissue. Complete absence of Doppler signals with rigor would indicate irreversibility.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with PAD undergoes treadmill exercise testing. His resting ABI is 0.92 on the right and 0.95 on the left. After 5 minutes of walking on the treadmill, he develops right calf pain and his right ABI drops to 0.55 while the left remains at 0.90. Which of the following best explains the utility of exercise ABI testing in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It confirms the diagnosis of PAD that was missed by the resting ABI due to hemodynamically significant stenosis unmasked by exercise

choices.label: B
choices.text: It reveals acute arterial thrombosis that occurred during exercise and requires emergent intervention

choices.label: C
choices.text: It demonstrates venous insufficiency causing exercise-induced calf pain with normal arterial flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: It indicates popliteal artery entrapment syndrome that is only manifest during muscle contraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: It excludes PAD because the resting ABI is within the normal range bilaterally

explanation.summary: In patients with claudication symptoms but normal or borderline resting ABI (0.91-0.99), exercise ABI testing can unmask hemodynamically significant stenosis. A post-exercise ABI decrease of >20% or to <0.90 confirms PAD.
explanation.whyCorrect: A resting ABI of 0.91-0.99 is borderline normal and may not reveal hemodynamically significant stenosis at rest due to collateral compensation. During exercise, increased flow demand across a stenosis causes a pressure drop distal to the lesion, reducing the ABI. A post-exercise drop to 0.55 (>20% decrease) confirms the presence of hemodynamically significant PAD that was not apparent at rest.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute arterial thrombosis would present with sudden severe ischemia (pain, pallor, pulselessness), not gradual onset of claudication with a predictable ABI drop during exercise. The post-exercise ABI returns to baseline after rest.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Venous insufficiency causes heaviness and swelling, not classic claudication, and would not cause a drop in ABI (an arterial measurement). The ABI drop confirms arterial insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Popliteal entrapment syndrome typically affects young athletes and involves anomalous muscle compression of the popliteal artery. While exercise provocation testing is used, the clinical context (older man with cardiovascular risk factors) strongly favors atherosclerotic PAD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A borderline resting ABI does not exclude PAD. The significant post-exercise ABI decrease clearly demonstrates hemodynamically significant stenosis that was compensated at rest.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with critical limb ischemia of his left leg (ABI 0.30, rest pain, and a small toe ulcer) undergoes angiography revealing a long segment occlusion of the left superficial femoral artery. The popliteal artery reconstitutes via collaterals with patent tibial runoff. He has suitable saphenous vein anatomy. Which of the following is the most appropriate revascularization strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty with drug-eluting stent placement in the superficial femoral artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Femoral-to-popliteal bypass using an autologous reversed great saphenous vein graft

choices.label: C
choices.text: Femoral-to-popliteal bypass using a prosthetic expanded polytetrafluoroethylene (ePTFE) graft

choices.label: D
choices.text: Axillofemoral bypass using a prosthetic graft from the axillary artery to the common femoral artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atherectomy alone without stenting to debulk the atherosclerotic plaque from the occluded segment

explanation.summary: For long-segment SFA occlusion with critical limb ischemia and suitable vein, autologous saphenous vein bypass has superior long-term patency compared to endovascular intervention or prosthetic grafts.
explanation.whyCorrect: For long-segment (>25 cm) SFA occlusion causing critical limb ischemia, surgical bypass with autologous great saphenous vein (reversed or in-situ) provides the best long-term patency rates (70-80% at 5 years). When suitable saphenous vein is available, it is the conduit of choice for infrainguinal bypass, especially for below-knee targets, due to superior patency compared to prosthetic conduits.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Endovascular intervention (PTA with stenting) is reasonable for short-segment disease or patients with high surgical risk, but for long-segment SFA occlusion with critical limb ischemia, surgical bypass with vein has superior long-term patency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prosthetic (ePTFE) grafts have inferior patency rates compared to autologous saphenous vein, particularly for below-knee bypass targets. They should only be used when suitable vein is unavailable.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Axillofemoral bypass is an extra-anatomic procedure reserved for patients who cannot undergo direct aortic or femoral reconstruction (e.g., hostile abdomen, aortic infection). It is not appropriate for femoropopliteal disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atherectomy alone for a long-segment occlusion has poor long-term patency and is not the standard of care for critical limb ischemia requiring durable revascularization.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with PAD and intermittent claudication is enrolled in a supervised exercise therapy program. After 12 weeks, his maximal walking distance has increased from 200 meters to 450 meters. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which supervised exercise improves claudication symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Regression of atherosclerotic plaque in the stenotic arteries leading to improved luminal diameter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of new arterial collateral vessels and improved skeletal muscle oxygen extraction efficiency

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conversion of type II fast-twitch muscle fibers to type I slow-twitch fibers in the affected limb

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction of systemic blood pressure leading to decreased peripheral vascular resistance at rest

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dissolution of microthrombi within the tibial arteries due to increased fibrinolytic activity

explanation.summary: Supervised exercise therapy improves claudication primarily through angiogenesis (collateral vessel formation), improved endothelial function, enhanced skeletal muscle oxygen utilization, and metabolic adaptation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The benefits of supervised exercise in PAD are multifactorial: (1) stimulation of arteriogenesis and angiogenesis, promoting collateral vessel development around the stenotic segment; (2) improved endothelial function with increased nitric oxide bioavailability; (3) enhanced skeletal muscle mitochondrial function and oxygen extraction efficiency; and (4) improved walking biomechanics and pain tolerance. These adaptations occur without significant change in the underlying stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Exercise does not cause regression of atherosclerotic plaque. The ABI typically does not significantly improve with exercise training, confirming that the primary stenosis remains unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While some fiber-type adaptation may occur with endurance training, this is not the primary mechanism of claudication improvement. The main benefits are vascular (collateral development) and metabolic (improved oxygen extraction).
whyWrongByChoice.D: While exercise may modestly reduce blood pressure, this is not the mechanism by which walking distance improves. The benefit is from local vascular and metabolic adaptations in the affected limb.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Exercise does not dissolve microthrombi. The claudication in PAD is due to fixed atherosclerotic stenosis, not acute thrombotic occlusion.

questions.stem: A 57-year-old man with a history of smoking and hyperlipidemia presents with sudden onset of a cool, pale, painful right foot. He has an irregularly irregular pulse on cardiac monitoring. Right pedal pulses are absent. Emergent CT angiography reveals an abrupt cutoff of the right popliteal artery with no significant atherosclerotic disease in the proximal vessels. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of his acute limb ischemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In situ thrombosis of the popliteal artery due to rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque

choices.label: B
choices.text: Popliteal artery aneurysm with acute thrombotic occlusion causing distal embolization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial embolism from a left atrial thrombus secondary to atrial fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale from a deep venous thrombosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic dissection with extension of the dissection flap into the right popliteal artery

explanation.summary: The combination of acute limb ischemia, an irregularly irregular pulse (atrial fibrillation), and an abrupt arterial cutoff without proximal atherosclerotic disease strongly suggests cardioembolism from a left atrial thrombus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atrial fibrillation is the most common cause of arterial embolism to the extremities. Blood stasis in the fibrillating left atrium promotes thrombus formation (typically in the left atrial appendage). Embolization to a peripheral artery causes sudden arterial occlusion, presenting as acute limb ischemia. The abrupt cutoff on angiography without proximal disease is characteristic of embolism (as opposed to in situ thrombosis, which usually shows diffuse atherosclerotic disease).

whyWrongByChoice.A: In situ thrombosis from plaque rupture typically occurs in the setting of diffuse atherosclerotic disease with prior claudication history. The CT shows no significant proximal atherosclerosis, making embolism more likely than in situ thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Popliteal artery aneurysm with thrombosis is possible but would show aneurysmal dilation of the popliteal artery on CT. The presence of atrial fibrillation strongly favors cardioembolism as the etiology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paradoxical embolism through a PFO is uncommon and would require the presence of a DVT with right-to-left shunting. There is no mention of DVT, and the atrial fibrillation provides a more straightforward embolic source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic dissection causing isolated popliteal artery occlusion without involvement of the aorta or iliac arteries is extremely unlikely. Additionally, dissection would show characteristic findings on CT rather than a simple abrupt cutoff.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and a 50-pack-year smoking history presents after a transient episode of right-sided arm and leg weakness lasting 25 minutes that resolved completely. Carotid duplex ultrasonography reveals a peak systolic velocity of 290 cm/s and an end-diastolic velocity of 110 cm/s in the left internal carotid artery. CT angiography confirms 80% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. The patient's neurological examination is now normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiate dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel and repeat imaging in 6 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Schedule carotid endarterectomy to be performed within 2 weeks of the symptomatic event

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for carotid artery stenting as the preferred intervention for all symptomatic stenoses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Begin systemic anticoagulation with intravenous heparin followed by long-term warfarin therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform emergent intracranial thrombectomy to prevent recurrent cerebrovascular events

explanation.summary: This patient had a TIA with ipsilateral high-grade (80%) symptomatic carotid stenosis. Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) performed within 2 weeks of a symptomatic event provides the greatest benefit in reducing future stroke risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: For symptomatic carotid stenosis of 70-99%, CEA is strongly recommended by current guidelines. The benefit of CEA is greatest when performed within 2 weeks of the index neurological event (TIA or non-disabling stroke), as the risk of recurrent stroke is highest in the early period. Multiple landmark trials (NASCET, ECST) demonstrated significant stroke risk reduction with early CEA in this population.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While antiplatelet therapy is part of the medical management, relying solely on dual antiplatelet therapy without revascularization for high-grade symptomatic stenosis is insufficient. CEA provides superior stroke risk reduction compared to medical therapy alone in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carotid artery stenting is an alternative to CEA in select patients (e.g., high surgical risk, prior neck radiation, recurrent stenosis post-CEA), but CEA remains the preferred intervention for most patients with symptomatic high-grade stenosis, particularly in older patients where stenting carries higher periprocedural stroke risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic anticoagulation is not the standard treatment for atherosclerotic carotid stenosis. Anticoagulation is indicated for cardioembolic sources (e.g., atrial fibrillation) but does not address the fixed atherosclerotic lesion and increases bleeding risk without proven benefit in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intracranial thrombectomy is indicated for acute large-vessel occlusion stroke within a specific time window, not for a resolved TIA. This patient's symptoms have completely resolved, and there is no ongoing intracranial occlusion to address.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old asymptomatic woman undergoes a screening carotid duplex ultrasound that reveals 75% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. She has well-controlled hypertension, takes a statin, and has no history of transient ischemic attack or stroke. Her estimated perioperative stroke/death risk at the local surgical center is 2.5%. Which of the following best describes the current evidence-based recommendation for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate carotid endarterectomy is mandatory for all asymptomatic stenoses exceeding 60%

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carotid artery stenting is preferred over endarterectomy for asymptomatic carotid disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Optimal medical therapy alone is sufficient, and revascularization offers no additional benefit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carotid endarterectomy may be considered if the perioperative stroke and death rate is less than 3%

choices.label: E
choices.text: External carotid-to-internal carotid bypass is the procedure of choice for asymptomatic disease

explanation.summary: For asymptomatic carotid stenosis of 60-99%, CEA may be considered if life expectancy exceeds 5 years and the perioperative stroke/death rate is less than 3%. The benefit over optimal medical therapy is modest.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ACAS and ACST trials showed that CEA for asymptomatic stenosis ≥60% reduces the 5-year stroke risk, but only when the perioperative complication rate is below 3%. Given the improvements in modern medical therapy (statins, antihypertensives, antiplatelets), the absolute benefit of CEA in asymptomatic patients is modest, and the decision requires careful risk-benefit analysis on an individual basis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CEA is not mandatory for all asymptomatic stenoses exceeding 60%. Current guidelines recommend shared decision-making that weighs the modest benefit of surgery against operative risk, patient life expectancy, and adequacy of medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: For asymptomatic disease, carotid artery stenting has not been demonstrated to be superior to CEA. The CREST trial showed similar long-term outcomes but higher periprocedural stroke rates with stenting, particularly in older patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While optimal medical therapy is critically important and may be sufficient for many asymptomatic patients, stating that revascularization offers no additional benefit is incorrect. Trials have shown a small but statistically significant reduction in stroke risk with CEA in carefully selected asymptomatic patients.
whyWrongByChoice.E: External carotid-to-internal carotid bypass is not a standard procedure for carotid stenosis. CEA with patch angioplasty or eversion technique is the established surgical approach.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of left carotid endarterectomy 3 years ago presents with recurrent episodes of right hand clumsiness and word-finding difficulty each lasting 10-15 minutes. Carotid duplex ultrasonography reveals 85% restenosis at the prior endarterectomy site in the left internal carotid artery. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of restenosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intimal hyperplasia characterized by smooth muscle cell proliferation and extracellular matrix deposition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive atherosclerotic plaque accumulation at the endarterectomy site identical to the original lesion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibromuscular dysplasia developing de novo at the surgically manipulated arterial segment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thrombotic occlusion caused by heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with ongoing platelet activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasospasm of the internal carotid artery triggered by surgical scar tissue compression

explanation.summary: Restenosis occurring within 2-5 years after CEA is predominantly caused by myointimal hyperplasia, characterized by smooth muscle cell migration and proliferation with excessive extracellular matrix production at the endarterectomy site.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-CEA restenosis is classified by timing: early restenosis (within months to ~3 years) is predominantly due to myointimal hyperplasia, where vascular smooth muscle cells migrate from the media to the intima and proliferate in response to surgical injury. This process involves excessive extracellular matrix (collagen, proteoglycan) deposition. Late restenosis (>3 years) is more often due to recurrent atherosclerosis. At 3 years, this patient's restenosis is most consistent with intimal hyperplasia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Recurrent atherosclerosis is the predominant mechanism of late restenosis (typically >3-5 years after CEA). At 3 years post-procedure, intimal hyperplasia is more likely than a fully developed recurrent atherosclerotic plaque.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibromuscular dysplasia is an idiopathic, non-atherosclerotic, non-inflammatory vascular disease that does not develop de novo at sites of surgical manipulation. It is a congenital/developmental condition affecting specific vascular beds.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) would present acutely during or shortly after heparin exposure, not 3 years postoperatively. HIT-related thrombosis is an acute prothrombotic condition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic vasospasm is not a recognized mechanism of post-CEA restenosis. Scar tissue does not cause clinically significant vasospasm leading to high-grade stenosis.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with a sudden episode of complete vision loss in her left eye that lasted approximately 5 minutes before spontaneously resolving. She describes it as a "curtain coming down" over her visual field. She has a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Carotid duplex ultrasonography reveals 90% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. Fundoscopic examination performed at the time of the event would most likely have shown which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Papilledema with blurring of the optic disc margins and absent venous pulsations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hollenhorst plaques (refractile cholesterol crystals) at retinal arteriolar bifurcations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cotton-wool spots scattered diffusely throughout the retina with dot-blot hemorrhages

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cupping of the optic disc with an increased cup-to-disc ratio greater than 0.6

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neovascularization of the optic disc with preretinal and vitreous hemorrhages

explanation.summary: Amaurosis fugax (transient monocular vision loss) associated with ipsilateral carotid stenosis is caused by retinal artery embolization. Hollenhorst plaques (cholesterol crystal emboli) at arteriolar bifurcations are the classic fundoscopic finding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amaurosis fugax from carotid stenosis results from cholesterol-rich atheromatous debris embolizing from the carotid plaque to the ophthalmic and retinal circulation. Hollenhorst plaques are bright, refractile, yellow-orange cholesterol crystals that lodge at retinal arteriolar bifurcation points. Their presence on fundoscopy is pathognomonic for proximal atheroembolism, most commonly from ipsilateral carotid disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Papilledema results from elevated intracranial pressure and presents with bilateral optic disc swelling, not transient monocular vision loss. It is unrelated to carotid stenosis or retinal embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diffuse cotton-wool spots with dot-blot hemorrhages suggest diabetic retinopathy or hypertensive retinopathy, which are chronic microvascular conditions, not the acute embolic mechanism underlying amaurosis fugax.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Optic disc cupping with an increased cup-to-disc ratio is characteristic of chronic open-angle glaucoma, a condition caused by optic nerve damage from elevated intraocular pressure, unrelated to carotid embolic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neovascularization of the disc with preretinal and vitreous hemorrhages is seen in proliferative diabetic retinopathy, driven by chronic retinal ischemia and VEGF-mediated angiogenesis, not acute atheroembolism from carotid disease.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man undergoes an uneventful left carotid endarterectomy for symptomatic 85% internal carotid artery stenosis. Approximately 6 hours postoperatively, the patient develops severe right-sided headache, hypertension (blood pressure 210/115 mmHg), and a new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizure. CT of the head shows no evidence of intracranial hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute postoperative stroke from thrombosis at the endarterectomy site

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome following restoration of carotid blood flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertensive encephalopathy unrelated to the surgical procedure

choices.label: D
choices.text: New-onset primary epilepsy precipitated by general anesthesia and surgical stress

choices.label: E
choices.text: Postoperative meningitis from intraoperative contamination of the carotid sheath

explanation.summary: Cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome (CHS) occurs after CEA when restoration of blood flow to a chronically hypoperfused hemisphere exceeds cerebral autoregulatory capacity, leading to headache, hypertension, seizures, and potentially intracerebral hemorrhage.
explanation.whyCorrect: CHS typically develops within hours to days after CEA and results from impaired cerebral autoregulation in a hemisphere that has been chronically hypoperfused due to severe carotid stenosis. When normal perfusion pressure is restored, the maximally vasodilated cerebral vasculature cannot autoregulate, leading to hyperperfusion. Clinical features include ipsilateral headache, hypertension, seizures, focal neurological deficits, and potentially fatal intracerebral hemorrhage. Strict blood pressure control is essential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute thrombotic stroke at the endarterectomy site would present with contralateral focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) rather than ipsilateral headache and seizures. Additionally, a CT showing no hemorrhage would likely show early ischemic changes if a large-territory stroke had occurred.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While hypertensive encephalopathy shares features with CHS (headache, seizures, hypertension), the temporal relationship to CEA and the pathophysiology of impaired autoregulation in a previously hypoperfused hemisphere make CHS the more specific and accurate diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: New-onset primary epilepsy in a 69-year-old is unlikely, and the temporal association with CEA, ipsilateral headache, and marked hypertension strongly suggest CHS rather than idiopathic seizure onset.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Postoperative meningitis would not present within 6 hours and would be expected to cause fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status rather than the triad of ipsilateral headache, hypertension, and seizures seen in CHS.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man undergoes a left carotid endarterectomy. Postoperatively, the patient is noted to have a left-sided tongue deviation and difficulty with speech articulation, but his tongue sensation is intact and he has no facial droop. Which of the following nerves was most likely injured during the procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve (CN VII)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of CN X)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Superior laryngeal nerve (branch of CN X)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)

explanation.summary: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) crosses the internal and external carotid arteries and is vulnerable to injury during CEA. Damage causes ipsilateral tongue deviation toward the side of injury and dysarthria.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hypoglossal nerve courses superficially over the internal and external carotid arteries, making it the most commonly injured cranial nerve during CEA. Injury causes ipsilateral tongue deviation (toward the affected side) due to unopposed contralateral genioglossus muscle action, along with dysarthria. Tongue sensation is spared because it is supplied by the lingual nerve (CN V3) and glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX).

whyWrongByChoice.A: The marginal mandibular branch of CN VII innervates the depressor muscles of the lower lip. Injury would cause asymmetric smile and inability to depress the lower lip on the affected side, not tongue deviation or dysarthria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causes ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis, presenting as hoarseness, not tongue deviation. While it can be injured during CEA, the clinical presentation described is inconsistent with this nerve's function.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The superior laryngeal nerve (external branch) innervates the cricothyroid muscle. Injury causes loss of high-pitched vocalization and voice fatigue, not tongue deviation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) provides sensation to the posterior tongue and pharynx and innervates the stylopharyngeus muscle. Injury would impair the gag reflex and pharyngeal sensation, not cause tongue deviation.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease and prior neck radiation for laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma 8 years ago is found to have symptomatic 80% left internal carotid artery stenosis after experiencing a transient ischemic attack. He is being evaluated for carotid revascularization. Which of the following factors most strongly favors carotid artery stenting over carotid endarterectomy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age greater than 65 years, which is associated with lower periprocedural risk with stenting

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concomitant coronary artery disease requiring future coronary artery bypass grafting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prior neck radiation causing tissue fibrosis and increased surgical complication risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presence of a calcified atherosclerotic plaque, which is more safely treated with stenting

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left-sided location of the stenosis, which provides better anatomic access for stenting

explanation.summary: Prior cervical radiation is a major factor favoring carotid artery stenting (CAS) over CEA because radiation-induced tissue fibrosis significantly increases the risk of wound complications, cranial nerve injury, and technical difficulty during open surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prior neck radiation causes extensive fibrosis and scarring of cervical tissues, making surgical dissection during CEA technically challenging and increasing the risk of cranial nerve injury, wound healing complications, and vascular injury. This is a well-established indication for CAS over CEA in current guidelines. CAS avoids the need for cervical dissection through irradiated tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Advanced age (>70 years) is actually associated with higher periprocedural stroke risk with CAS compared to CEA. The CREST trial demonstrated that older patients had better outcomes with CEA, while younger patients had comparable outcomes with either procedure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While concomitant coronary artery disease increases overall surgical risk, it is not a specific indication for CAS over CEA. If CABG is planned, combined or staged procedures are considered, but the decision depends on the urgency and clinical context.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heavily calcified plaques can actually be more difficult to treat with stenting because they resist adequate expansion of the stent and increase the risk of inadequate plaque coverage. Calcification is not a factor that favors stenting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The side of stenosis does not determine procedural preference between CAS and CEA. Both procedures can be performed on either side, and the laterality does not influence the relative safety or efficacy of the two approaches.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents for evaluation of a neck bruit detected incidentally during a routine physical examination. She has no history of transient ischemic attack, stroke, or visual symptoms. Carotid duplex ultrasonography reveals 55% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. Her LDL cholesterol is 145 mg/dL, and she is not currently on any medications. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proceed with carotid endarterectomy given the presence of a hemodynamically significant stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate carotid artery stenting with an embolic protection device to prevent future stroke

choices.label: C
choices.text: Begin optimal medical therapy with antiplatelet agent, statin, and antihypertensive as indicated

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain a cerebral angiogram to better characterize the plaque morphology before treatment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform a transcranial Doppler to assess microembolic signals before deciding on intervention

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic carotid stenosis <60% does not meet the threshold for surgical intervention. Optimal medical therapy (antiplatelet, statin, risk factor modification) is the cornerstone of management.
explanation.whyCorrect: For asymptomatic carotid stenosis less than 60%, revascularization is not recommended regardless of the patient's surgical risk. The annual stroke risk is low (~1-2%/year), and optimal medical therapy alone provides adequate risk reduction. This includes antiplatelet therapy (aspirin), high-intensity statin therapy to achieve LDL <70 mg/dL, blood pressure control, smoking cessation, and periodic surveillance ultrasound.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CEA is not indicated for asymptomatic stenosis of 55%. The ACAS and ACST trials established the threshold at ≥60% stenosis for potential surgical benefit, and even then, the benefit is marginal and requires a low perioperative complication rate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Carotid artery stenting is not indicated for moderate asymptomatic stenosis. The procedural risk would outweigh any potential benefit in a patient with a 55% lesion and no symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cerebral angiography is an invasive procedure with its own stroke risk (~0.5-1%) and is not routinely indicated for moderate asymptomatic stenosis already characterized by duplex ultrasonography. It adds risk without changing management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While transcranial Doppler detection of microembolic signals is an area of research for risk stratification in asymptomatic stenosis, it is not the standard of care and would not change the recommendation for medical management in a 55% asymptomatic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with symptomatic left internal carotid artery stenosis undergoes preoperative evaluation for carotid endarterectomy. Angiography reveals that the left internal carotid artery stenosis is at the level of the C1-C2 vertebral body, well above the angle of the mandible. The contralateral internal carotid artery is occluded, and the circle of Willis is incomplete with an absent right A1 segment. Which of the following features of this patient's anatomy most increases the perioperative risk of CEA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High location of the carotid lesion requiring distal dissection beyond the angle of the mandible

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left-sided laterality of the stenosis, which is associated with higher stroke risk during CEA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Location of the stenosis at the C1-C2 level, which indicates intracranial extension of the plaque

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presence of bilateral carotid disease, which requires simultaneous bilateral endarterectomy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Absence of the right A1 segment, which prevents collateral flow through the anterior communicating artery

explanation.summary: An incomplete circle of Willis (absent A1 segment) with contralateral ICA occlusion severely limits collateral cerebral perfusion during cross-clamping of the operative ICA, significantly increasing perioperative stroke risk during CEA.
explanation.whyCorrect: During CEA, the internal carotid artery is cross-clamped, making cerebral perfusion entirely dependent on collateral flow. The circle of Willis is the primary collateral pathway, with the anterior communicating artery connecting the two anterior cerebral circulations. With a contralateral ICA occlusion AND an absent right A1 segment, the anterior communicating artery cannot provide cross-flow to the hemisphere during clamping, leaving inadequate collateral perfusion. This dramatically increases the risk of perioperative cerebral ischemia and may necessitate shunt placement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While a high carotid bifurcation or distal lesion can increase technical difficulty, it does not pose as significant a risk as inadequate collateral perfusion. Mandibular subluxation or other techniques can be used to improve exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The laterality of stenosis (left vs. right) does not independently influence perioperative stroke risk during CEA. Risk is determined by stenosis severity, collateral circulation, and surgical technique.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A stenosis at C1-C2 does not necessarily indicate intracranial plaque extension. The extracranial ICA extends to the skull base (approximately C1-C2 level), and while a high lesion increases surgical complexity, it remains extracranial.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral carotid disease does not require simultaneous bilateral endarterectomy. Bilateral CEA should never be performed simultaneously due to the risk of bilateral cranial nerve injury and cerebral hyperperfusion. Staged procedures are standard.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with well-controlled hypertension undergoes carotid duplex ultrasonography as part of preoperative evaluation for coronary artery bypass grafting. The study reveals a heterogeneous, predominantly echolucent plaque causing 70% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. She has never had neurological symptoms. Compared to a homogeneous, echogenic plaque of the same degree of stenosis, which of the following statements about this patient's plaque is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The echolucent plaque has a higher lipid and hemorrhagic content, conferring greater vulnerability to rupture

choices.label: B
choices.text: The echolucent plaque is more heavily calcified, making it less prone to rupture but harder to stent

choices.label: C
choices.text: The echolucent plaque has a lower lipid content and a thicker fibrous cap, reducing embolization risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plaque echogenicity on ultrasound does not correlate with histological composition or clinical risk

choices.label: E
choices.text: The echolucent plaque represents post-stenotic turbulent flow artifact rather than true plaque characteristics

explanation.summary: Echolucent (hypoechoic) carotid plaques are associated with higher lipid content, intraplaque hemorrhage, and a thin or absent fibrous cap, making them more vulnerable to rupture and thromboembolism compared to echogenic (calcified/fibrous) plaques.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ultrasound plaque characterization provides valuable information about composition and risk. Echolucent plaques correspond histologically to lipid-rich, hemorrhagic lesions with thin fibrous caps (vulnerable or unstable plaques). These features are associated with a higher risk of plaque rupture, surface ulceration, and distal embolization. Echogenic plaques, by contrast, are typically fibrous or calcified and more stable. Multiple studies have shown that echolucent plaque morphology is an independent predictor of future neurological events.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Calcified plaques appear hyperechoic (bright) with acoustic shadowing on ultrasound, not echolucent. Echolucency indicates the presence of lipid, hemorrhage, or necrotic debris rather than calcification.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This describes the characteristics of echogenic (stable) plaques, not echolucent ones. Echolucent plaques have higher lipid content and thinner fibrous caps, increasing the risk of rupture and embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This is incorrect. Multiple studies have demonstrated strong correlations between ultrasound plaque echogenicity and histological composition. Plaque characterization by ultrasound is a validated tool for risk stratification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Echolucent plaque appearance represents true tissue characteristics on B-mode ultrasound and is not an artifact of turbulent flow. Flow artifacts are identified on Doppler imaging and are distinct from B-mode plaque morphology.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation who is not on anticoagulation presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left leg pain, pallor, and inability to move her toes. Physical examination reveals a cold, pale left lower extremity with absent femoral, popliteal, and pedal pulses. The contralateral limb has normal pulses. Her heart rate is 98 bpm and irregularly irregular. Which of the following findings on further evaluation would be most consistent with acute arterial embolism rather than acute arterial thrombosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: History of progressive intermittent claudication in the affected limb over the preceding 6 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of well-developed collateral vessels on angiography of the affected lower extremity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diffuse atherosclerotic changes with multi-level stenosis on bilateral lower extremity angiography

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abrupt onset of symptoms without antecedent peripheral arterial disease in the affected limb

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gradual progression of ischemic symptoms over 24-48 hours with partial preservation of motor function

explanation.summary: Acute arterial embolism typically presents with sudden, severe ischemic symptoms in a limb without prior peripheral arterial disease, in contrast to acute thrombosis which usually occurs on a background of pre-existing atherosclerotic disease with collaterals.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arterial embolism characteristically produces abrupt-onset severe ischemia (the classic '6 Ps': pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesias, paralysis) in a limb without prior symptoms of peripheral arterial disease. The absence of pre-existing PAD means no collateral circulation has developed, resulting in more severe acute ischemia. The combination of atrial fibrillation (embolic source) and sudden onset in a previously normal limb strongly favors embolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A history of progressive intermittent claudication suggests pre-existing peripheral arterial disease, which is more consistent with acute arterial thrombosis superimposed on chronic atherosclerotic stenosis rather than embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Well-developed collateral vessels indicate chronic arterial insufficiency, which is seen with pre-existing PAD and is more consistent with in situ thrombosis. Embolic occlusion occurs in previously normal vessels that lack established collaterals.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diffuse bilateral atherosclerotic changes with multi-level stenosis suggest chronic peripheral arterial disease, which predisposes to acute thrombosis rather than embolism. Embolic disease typically affects a single arterial segment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gradual progression of symptoms over 24-48 hours suggests thrombotic occlusion with progressive propagation, not embolism. Embolic occlusion produces an abrupt, well-demarcated ischemic event.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with acute onset of severe right lower extremity pain and numbness beginning 3 hours ago. Physical examination reveals a cold, mottled right leg with absent popliteal and pedal pulses. Motor function is intact but sensation is diminished below the knee. Doppler examination shows no arterial signal at the ankle. Echocardiography reveals a dilated left atrium with a mobile mass attached to the interatrial septum. Which of the following is the most likely source of this patient's arterial embolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left atrial thrombus formed in the setting of chronic atrial fibrillation and stasis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vegetation on the mitral valve from infective endocarditis with septic embolization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left atrial myxoma with tumor fragmentation and distal arterial embolization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism from a deep venous thrombus traversing a patent foramen ovale

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cholesterol crystal embolization from an ulcerated descending thoracic aortic plaque

explanation.summary: A mobile mass on the interatrial septum is characteristic of a left atrial myxoma, the most common primary cardiac tumor. Myxomas can fragment and embolize to any systemic artery, causing acute arterial occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left atrial myxoma is a benign cardiac tumor that typically arises from the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis. It is the most common primary cardiac tumor. Myxomas have a gelatinous, friable surface that is prone to fragmentation, leading to systemic arterial embolization in approximately 30-40% of cases. The description of a mobile mass on the interatrial septum is classic for myxoma. Embolic events can affect cerebral, visceral, or peripheral arteries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left atrial thrombus from atrial fibrillation typically forms in the left atrial appendage, not on the interatrial septum. While it is the most common source of cardiac embolism overall, the location of the mass on the septum is characteristic of myxoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mitral valve vegetations from endocarditis would be attached to the valve leaflets, not the interatrial septum. Additionally, the patient lacks the typical features of endocarditis such as fever, murmur, and peripheral stigmata.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paradoxical embolism through a PFO involves right-to-left shunting of venous thrombus. The echocardiographic finding described is a mass on the septum, not a PFO with transit of thrombus, and the presentation is more consistent with myxoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cholesterol crystal embolization (atheroembolism) from aortic plaques typically causes small-vessel occlusion (blue toe syndrome, livedo reticularis, renal failure), not large-vessel occlusion causing absent popliteal and pedal pulses.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a recent anterior wall ST-elevation myocardial infarction complicated by a large apical akinetic segment presents 10 days later with sudden severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, and abdominal distension. He is tachycardic, hypotensive, and has diffuse abdominal tenderness with guarding. Laboratory studies show a lactate of 8.2 mmol/L, leukocyte count of 22,000/μL, and metabolic acidosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute mesenteric ischemia from embolism to the superior mesenteric artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute pancreatitis from common bile duct obstruction by a gallstone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Perforated duodenal ulcer with secondary bacterial peritonitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemic colitis from non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia in the watershed areas

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clostridium difficile colitis from recent broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure

explanation.summary: This patient with a recent anterior MI and apical akinesis (predisposing to left ventricular mural thrombus) now presents with signs of acute mesenteric ischemia from arterial embolism to the SMA, manifesting as severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, and elevated lactate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anterior wall MI with apical akinesis creates a nidus for mural thrombus formation in the left ventricle. Embolization of this thrombus to the superior mesenteric artery is a classic cause of acute mesenteric ischemia. The SMA is the most common site of mesenteric embolization due to its acute angle of origin from the aorta. The triad of severe abdominal pain out of proportion to examination (early), followed by bloody diarrhea and peritoneal signs (late), combined with markedly elevated lactate and leukocytosis, is highly suggestive of mesenteric infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute pancreatitis typically presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back, elevated amylase/lipase, and nausea/vomiting. Bloody diarrhea is not a feature of pancreatitis, and the clinical scenario with recent MI and markedly elevated lactate points to mesenteric ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Perforated duodenal ulcer causes sudden epigastric pain with peritonitis but does not typically present with bloody diarrhea or the markedly elevated lactate seen here. Free air under the diaphragm would be expected on imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Non-occlusive mesenteric ischemia (NOMI) affects watershed areas and is typically associated with low cardiac output states or vasopressor use. While possible post-MI, NOMI usually causes ischemic colitis limited to the splenic flexure, not the fulminant presentation described here with a clear embolic source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: C. difficile colitis causes watery (not typically bloody) diarrhea and usually follows antibiotic exposure. It does not cause the degree of hemodynamic instability, markedly elevated lactate, and peritoneal signs seen here, which point to mesenteric infarction.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man presents to the emergency department 2 hours after the sudden onset of a cold, painful, and numb right hand. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and recently stopped taking his warfarin because of a nosebleed. His right hand is pale with absent radial and ulnar pulses. Motor function of the fingers is present but weak. Allen test on the left hand is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate systemic thrombolysis with intravenous alteplase to dissolve the embolus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emergent surgical embolectomy using a Fogarty balloon catheter via brachial arteriotomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate intravenous heparin anticoagulation and schedule urgent surgical embolectomy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply warming blankets to the extremity and start oral anticoagulation with warfarin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform immediate fasciotomy of the forearm compartments to prevent compartment syndrome

explanation.summary: For acute arterial embolism to the upper extremity with viable but threatened tissue, the initial management is systemic heparinization to prevent clot propagation, followed by urgent surgical embolectomy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The initial step in managing acute arterial embolism is systemic anticoagulation with intravenous unfractionated heparin to prevent proximal and distal thrombus propagation while definitive treatment is organized. The limb has motor function (viable but threatened), and surgical embolectomy using a Fogarty balloon catheter is the definitive treatment. Heparin should be started immediately upon diagnosis, and embolectomy should follow urgently. This combined approach provides the best outcomes for limb salvage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Systemic thrombolysis is generally not the first-line treatment for acute embolic arterial occlusion when surgical embolectomy is readily available. Thrombolysis may be considered for thrombotic occlusions or when emboli lodge in distal vessels inaccessible to catheter embolectomy, but it carries higher bleeding risk and is slower than surgical embolectomy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While embolectomy is the definitive treatment, performing it without first administering heparin is suboptimal. Heparin prevents propagation of thrombus proximal and distal to the embolus during the time needed to prepare for surgery and reduces recurrent embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Passive warming and oral anticoagulation alone are wholly inadequate for acute limb-threatening ischemia. This approach fails to address the acute arterial occlusion and risks irreversible tissue damage. Oral warfarin takes days to achieve therapeutic effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fasciotomy is indicated for established compartment syndrome, typically occurring after revascularization (reperfusion injury), not as the initial management of acute arterial embolism. Performing fasciotomy before restoring arterial flow does not address the underlying ischemia.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 1.3) presents with sudden onset of right leg weakness and inability to dorsiflex the right foot. Examination reveals a cool right lower extremity with mottled skin, absent right popliteal and pedal pulses, and loss of sensation over the dorsum of the foot. The left leg is normal. Angiography reveals an abrupt cutoff at the right popliteal artery with a meniscus sign. What does the meniscus sign on angiography indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A chronic atherosclerotic lesion with gradual luminal narrowing and collateral vessel formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: An intimal dissection flap causing partial obstruction of the arterial lumen

choices.label: C
choices.text: A fusiform aneurysmal dilation of the popliteal artery with intraluminal thrombus

choices.label: D
choices.text: An abrupt convex obstruction at the site of an embolus lodged within the arterial lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: External compression of the popliteal artery by a Baker cyst in the popliteal fossa

explanation.summary: The meniscus sign on angiography is the hallmark of arterial embolism: an abrupt, convex intraluminal filling defect where the embolus lodges, producing a cup-shaped cutoff of the contrast column.
explanation.whyCorrect: The meniscus sign (also called the 'reversed meniscus' or 'cup sign') on angiography shows an abrupt, convex obstruction of the arterial lumen where an embolus has lodged. The contrast column terminates abruptly with a concave upper margin (meniscus), as the round embolus sits within a previously normal vessel. This is in contrast to thrombotic occlusions, which typically show tapered (rat-tail) tapering of the vessel with irregular contours and evidence of collateral vessels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chronic atherosclerotic lesions show gradual luminal narrowing with irregular vessel contours and well-developed collateral vessels, not an abrupt cutoff with a meniscus sign.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An intimal dissection flap would show a linear intraluminal filling defect with a double-lumen appearance or irregular intimal irregularity, not the convex filling defect characteristic of embolism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A fusiform popliteal aneurysm with thrombus would show vessel dilation with mural thrombus, not an abrupt cutoff in a normal-caliber vessel.
whyWrongByChoice.E: External compression from a Baker cyst would cause smooth extrinsic narrowing of the popliteal artery, not the intraluminal convex filling defect seen with embolism.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a mechanical aortic valve prosthesis on warfarin therapy presents with sudden onset of left-sided weakness and expressive aphasia. CT of the head shows no hemorrhage. CT angiography reveals occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery. His INR on presentation is 1.8. Intravenous alteplase is administered within the treatment window. Which of the following is the most common cardiac source of arterial emboli causing ischemic stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular mural thrombus following acute myocardial infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nonbacterial thrombotic (marantic) endocarditis on native valve leaflets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mechanical prosthetic valve thrombosis despite therapeutic anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patent foramen ovale with paradoxical embolism from the venous system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left atrial thrombus associated with atrial fibrillation

explanation.summary: Atrial fibrillation with left atrial thrombus formation (typically in the left atrial appendage) is the most common cardiac source of arterial embolism and cardioembolic stroke, accounting for approximately 50% of all cardiogenic emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atrial fibrillation is the most common cardiac source of arterial embolism overall. The loss of organized atrial contraction leads to blood stasis, particularly in the left atrial appendage, promoting thrombus formation. These thrombi can embolize to the cerebral, mesenteric, renal, and peripheral arteries. AF is responsible for approximately 15-20% of all ischemic strokes and is the single most common cause of cardioembolic stroke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left ventricular mural thrombus after MI is an important source of arterial embolism, particularly in anterior MI with apical akinesis, but it is less common than AF-related left atrial thrombus as an overall cause of cardioembolic stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE/marantic endocarditis) is associated with advanced malignancy and hypercoagulable states and can cause arterial embolism, but it is uncommon compared to atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prosthetic valve thrombosis is a serious complication but is relatively rare compared to AF-related thromboembolism. Although this patient has a mechanical valve, the question asks about the most common cardiac source overall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paradoxical embolism through a PFO is an important consideration, particularly in cryptogenic stroke in younger patients, but it is far less common than AF as a source of cardioembolic stroke in the general population.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman undergoes successful surgical embolectomy of the right femoral artery for acute limb ischemia caused by a cardiogenic embolus. Approximately 4 hours after revascularization, she develops worsening right leg pain, tense swelling of the calf, pain with passive extension of the toes, and diminished sensation in the first web space. Her creatine kinase is 12,000 U/L and potassium is 6.1 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate and insulin with dextrose for hyperkalemia management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apply a compression bandage and elevate the extremity to reduce postoperative edema

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate continuous renal replacement therapy for rhabdomyolysis-induced acute kidney injury

choices.label: D
choices.text: Return to the operating room for re-exploration of the embolectomy site for recurrent occlusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform emergent four-compartment fasciotomy of the right lower leg

explanation.summary: This patient has developed acute compartment syndrome of the lower leg following reperfusion, evidenced by tense swelling, pain with passive stretch, and sensory deficit. Emergent four-compartment fasciotomy is the definitive treatment to prevent irreversible neuromuscular damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reperfusion after acute limb ischemia causes ischemia-reperfusion injury with capillary leak, tissue edema, and elevated compartment pressures. The clinical findings (tense compartments, pain with passive stretch of the involved muscles, sensory deficit in the deep peroneal nerve distribution) are classic for compartment syndrome. Emergent four-compartment fasciotomy (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior) is mandatory to prevent irreversible muscle necrosis and nerve damage. The elevated CK and hyperkalemia reflect rhabdomyolysis from muscle damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While managing hyperkalemia is important (and should be done concurrently), it addresses the systemic consequence rather than the underlying cause. Without fasciotomy, ongoing muscle necrosis will continue to release potassium and myoglobin, worsening both hyperkalemia and renal injury.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Compression bandaging is absolutely contraindicated in compartment syndrome as it would further increase compartment pressure and worsen ischemic damage. Elevation alone is insufficient to address established compartment syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renal replacement therapy may ultimately be needed if acute kidney injury develops from myoglobinuria, but the immediate priority is fasciotomy to halt ongoing muscle destruction. Treating the compartment syndrome is the primary intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The clinical presentation with tense compartments and pain with passive stretch is consistent with compartment syndrome, not recurrent arterial occlusion. Recurrent occlusion would present with a pale, cool extremity with absent pulses, not the tense swelling and reperfusion findings described.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman undergoes cardiac catheterization via the right femoral artery for evaluation of chest pain. Three days later, she develops painful, cyanotic toes on both feet (blue toe syndrome), livedo reticularis of the lower extremities, and an elevated serum creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). Peripheral pulses are palpable bilaterally. Eosinophilia is noted on the complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large-vessel thromboembolism from the catheterization site to the bilateral iliac arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contrast-induced nephropathy with coincidental acrocyanosis from peripheral vasospasm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cholesterol crystal embolization syndrome from disruption of aortic atheromatous plaques

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with bilateral lower extremity deep venous thrombosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systemic vasculitis with renal involvement and digital ischemia from immune complex deposition

explanation.summary: Cholesterol crystal embolization (atheroembolic disease) is caused by disruption of aortic atheromatous plaques during catheterization, releasing cholesterol crystals that lodge in small arterioles. It presents with blue toe syndrome, livedo reticularis, renal failure, and eosinophilia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cholesterol crystal embolization occurs when atheromatous debris is dislodged from the aorta during catheterization, surgery, or anticoagulation. Cholesterol crystals shower into small arterioles, causing mechanical occlusion and inflammatory reaction. The classic triad is blue/purple toes with palpable pulses (distinguishing from large-vessel occlusion), livedo reticularis, and renal insufficiency. Eosinophilia, elevated ESR, and hypocomplementemia are characteristic laboratory findings. Skin biopsy shows biconvex (needle-shaped) cholesterol clefts within arterioles.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Large-vessel thromboembolism would cause acute limb ischemia with absent pulses, not blue toes with palpable pulses. The preservation of pedal pulses is a key distinguishing feature of small-vessel cholesterol embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Contrast-induced nephropathy can cause renal failure after catheterization, but it does not explain the blue toes, livedo reticularis, or eosinophilia. CIN typically presents with isolated creatinine elevation 24-72 hours post-contrast.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIT causes thrombocytopenia and thrombosis (arterial or venous), but the presentation with bilateral blue toes, livedo reticularis, palpable pulses, eosinophilia, and renal failure is not characteristic of HIT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Systemic vasculitis could cause renal failure and digital ischemia, but the temporal relationship to catheterization, the blue toe syndrome with palpable pulses, and eosinophilia are classic for cholesterol embolization rather than vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of left flank pain, nausea, and hematuria. His blood pressure is 165/95 mmHg and heart rate is 110 bpm with an irregularly irregular rhythm. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) of 850 U/L, elevated serum creatinine of 2.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.1 mg/dL), and microscopic hematuria. CT angiography of the abdomen reveals a wedge-shaped perfusion defect in the left kidney. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left renal vein thrombosis causing venous infarction of the kidney

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from recent medication exposure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal artery embolism causing segmental renal infarction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous rupture of a renal cell carcinoma with hemorrhagic infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute pyelonephritis with focal abscess formation in the renal parenchyma

explanation.summary: This patient with atrial fibrillation presents with classic features of renal artery embolism causing renal infarction: acute flank pain, hematuria, elevated LDH, acute kidney injury, and a wedge-shaped perfusion defect on CT angiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Renal artery embolism, most commonly from left atrial thrombus in the setting of atrial fibrillation, causes segmental or complete renal infarction. The classic presentation includes sudden-onset flank pain, nausea, hematuria, and markedly elevated LDH (a sensitive marker of tissue infarction). CT angiography shows a characteristic wedge-shaped perfusion defect corresponding to the affected renal arterial territory. The combination of AF, sudden flank pain, elevated LDH, and the imaging findings is highly characteristic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Renal vein thrombosis causes renal congestion and edema with flank pain and hematuria, but it is associated with nephrotic syndrome and hypercoagulable states. The wedge-shaped arterial perfusion defect and markedly elevated LDH are more consistent with arterial infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute interstitial nephritis presents with fever, rash, eosinophilia, and sterile pyuria following medication exposure. It does not cause wedge-shaped perfusion defects or the markedly elevated LDH seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Spontaneous rupture of a renal mass could cause flank pain and hematuria, but the wedge-shaped perfusion defect, markedly elevated LDH, and clinical context of atrial fibrillation strongly favor arterial embolism over a tumor-related event.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute pyelonephritis with abscess presents with fever, pyuria, and flank pain but would show a rim-enhancing fluid collection rather than a wedge-shaped perfusion defect. The markedly elevated LDH and absence of infectious symptoms make this unlikely.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with chronic atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban presents with sudden onset of severe right lower extremity pain. Examination reveals a cold, pale right leg with no palpable pulses below the femoral artery. Sensation and motor function are absent below the knee. The limb has been ischemic for an estimated 8 hours. CT angiography confirms complete occlusion of the right superficial femoral artery with no reconstitution distally. Which of the following is the most significant concern regarding revascularization at this time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Graft occlusion from poor arterial runoff in the distal vasculature

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myonephropathic metabolic syndrome from reperfusion of irreversibly ischemic tissue

choices.label: C
choices.text: Distal embolization of residual thrombus into the tibial arteries during embolectomy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pseudoaneurysm formation at the arteriotomy site requiring surgical revision

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recurrent embolization from the left atrial appendage in the immediate postoperative period

explanation.summary: With 8 hours of complete ischemia and loss of sensory and motor function, the limb likely has irreversible muscle damage. Revascularization at this point risks myonephropathic metabolic syndrome (reperfusion syndrome) with release of potassium, myoglobin, lactate, and other toxic metabolites causing hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, cardiac arrest, and acute renal failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Myonephropathic metabolic syndrome (reperfusion syndrome) is the most feared complication of revascularizing an irreversibly ischemic limb. After prolonged ischemia (>6 hours with complete sensorimotor loss), skeletal muscle undergoes extensive necrosis. Reperfusion releases massive amounts of potassium (causing fatal hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest), myoglobin (causing acute tubular necrosis and renal failure), lactic acid (causing severe metabolic acidosis), and inflammatory mediators. In some cases, primary amputation may be safer than attempted revascularization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Poor distal runoff is a concern for long-term graft patency but is not the most immediate life-threatening concern in this scenario. The systemic metabolic consequences of reperfusion pose a far greater acute risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Distal embolization during embolectomy is a technical concern but can be managed with completion angiography and additional catheter passes. It is not the primary concern when weighing the decision to revascularize an irreversibly ischemic limb.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pseudoaneurysm formation at the arteriotomy site is an uncommon postoperative complication that can be managed electively. It is not the major concern driving the revascularization decision in prolonged ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Recurrent embolization is a long-term concern managed with anticoagulation, but it is not the most significant immediate risk when considering revascularization of an 8-hour ischemic limb with complete sensorimotor loss.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a recent ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with primary percutaneous coronary intervention 5 days ago presents with acute-onset right-sided weakness and global aphasia. CT of the head is negative for hemorrhage, and CT angiography shows occlusion of the left middle cerebral artery M1 segment. His NIH Stroke Scale score is 18 and symptom onset was 2 hours ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's acute stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous alteplase followed by endovascular mechanical thrombectomy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous alteplase alone, as thrombectomy is only indicated for posterior circulation strokes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endovascular thrombectomy alone without intravenous thrombolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of intravenous heparin anticoagulation and admission to a stroke unit for monitoring

choices.label: E
choices.text: Emergent decompressive hemicraniectomy to prevent herniation from cerebral edema

explanation.summary: For acute ischemic stroke with large-vessel occlusion (MCA M1) presenting within the treatment window, the standard of care is intravenous alteplase (if within 4.5 hours) combined with endovascular mechanical thrombectomy (if within 24 hours with appropriate imaging selection).
explanation.whyCorrect: Current guidelines recommend a bridging approach for acute ischemic stroke with large-vessel anterior circulation occlusion: intravenous alteplase should be administered as soon as possible (within 4.5 hours of onset), and endovascular mechanical thrombectomy should be performed in addition (within 6-24 hours depending on imaging criteria). Multiple landmark trials (MR CLEAN, ESCAPE, EXTEND-IA, SWIFT PRIME, REVASCAT) demonstrated that thrombectomy significantly improves functional outcomes in large-vessel occlusion stroke. IV tPA should not be delayed for thrombectomy preparation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombectomy is primarily indicated for large-vessel anterior circulation strokes (ICA, MCA M1/M2), not exclusively posterior circulation strokes. In fact, the strongest evidence for thrombectomy is in anterior circulation large-vessel occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IV alteplase should not be withheld when the patient is within the treatment window (4.5 hours). While some recent data suggest thrombectomy alone may be non-inferior in certain settings, the current standard of care remains bridging with IV tPA followed by thrombectomy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anticoagulation alone is insufficient for acute large-vessel occlusion stroke. Heparin does not dissolve established thrombus and does not provide the acute recanalization needed to salvage ischemic penumbra tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decompressive hemicraniectomy is considered for malignant MCA territory infarction with cerebral edema and impending herniation, typically 24-48 hours after stroke onset. It is not the initial treatment for acute stroke presentation.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with a history of chronic atrial fibrillation, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, prior stroke, and congestive heart failure (CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 7) presents for discussion of stroke prevention. He has been on aspirin monotherapy. His renal function is normal (CrCl 78 mL/min), and he has no history of mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy to reduce his risk of arterial thromboembolism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue aspirin monotherapy, as his bleeding risk outweighs the benefit of anticoagulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add clopidogrel to aspirin for dual antiplatelet therapy as an alternative to anticoagulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate dose-adjusted warfarin with a target INR of 2.0-3.0 as the preferred first-line agent

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiate a direct oral anticoagulant such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, or dabigatran

choices.label: E
choices.text: Refer for percutaneous left atrial appendage closure device as the sole intervention

explanation.summary: For nonvalvular atrial fibrillation with a high CHA₂DS₂-VASc score, direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are recommended over warfarin as first-line therapy for stroke prevention due to superior safety profile, predictable pharmacokinetics, and at least equivalent efficacy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Current guidelines recommend DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) as first-line anticoagulation over warfarin for stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. This patient has no mechanical valve or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (which would require warfarin). DOACs have been shown in large randomized trials (RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48) to be at least as effective as warfarin in preventing stroke/systemic embolism, with significantly lower rates of intracranial hemorrhage. His CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 7 confers very high annual stroke risk (~9-10%), making anticoagulation essential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin monotherapy is inadequate for stroke prevention in AF with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 7. Aspirin provides minimal stroke risk reduction compared to anticoagulation and is no longer recommended as an alternative to oral anticoagulation for AF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) is inferior to oral anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF and carries a significant bleeding risk. It is only considered when oral anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While warfarin is effective for stroke prevention in AF, DOACs are preferred over warfarin as first-line therapy in nonvalvular AF due to their superior safety profile (lower intracranial hemorrhage risk), lack of need for regular INR monitoring, fewer drug-food interactions, and predictable pharmacokinetics.
