whyWrongByChoice.E: Left atrial appendage closure (e.g., Watchman device) is considered for patients with AF who have contraindications to long-term anticoagulation. It is not recommended as the sole first-line intervention when oral anticoagulation can be used safely.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with progressive left leg swelling and pain over the past 3 days. She returned from a transatlantic flight 5 days ago and has been taking combined oral contraceptive pills for the past 2 years. Physical examination reveals pitting edema of the left calf and thigh with a circumference difference of 4 cm compared to the right leg. Compression ultrasonography of the left lower extremity confirms a noncompressible segment of the femoral vein. Which of the following best explains the primary mechanism by which oral contraceptive use predisposes this patient to thrombus formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct toxic injury to the venous endothelium resulting in exposure of subendothelial collagen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased hepatic synthesis of procoagulant factors and decreased synthesis of natural anticoagulant proteins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors leading to enhanced platelet aggregation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Suppression of fibrinolytic activity through upregulation of plasminogen activator inhibitor-1

choices.label: E
choices.text: Induction of antiphospholipid antibodies that interfere with the protein C anticoagulant pathway

explanation.summary: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives increase hepatic production of procoagulant factors (fibrinogen, factors II, VII, VIII, X) while simultaneously decreasing synthesis of natural anticoagulants (antithrombin III, protein S), creating a hypercoagulable state consistent with Virchow's triad (hypercoagulability).
explanation.whyCorrect: Estrogen stimulates the liver to produce increased levels of several clotting factors, including fibrinogen and factors II, VII, VIII, and X. Concurrently, it reduces the hepatic synthesis of anticoagulant proteins such as antithrombin III and protein S, and increases activated protein C resistance. This shift in the coagulation balance toward a prothrombotic state is the primary mechanism of OCP-associated venous thromboembolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: OCPs do not cause direct endothelial toxicity or injury. Endothelial damage is a separate component of Virchow's triad and is more relevant to trauma, surgery, or catheter-related thrombosis, not hormonal contraceptive use.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GPIIb/IIIa activation is primarily involved in arterial thrombus formation (platelet-rich, white thrombi). Venous thrombosis is predominantly driven by coagulation cascade activation and fibrin formation rather than primary platelet aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some studies suggest estrogen may modestly affect fibrinolytic parameters, the primary mechanism of OCP-associated thrombosis is increased procoagulant factor synthesis and decreased anticoagulant proteins, not suppression of fibrinolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antiphospholipid antibodies are produced in antiphospholipid syndrome, an autoimmune condition. OCPs do not induce the formation of antiphospholipid antibodies.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man undergoes right total hip arthroplasty for severe osteoarthritis. On postoperative day 4, he develops acute swelling, warmth, and tenderness of the right lower extremity. Duplex ultrasonography reveals an occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal vein extending into the superficial femoral vein. He has no history of bleeding disorders or recent gastrointestinal hemorrhage. His platelet count is 210,000/mm³ and creatinine clearance is 85 mL/min. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial anticoagulation therapy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin monotherapy with a target international normalized ratio of 2.0 to 3.0

choices.label: B
choices.text: Unfractionated heparin infusion bridged with warfarin until the INR is therapeutic for 24 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-molecular-weight heparin with transition to warfarin or a direct oral anticoagulant

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin 325 mg daily combined with clopidogrel for dual antiplatelet therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inferior vena cava filter placement as primary treatment without anticoagulation therapy

explanation.summary: For acute proximal DVT, initial parenteral anticoagulation with LMWH (or fondaparinux or UFH) followed by transition to long-term oral anticoagulation (warfarin or DOAC) is the standard of care. LMWH is preferred over UFH in most patients due to predictable pharmacokinetics and lower risk of HIT.
explanation.whyCorrect: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) is the preferred initial anticoagulant for most patients with acute proximal DVT. It provides immediate anticoagulation with predictable dosing, does not require routine monitoring, has a lower incidence of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia than UFH, and can be transitioned to either warfarin (with overlap until INR 2-3 for at least 24 hours and minimum 5-day LMWH course) or a direct oral anticoagulant.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Warfarin monotherapy without initial parenteral anticoagulation is contraindicated because warfarin initially creates a transient prothrombotic state by depleting protein C and protein S (short half-life anticoagulant factors) before reducing procoagulant factor levels, potentially causing warfarin-induced skin necrosis or thrombus propagation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While UFH bridged with warfarin is an acceptable regimen, LMWH is generally preferred over UFH for most patients with acute DVT due to its superior bioavailability, predictable dose-response, reduced need for monitoring, and lower risk of HIT. UFH is reserved for patients with severe renal insufficiency or when rapid reversibility is needed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dual antiplatelet therapy is used for arterial thrombotic conditions such as acute coronary syndrome and post-coronary stent placement. It is not appropriate for the treatment of venous thromboembolism, which requires anticoagulation targeting the coagulation cascade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IVC filters are indicated only when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated (e.g., active hemorrhage, recent hemorrhagic stroke) or as an adjunct in patients who fail anticoagulation. They do not treat the existing DVT and carry risks of filter thrombosis, migration, and caval penetration.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of unprovoked bilateral pulmonary embolism 3 years ago, currently on warfarin, presents for evaluation of recurrent left leg swelling. She mentions she also had a spontaneous DVT at age 40. Laboratory studies reveal an INR of 2.5. Her family history is significant for a brother who died of a massive PE at age 50. A thrombophilia workup reveals a markedly elevated homocysteine level, functional protein C and protein S within normal limits, and a negative lupus anticoagulant screen. Factor V Leiden testing is positive for a homozygous mutation. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this genetic mutation promotes venous thrombosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Production of a structurally abnormal prothrombin molecule that is resistant to inactivation by antithrombin III

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deficiency of tissue factor pathway inhibitor resulting in unopposed activation of the extrinsic coagulation pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Point mutation in factor V rendering it resistant to proteolytic inactivation by activated protein C

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gain-of-function mutation in factor VIII leading to excessive intrinsic pathway amplification

choices.label: E
choices.text: Impaired release of tissue plasminogen activator from endothelial cells causing defective fibrinolysis

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden is caused by a point mutation (G1691A) in the factor V gene, resulting in an arginine-to-glutamine substitution at position 506. This makes activated factor V resistant to cleavage and inactivation by activated protein C (APC resistance), leading to sustained thrombin generation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia. The mutation at the APC cleavage site (Arg506Gln) prevents activated protein C from cleaving and inactivating factor Va. This results in prolonged factor Va activity, continued prothrombinase complex function, excessive thrombin generation, and a hypercoagulable state. Homozygous individuals have a 50-80-fold increased risk of VTE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This does not describe Factor V Leiden. The prothrombin G20210A mutation is a separate thrombophilia that causes elevated prothrombin levels (not resistance to antithrombin III), and it involves the prothrombin gene, not factor V.
whyWrongByChoice.B: TFPI deficiency is extremely rare and is not the mechanism of Factor V Leiden. Factor V Leiden specifically involves resistance to activated protein C, not a defect in the tissue factor pathway inhibitor system.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated factor VIII levels are an independent risk factor for VTE, but this is not the mechanism of Factor V Leiden. Factor V Leiden involves a structural change in factor V itself, not a gain-of-function mutation in factor VIII.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Impaired tPA release causing defective fibrinolysis is not the mechanism of Factor V Leiden. This mutation affects the protein C anticoagulant pathway, not the fibrinolytic system.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with a newly diagnosed proximal DVT of the left lower extremity. Anticoagulation with unfractionated heparin is initiated. On day 6 of therapy, routine laboratory studies reveal a platelet count of 62,000/mm³, decreased from an admission value of 245,000/mm³. The patient has developed new erythema and pain at the heparin injection site, and a repeat ultrasound shows extension of the thrombus into the iliac vein. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue the current unfractionated heparin infusion and add aspirin to improve platelet function

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discontinue all forms of heparin and initiate anticoagulation with argatroban

choices.label: C
choices.text: Switch from unfractionated heparin to low-molecular-weight heparin at therapeutic doses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transfuse pooled platelets to achieve a platelet count above 100,000/mm³ before continuing heparin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Discontinue heparin and immediately start warfarin monotherapy at a loading dose of 10 mg

explanation.summary: This patient has heparin-induced thrombocytopenia type II (HIT), evidenced by a >50% drop in platelets occurring 5-10 days after heparin initiation with paradoxical thrombotic extension. All heparin must be stopped and a non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban or bivalirudin) must be started.
explanation.whyCorrect: HIT type II is an immune-mediated prothrombotic condition caused by IgG antibodies against the heparin-platelet factor 4 (PF4) complex. This patient has a high 4T score: >50% platelet drop, timing at day 5-10, new thrombosis, and no alternative cause. Management requires immediate cessation of ALL heparin products and initiation of a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban (a direct thrombin inhibitor), which does not cross-react with HIT antibodies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Continuing heparin in HIT is dangerous and can lead to life-threatening thrombotic complications including limb gangrene, stroke, PE, and death. Aspirin does not treat HIT and would not prevent the antibody-mediated platelet activation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: LMWH has significant cross-reactivity (>90% in vitro) with HIT antibodies and is contraindicated in confirmed or suspected HIT. Switching to LMWH would perpetuate the immune-mediated process and potentially worsen thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet transfusion is contraindicated in HIT because it provides additional substrate for antibody-mediated platelet activation, potentially fueling further thrombosis. Thrombocytopenia in HIT is consumptive, not due to production failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warfarin is contraindicated as initial therapy in acute HIT because it rapidly depletes protein C, which can precipitate venous limb gangrene and warfarin-induced skin necrosis. Warfarin should only be introduced after the platelet count has recovered to >150,000/mm³ while on a non-heparin anticoagulant.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks' gestation presents with acute onset of left leg swelling, pain, and discoloration. She has no prior history of thromboembolism. On examination, the left leg is markedly edematous from the groin to the ankle, tense, and cyanotic with diminished pedal pulses. Compression ultrasonography reveals extensive thrombosis involving the left common iliac, external iliac, common femoral, and superficial femoral veins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superficial thrombophlebitis of the great saphenous vein with secondary cellulitis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute arterial embolism to the left lower extremity from a cardiac source

choices.label: C
choices.text: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens from massive iliofemoral deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Necrotizing fasciitis of the left lower extremity with compartment syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic venous insufficiency with acute lipodermatosclerosis and stasis dermatitis

explanation.summary: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens is a severe form of DVT characterized by massive iliofemoral venous thrombosis causing near-total venous outflow obstruction. It presents with a painful, edematous, cyanotic limb with potential compromise of arterial inflow due to elevated compartment pressures.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient presents with the classic triad of phlegmasia cerulea dolens: severe pain, massive edema, and cyanosis of the entire limb with diminished pulses. The extensive iliofemoral venous thrombosis causes near-complete obstruction of venous return, leading to massive fluid sequestration in the limb, elevated tissue pressures, and secondary arterial compromise. Pregnancy is a known risk factor due to compression of the left iliac vein by the gravid uterus and hypercoagulability of pregnancy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Superficial thrombophlebitis presents with localized erythema, tenderness, and a palpable cord along a superficial vein. It does not cause massive limb edema, cyanosis, or diminished pulses as seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute arterial embolism presents with the 6 P's (pain, pallor, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia) and a pale, cold limb—not the cyanotic, edematous limb seen here. Arterial occlusion does not cause massive edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Necrotizing fasciitis presents with rapidly spreading erythema, severe pain disproportionate to findings, crepitus, systemic toxicity, and bullae formation. It is an infectious condition, not a venous thrombotic process, and would not show extensive venous thrombosis on ultrasound.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic venous insufficiency develops over months to years and presents with chronic edema, hemosiderin deposition, lipodermatosclerosis, and venous ulceration. It does not present acutely with cyanosis and diminished pulses.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of proximal DVT treated with 6 months of anticoagulation 2 years ago presents with chronic left leg heaviness, aching, and swelling that worsens by the end of the day. Physical examination reveals pitting edema of the left ankle, brownish discoloration of the medial malleolar skin, and a shallow, irregularly shaped ulcer above the medial malleolus. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this patient's skin findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recurrent microembolization of thrombus fragments to the dermal capillary bed causing tissue infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sustained ambulatory venous hypertension from valvular incompetence causing capillary leak and fibrin cuff formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial insufficiency from progressive atherosclerosis of the tibial and peroneal arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymphatic obstruction from residual thrombus causing protein-rich interstitial edema and fibrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autoimmune vasculitis of small dermal vessels triggered by prior thrombus-associated antigen exposure

explanation.summary: This patient has post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS), a common long-term complication of DVT. Destruction of venous valves by the prior thrombus leads to chronic ambulatory venous hypertension, capillary distension, leakage of fibrinogen and red blood cells, pericapillary fibrin cuff formation, hemosiderin deposition, lipodermatosclerosis, and ultimately venous stasis ulceration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-thrombotic syndrome occurs in 20-50% of patients after proximal DVT. Thrombus-induced venous valve destruction and residual luminal obstruction cause persistent ambulatory venous hypertension. This leads to capillary distension and increased permeability, extravasation of macromolecules and red blood cells, pericapillary fibrin cuff formation (which impairs oxygen and nutrient diffusion), hemosiderin deposition (causing the brownish skin discoloration), and progressive tissue injury culminating in venous ulceration at the medial malleolus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Microembolization is not the mechanism of PTS. The skin changes result from sustained venous hypertension transmitted to the dermal microcirculation, not from showering of embolic fragments.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Arterial insufficiency ulcers typically occur on the distal toes, foot dorsum, or lateral malleolus, are painful, have a punched-out appearance with pale base, and are associated with absent pulses and claudication. This patient's medial malleolar ulcer with hemosiderin staining is characteristic of venous disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some lymphatic dysfunction may develop secondarily, the primary pathophysiology of PTS is venous valvular incompetence and ambulatory venous hypertension, not primary lymphatic obstruction. Lymphedema characteristically causes non-pitting edema, not the pitting edema and hemosiderin changes seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PTS is not an autoimmune condition. It results from mechanical venous valve damage and chronic venous hypertension following DVT, not from an immune-mediated vasculitic process.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma presents with acute right calf pain and swelling. Compression ultrasonography confirms an isolated popliteal vein DVT. He has a platelet count of 85,000/mm³ and has been experiencing intermittent hematuria from a bladder mass. His oncologist notes he has a poor prognosis with an estimated life expectancy of 3 months. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation strategy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warfarin with a target INR of 2.0-3.0 for a minimum duration of 3 months

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rivaroxaban at standard dosing for 6 months with reassessment at that time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-molecular-weight heparin monotherapy for the duration of active malignancy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin 81 mg daily as sole therapy given his limited life expectancy and bleeding risk

choices.label: E
choices.text: No anticoagulation given the high bleeding risk; place an inferior vena cava filter instead

explanation.summary: In cancer-associated VTE, LMWH has traditionally been the preferred long-term anticoagulation over warfarin due to superior efficacy in reducing recurrent VTE in cancer patients. LMWH monotherapy (without transition to an oral agent) is recommended for the duration of active malignancy.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CLOT trial and subsequent studies demonstrated that LMWH monotherapy is superior to warfarin for reducing recurrent VTE in cancer patients. LMWH is preferred because cancer patients have erratic INR responses to warfarin (due to drug interactions, malnutrition, hepatic dysfunction), and LMWH can be easily held and restarted around procedures or bleeding episodes. While DOACs (edoxaban, rivaroxaban) are now acceptable alternatives for some cancer-associated VTE, they carry higher GI bleeding risk in patients with GI/GU malignancies, making LMWH the safer choice in this patient with hematuria from a bladder mass.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Warfarin is inferior to LMWH for cancer-associated VTE. Cancer patients on warfarin have higher rates of recurrent VTE and more difficulty maintaining a stable therapeutic INR due to drug-drug interactions, poor oral intake, and hepatic dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While DOACs like rivaroxaban are now options for some cancer-associated VTE, they are associated with increased mucosal bleeding risk, particularly in patients with GI or genitourinary malignancies. Given this patient's active hematuria from a bladder mass, rivaroxaban would carry an unacceptably high bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin has no role as primary therapy for acute DVT. It is insufficient to prevent thrombus propagation or recurrent VTE and does not achieve the anticoagulant effect necessary to treat venous thromboembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IVC filter placement without anticoagulation is reserved for patients with absolute contraindications to all forms of anticoagulation. While this patient has relative bleeding risks, his thrombocytopenia is mild and does not preclude LMWH therapy with appropriate dose adjustment and monitoring.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician for evaluation of left lower extremity swelling. She reports that the swelling has been gradually worsening over 2 weeks. She has no recent travel, surgery, or immobilization. She denies chest pain, dyspnea, or hemoptysis. Her medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Physical examination reveals unilateral left leg edema. The physician calculates a Wells score. Which of the following clinical features is included in the Wells scoring system for DVT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of a positive Homan sign elicited by passive dorsiflexion of the ankle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated D-dimer level above 500 ng/mL measured by quantitative ELISA assay

choices.label: C
choices.text: Active malignancy defined as treatment within 6 months or currently receiving palliative care

choices.label: D
choices.text: History of prior myocardial infarction or peripheral arterial disease within the past year

choices.label: E
choices.text: Body mass index greater than 30 kg/m² as a surrogate marker for venous stasis

explanation.summary: The Wells score for DVT is a clinical prediction tool that includes: active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative), paralysis/paresis/recent immobilization of lower extremity, recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks, localized tenderness along deep venous system, entire leg swollen, calf swelling >3 cm vs asymptomatic side, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, previously documented DVT, and an alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT (negative points).
explanation.whyCorrect: Active malignancy (defined as treatment ongoing or within 6 months, or palliative) is one of the original Wells criteria for DVT and contributes +1 point to the score. Cancer is included because of the strong association between malignancy and venous thromboembolism due to the prothrombotic state induced by tumor-derived procoagulants, chemotherapy, and immobility.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Homan sign (calf pain on ankle dorsiflexion) is not part of the Wells score. It is a physical examination finding with very poor sensitivity and specificity for DVT and is no longer recommended as a reliable clinical tool.
whyWrongByChoice.B: D-dimer is a laboratory test used in conjunction with the Wells score but is not a component of the Wells scoring system itself. D-dimer is used to rule out DVT in patients with a low pretest probability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: History of myocardial infarction or peripheral arterial disease is not a Wells criterion. The Wells score focuses on venous thromboembolic risk factors, not arterial disease history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Obesity (BMI >30) is a recognized risk factor for DVT but is not included in the Wells scoring criteria. The Wells score emphasizes clinical signs and specific risk factors directly associated with acute DVT.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with her third episode of DVT in 5 years. She has also had two first-trimester pregnancy losses. Laboratory evaluation reveals a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) that does not correct on mixing study, a positive dilute Russell viper venom time, and persistently positive anticardiolipin antibodies on repeat testing 14 weeks apart. Which of the following best explains the paradox of a prolonged aPTT in a patient with a prothrombotic condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lupus anticoagulant antibody inhibits thrombomodulin-mediated activation of protein C in vivo

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lupus anticoagulant antibody binds phospholipid surfaces used in the aPTT assay, prolonging clotting time in vitro

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anticardiolipin antibodies directly inhibit factor XII activation in the intrinsic coagulation pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient has a concurrent factor VIII deficiency that is masked by the prothrombotic state

choices.label: E
choices.text: The prolonged aPTT reflects consumption of clotting factors from ongoing disseminated intravascular coagulation

explanation.summary: The lupus anticoagulant (LA) is an antiphospholipid antibody that binds to phospholipid-protein complexes. In vitro, it interferes with phospholipid-dependent coagulation assays (like the aPTT), prolonging clotting times. In vivo, however, LA promotes thrombosis through mechanisms including endothelial cell activation, complement activation, and interference with natural anticoagulant pathways.
explanation.whyCorrect: The aPTT assay depends on phospholipid reagents to activate the coagulation cascade in vitro. The lupus anticoagulant binds to these phospholipid surfaces (specifically beta-2 glycoprotein I bound to phospholipids), competing with coagulation factors for phospholipid binding sites and thereby prolonging the clotting time. This is a laboratory artifact that does not reflect in vivo anticoagulation. In vivo, antiphospholipid antibodies are prothrombotic through activation of endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and complement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While antiphospholipid antibodies can interfere with the protein C pathway in vivo, this does not explain the prolonged aPTT. The aPTT prolongation is specifically due to in vitro interference with phospholipid-dependent assay reagents, not inhibition of thrombomodulin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticardiolipin antibodies do not directly inhibit factor XII. The mechanism of aPTT prolongation is phospholipid interference by the lupus anticoagulant, not specific factor inhibition. Additionally, the mixing study did not correct, which is consistent with an inhibitor (lupus anticoagulant), not a factor deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no evidence of factor VIII deficiency. The non-correcting mixing study indicates the presence of an inhibitor, not a factor deficiency. A factor deficiency would correct on mixing with normal plasma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DIC would present with consumption of multiple coagulation factors, elevated fibrin degradation products, low fibrinogen, schistocytes on peripheral smear, and both bleeding and thrombotic manifestations. The clinical presentation is consistent with antiphospholipid syndrome, not DIC.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old previously healthy man presents to the emergency department with acute-onset left leg pain and massive swelling. He reports no recent travel, surgery, or immobilization. Physical examination reveals a markedly swollen, tense left leg that is white in color with diminished but palpable pedal pulses. The limb is cool to the touch. Duplex ultrasonography confirms extensive thrombosis of the entire left iliofemoral venous system. Which of the following is the most accurate description of this clinical entity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Phlegmasia alba dolens, characterized by massive iliofemoral DVT with limb pallor from arteriolar vasospasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute compartment syndrome of the left lower extremity requiring emergent fasciotomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: May-Thurner syndrome presenting as acute arterial occlusion with secondary venous congestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paget-Schroetter syndrome involving effort-related thrombosis of the axillosubclavian venous system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Budd-Chiari syndrome with secondary lower extremity venous hypertension and limb edema

explanation.summary: Phlegmasia alba dolens ('painful white leg') is a severe form of iliofemoral DVT presenting with massive leg swelling, pain, and limb pallor. The white appearance results from arteriolar vasospasm in response to the massive venous outflow obstruction. It can progress to phlegmasia cerulea dolens if obstruction worsens.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phlegmasia alba dolens is characterized by the triad of pain, edema, and blanching (white color) of the limb due to extensive iliofemoral DVT. The pallor results from reflex arteriolar vasospasm triggered by massive venous obstruction and elevated interstitial pressure. While pedal pulses are diminished, they remain palpable, distinguishing this from phlegmasia cerulea dolens (where arterial compromise is more severe, and the limb is cyanotic). This condition is a surgical emergency requiring aggressive anticoagulation and consideration of catheter-directed thrombolysis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute compartment syndrome presents with pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch, paresthesia, and tense compartments, typically after trauma or reperfusion injury. While elevated compartment pressures can develop in phlegmasia, the primary diagnosis is the massive iliofemoral DVT itself causing phlegmasia alba dolens.
whyWrongByChoice.C: May-Thurner syndrome (iliac vein compression syndrome) involves compression of the left common iliac vein by the overlying right common iliac artery, predisposing to left iliofemoral DVT. While it may be an underlying anatomic predisposition in this patient, it is not the clinical entity described. May-Thurner is an anatomic variant, not an acute presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Paget-Schroetter syndrome refers to effort-related thrombosis of the axillosubclavian vein (upper extremity DVT), typically in young athletes with thoracic outlet compression. This patient has lower extremity DVT, not upper extremity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Budd-Chiari syndrome is thrombosis of the hepatic veins causing hepatic congestion, ascites, and hepatomegaly. While it may cause lower extremity edema from caval compression, it does not present as acute unilateral limb swelling with pallor.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman undergoes elective total knee arthroplasty. The surgical team plans postoperative VTE prophylaxis. Her medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes. She has no history of VTE, bleeding disorders, or renal impairment. Which of the following prophylactic regimens is most appropriate to reduce the risk of postoperative DVT in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin 81 mg daily as sole pharmacologic prophylaxis combined with early ambulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Therapeutic-dose unfractionated heparin infusion targeting an aPTT of 60-80 seconds

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily beginning 12 hours preoperatively through discharge

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rivaroxaban 10 mg orally daily for a minimum of 10-14 days postoperatively

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intermittent pneumatic compression devices alone without any pharmacologic prophylaxis

explanation.summary: After major orthopedic surgery (total knee or hip arthroplasty), pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis is recommended for a minimum of 10-14 days. Approved regimens include LMWH, fondaparinux, low-dose warfarin, aspirin, or DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban). Rivaroxaban 10 mg daily is FDA-approved and guideline-recommended for this indication.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rivaroxaban 10 mg daily is FDA-approved for VTE prophylaxis after total knee and hip arthroplasty. The RECORD trials demonstrated superior efficacy of rivaroxaban compared to enoxaparin in reducing VTE after major orthopedic surgery with a comparable safety profile. It offers the convenience of oral administration, fixed dosing without monitoring, and a recommended duration of at least 10-14 days post-TKA (up to 35 days post-THA).

whyWrongByChoice.A: While the AAOS and some guidelines now include aspirin as an acceptable option for VTE prophylaxis after joint arthroplasty in standard-risk patients, it is generally considered less effective than anticoagulant-based regimens. A DOAC like rivaroxaban provides more robust prophylaxis with proven efficacy in large randomized trials.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Therapeutic-dose UFH (targeting aPTT 60-80 seconds) is used for treatment of established VTE, not prophylaxis. Prophylactic UFH dosing is 5,000 units subcutaneously every 8-12 hours. Therapeutic anticoagulation postoperatively would carry an unacceptably high bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While LMWH is an acceptable prophylactic option, the timing described is suboptimal. For major orthopedic surgery, LMWH is typically initiated either 12 hours before surgery or 12-24 hours after surgery, and should continue for the full recommended duration (10-14 days for TKA), not just through hospital discharge, which is often only 1-3 days.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mechanical prophylaxis alone (intermittent pneumatic compression) is insufficient for major orthopedic surgery, which carries one of the highest risks of postoperative VTE. Guidelines recommend combined pharmacologic and mechanical prophylaxis for optimal prevention.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. He was diagnosed with a left popliteal vein DVT 2 weeks ago but admits he has not been taking his prescribed anticoagulation medication. CT pulmonary angiography reveals a large saddle embolus at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery. His blood pressure is 82/54 mmHg, heart rate is 125 bpm, and oxygen saturation is 84% on room air. Bedside echocardiography shows right ventricular dilation with paradoxical septal motion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer intravenous unfractionated heparin bolus and infusion with close aPTT monitoring

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate systemic thrombolytic therapy with alteplase 100 mg intravenously over 2 hours

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place an inferior vena cava filter to prevent further embolization while initiating oral anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Start rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily and observe for hemodynamic improvement over 24 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform emergent surgical embolectomy only if the patient develops cardiac arrest

explanation.summary: This patient has a massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with hemodynamic instability (hypotension, tachycardia) and right ventricular dysfunction. Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase is the recommended treatment for massive PE to rapidly lyse the clot and restore pulmonary blood flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Massive PE is defined by sustained hemodynamic instability (systolic BP <90 mmHg). In this life-threatening scenario, systemic thrombolysis with alteplase (100 mg IV over 2 hours) is the first-line treatment as it rapidly dissolves the pulmonary artery thrombus, reduces pulmonary vascular resistance, and improves right ventricular function. Multiple trials have demonstrated reduced mortality with thrombolysis in massive PE. The benefits of thrombolysis clearly outweigh the bleeding risks in hemodynamically unstable patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While heparin is an essential component of PE management, anticoagulation alone is insufficient for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise. Heparin prevents thrombus propagation but does not lyse existing clot. This patient requires immediate clot dissolution to survive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IVC filter placement does not treat the existing massive PE and would not address the acute hemodynamic compromise. IVC filters prevent future embolization but are not a primary treatment for established massive PE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral anticoagulants such as rivaroxaban are appropriate for hemodynamically stable PE but are completely inadequate for massive PE with shock. The onset of action is too slow, and this patient requires immediate clot lysis, not gradual anticoagulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surgical embolectomy is an alternative to thrombolysis in massive PE, particularly when thrombolysis is contraindicated or has failed. However, waiting for cardiac arrest before performing embolectomy would be inappropriate. If thrombolysis is not contraindicated, it should be attempted first as it is less invasive and more rapidly available.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman who works as a hairdresser presents with aching, heaviness, and visible tortuous dilated veins on the medial aspect of her right leg that have been gradually worsening over the past 5 years. Symptoms are worse at the end of the work day and improve with leg elevation. She has had two vaginal deliveries. Physical examination reveals multiple dilated, tortuous subcutaneous veins along the distribution of the great saphenous vein. There is no edema, skin discoloration, or ulceration. Which of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiology of her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obstruction of the deep venous system by residual thrombus causing secondary superficial venous dilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary incompetence of the venous valves in the superficial venous system leading to retrograde venous flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arteriovenous fistula formation causing high-pressure arterial blood to enter the superficial venous system

choices.label: D
choices.text: Congenital absence of the deep venous system with compensatory superficial venous hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: External compression of the saphenofemoral junction by an enlarged inguinal lymph node

explanation.summary: Primary varicose veins result from intrinsic weakness of the venous wall and valvular incompetence in the superficial venous system (great saphenous vein and its tributaries). Valve failure allows retrograde blood flow (reflux), causing venous dilation, tortuosity, and symptoms of venous insufficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary varicose veins are caused by incompetent venous valves in the superficial venous system, most commonly at the saphenofemoral junction or within the great saphenous vein itself. Risk factors include female sex, multiparity, prolonged standing, and family history. When valves fail, gravitational forces cause retrograde flow during standing, leading to progressive venous distension, wall remodeling, and the characteristic tortuous, dilated superficial veins. This patient has multiple risk factors: female sex, two pregnancies, and prolonged standing occupation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Secondary varicose veins from deep venous obstruction (e.g., post-thrombotic syndrome) can occur but would typically present with a history of DVT, more severe symptoms, and signs of chronic venous insufficiency (edema, skin changes). This patient has no such history, making primary valvular incompetence more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Arteriovenous fistulae cause high-flow venous dilation and would present with a palpable thrill, bruit, and signs of high-output cardiac failure if significant. This is extremely uncommon and not consistent with the gradual bilateral pattern of primary varicose veins.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Congenital absence of the deep venous system (Klippel-Trenaunay syndrome) is a rare condition that presents in childhood with varicose veins, limb hypertrophy, and cutaneous capillary malformations. This adult-onset gradual presentation is consistent with primary varicose veins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: External compression of the SFJ by lymphadenopathy is rare and would typically present with unilateral sudden-onset symptoms and a palpable groin mass. It would not explain the gradual bilateral worsening seen in primary varicose veins.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old obese woman presents with a painful, firm, erythematous cord along the medial aspect of her left thigh. The area is warm and tender to palpation, and the overlying skin is erythematous. She has a history of varicose veins but no prior episodes of DVT. She is afebrile. Duplex ultrasonography shows thrombosis of the great saphenous vein extending to within 3 cm of the saphenofemoral junction, with no thrombus identified in the deep venous system. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Warm compresses, NSAIDs, and compression stockings with outpatient follow-up in 2 weeks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anticoagulation with a prophylactic dose of LMWH for 45 days given proximity to the saphenofemoral junction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emergent surgical ligation of the saphenofemoral junction to prevent thrombus propagation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systemic thrombolytic therapy with alteplase to dissolve the superficial venous thrombus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics to treat the presumed suppurative thrombophlebitis

explanation.summary: Superficial venous thrombosis (SVT) of the great saphenous vein extending close to the saphenofemoral junction (within 3-5 cm) carries a significant risk of propagation into the deep venous system, effectively becoming a DVT. Anticoagulation with prophylactic-dose LMWH (e.g., fondaparinux 2.5 mg daily) for 45 days is recommended.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CALISTO trial demonstrated that fondaparinux 2.5 mg daily for 45 days significantly reduced the risk of DVT extension, PE, and recurrent SVT in patients with acute symptomatic SVT of the lower extremity. Current guidelines recommend anticoagulation (prophylactic-dose LMWH or fondaparinux) for SVT that is extensive (>5 cm) or involves the proximal great saphenous vein near the saphenofemoral junction (within 3-5 cm), given the high risk of deep venous extension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While warm compresses, NSAIDs, and compression stockings are appropriate for short-segment, distal superficial thrombophlebitis, this patient's thrombus extends to within 3 cm of the saphenofemoral junction, placing her at high risk for DVT extension. Conservative management alone would be inadequate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Emergent surgical ligation of the SFJ was historically performed for SVT approaching the deep system but has largely been replaced by anticoagulation therapy, which is less invasive and equally effective. Surgery is now reserved for cases refractory to medical therapy or with documented extension into the deep system despite anticoagulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic thrombolysis is reserved for life-threatening conditions such as massive PE or acute limb-threatening DVT. It is not indicated for superficial venous thrombosis, as the bleeding risk far outweighs any benefit.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This patient has aseptic superficial thrombophlebitis, not suppurative (septic) thrombophlebitis. Suppurative thrombophlebitis typically occurs in the setting of intravenous catheters, presents with high fever, purulent drainage, and systemic sepsis, and requires IV antibiotics and surgical excision of the affected vein.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with longstanding bilateral varicose veins presents for evaluation. He has developed brownish discoloration of the skin around both medial malleoli, with areas of induration and thickened, fibrotic skin. He also notes persistent mild edema of both ankles that improves overnight. He has no history of DVT. Duplex ultrasonography confirms reflux in the great saphenous vein bilaterally with no deep venous obstruction. According to the CEAP classification, which clinical class best describes this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: C2: varicose veins greater than 3 mm in diameter without associated skin changes

choices.label: B
choices.text: C3: varicose veins with associated lower extremity edema only

choices.label: C
choices.text: C4a: varicose veins with pigmentation changes and eczematous dermatitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: C4b: varicose veins with lipodermatosclerosis and/or atrophie blanche

choices.label: E
choices.text: C6: varicose veins with active venous ulceration requiring wound care

explanation.summary: The CEAP classification is the standard system for categorizing chronic venous disease. This patient has skin induration (lipodermatosclerosis), fibrotic changes, pigmentation, and edema, which corresponds to C4b. C4 is divided into C4a (pigmentation, eczema) and C4b (lipodermatosclerosis, atrophie blanche), with C4b representing more advanced skin damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CEAP classification stratifies chronic venous disease: C0 (no visible signs), C1 (telangiectasias/reticular veins), C2 (varicose veins), C3 (edema), C4a (pigmentation/eczema), C4b (lipodermatosclerosis/atrophie blanche), C5 (healed ulcer), C6 (active ulcer). This patient has induration and fibrotic skin changes (lipodermatosclerosis) in addition to pigmentation and edema, placing him in C4b, which represents significant chronic venous skin damage with a high risk of ulcer development.

whyWrongByChoice.A: C2 describes varicose veins without any associated skin changes or edema. This patient has significant skin changes (pigmentation, induration, fibrosis) that place him well beyond C2.
whyWrongByChoice.B: C3 indicates varicose veins with edema but without skin changes. This patient has both edema and skin changes (pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis), making C3 an underclassification.
whyWrongByChoice.C: C4a describes pigmentation and/or eczematous dermatitis without the more advanced changes of lipodermatosclerosis. This patient's induration and fibrotic skin changes indicate lipodermatosclerosis, which upgrades the classification to C4b.
whyWrongByChoice.E: C6 describes active venous ulceration, which is not present in this patient. He has skin changes that predispose to ulceration but has not yet developed an ulcer.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with symptomatic great saphenous vein incompetence and varicose veins (CEAP C2s) is being evaluated for definitive treatment. She has failed conservative management with compression stockings and lifestyle modifications over the past year. Duplex ultrasonography confirms reflux greater than 0.5 seconds in the great saphenous vein from the saphenofemoral junction to the knee. The deep venous system is patent and competent. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continued use of graduated compression stockings at 30-40 mmHg with elevation and exercise

choices.label: B
choices.text: Open surgical high ligation and stripping of the incompetent great saphenous vein

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endovenous laser ablation or radiofrequency ablation of the incompetent great saphenous vein

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sclerotherapy injection of the great saphenous vein trunk with hypertonic saline

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iliac vein stenting to improve venous outflow and reduce superficial venous reflux

explanation.summary: Endovenous thermal ablation (laser or radiofrequency) is the first-line definitive treatment for symptomatic great saphenous vein incompetence that has failed conservative management. It has replaced open surgical stripping as the preferred approach due to equivalent efficacy with less pain, faster recovery, and fewer complications.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endovenous laser ablation (EVLA) and radiofrequency ablation (RFA) are the current standard of care for treatment of symptomatic GSV incompetence. Multiple randomized trials and meta-analyses have demonstrated that thermal ablation techniques achieve >90% occlusion rates at 5 years, with less postoperative pain, faster return to normal activities, and lower rates of wound complications compared to surgical stripping. They are performed as outpatient procedures under local anesthesia with tumescent infiltration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The patient has already failed conservative management with compression stockings over the past year. Continuing the same unsuccessful therapy is not appropriate when definitive treatment options are available and indicated for symptomatic C2 disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While high ligation and stripping is an effective treatment with long historical track record, it has been largely supplanted by endovenous thermal ablation as the first-line intervention. Surgical stripping requires general or regional anesthesia, has a higher rate of wound complications, causes more postoperative pain, and requires a longer recovery period compared to endovenous techniques.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sclerotherapy of the GSV trunk with hypertonic saline is not standard practice. Foam sclerotherapy with polidocanol or sodium tetradecyl sulfate can be used for truncal veins but has higher recurrence rates than thermal ablation and is generally reserved for patients who are not candidates for thermal ablation or as an adjunct for residual varicosities.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iliac vein stenting is indicated for iliac vein obstruction (e.g., May-Thurner syndrome or post-thrombotic iliac stenosis), not for primary superficial venous insufficiency with a patent and competent deep system. This patient has no evidence of deep venous obstruction.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man who is an avid weightlifter presents with a 2-day history of a painful, red, swollen area on his left forearm along the course of a visible vein. He had an intravenous line placed in that arm during a dental procedure 5 days ago. On examination, there is a 6-cm segment of erythema and induration along a superficial vein on the volar forearm with a palpable cord. He is afebrile with normal vital signs. There is no purulent drainage, lymphangitis, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Axillosubclavian vein effort thrombosis related to repetitive upper extremity exertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Superficial thrombophlebitis of a peripheral vein secondary to recent catheter insertion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cellulitis of the left forearm requiring systemic antibiotic therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lymphangitis with retrograde spread from a distal site of cutaneous inoculation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Septic thrombophlebitis requiring intravenous antibiotics and surgical vein excision

explanation.summary: This patient has superficial thrombophlebitis of a peripheral upper extremity vein, a common complication of intravenous catheterization. It presents with localized pain, erythema, induration, and a palpable cord along the affected vein. It is typically self-limited and managed conservatively.
explanation.whyCorrect: Catheter-related superficial thrombophlebitis is the most common complication of peripheral IV access. It results from endothelial injury caused by the catheter, exacerbated by irritating infusates. The classic findings are a tender, erythematous, indurated cord along a superficial vein at or near a previous IV site. The patient is afebrile without systemic signs of infection, making this a sterile (aseptic) inflammatory thrombophlebitis. Treatment includes catheter removal (already done), warm compresses, NSAIDs, and monitoring.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Paget-Schroetter syndrome (effort thrombosis) involves the axillosubclavian vein and presents with acute arm swelling, heaviness, and cyanosis of the entire upper extremity. This patient has a localized superficial process on the forearm without arm swelling or deep vein involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cellulitis causes diffuse erythema, warmth, and swelling of the skin and subcutaneous tissue without a palpable cord along a venous tract. The linear cord-like induration along a visible vein is pathognomonic for thrombophlebitis, not cellulitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymphangitis presents with erythematous linear streaking extending proximally from a site of infection, often with proximal lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms. This patient has no lymphangitis streaks, no lymphadenopathy, and the process follows a venous distribution, not a lymphatic one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Septic (suppurative) thrombophlebitis is characterized by fever, purulent drainage from the catheter site, bacteremia, and systemic toxicity. This patient is afebrile with no purulent drainage or systemic signs, making aseptic thrombophlebitis much more likely.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with chronic bilateral varicose veins undergoes duplex ultrasonography as part of her preoperative evaluation for endovenous ablation. The study reveals incompetence of the great saphenous vein with reflux duration of 1.2 seconds, incompetent perforating veins connecting the superficial and deep systems in the medial calf, and a competent deep venous system. Which of the following best describes the normal direction of blood flow in the perforating veins and the pathologic consequence of their incompetence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal flow is from deep to superficial; incompetence causes excessive blood to be diverted from the deep system to the skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal flow is from superficial to deep; incompetence allows high-pressure deep venous blood to reflux into the superficial system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal flow is bidirectional depending on the cardiac cycle; incompetence causes unidirectional flow toward the skin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal flow is from superficial to deep; incompetence causes blood to pool in the deep venous system creating deep venous hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perforating veins have no valves and flow is determined solely by the pressure gradient between compartments

explanation.summary: Perforating veins connect the superficial and deep venous systems and normally contain one-way valves that direct blood flow from the superficial system into the deep system (inward flow). When these valves become incompetent, high-pressure blood from the deep system refluxes outward into the superficial veins and subcutaneous tissues, contributing to venous hypertension and skin changes.
explanation.whyCorrect: In normal physiology, the calf muscle pump generates high pressures in the deep venous system during ambulation. Perforating vein valves ensure unidirectional flow from the superficial to the deep system during muscle relaxation and prevent reflux of high-pressure blood outward during muscle contraction. When perforating vein valves become incompetent, each calf muscle contraction drives high-pressure deep venous blood outward into the superficial and subcutaneous tissues, causing ambulatory venous hypertension, varicose vein worsening, and skin damage (pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis, ulceration).

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the normal direction of flow. Normal perforating vein flow is inward (superficial to deep), not outward (deep to superficial). Outward flow represents the pathologic state of perforator incompetence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Normal perforating vein flow is not bidirectional. Competent perforator valves ensure unidirectional flow from superficial to deep. Bidirectional flow would indicate incompetence.
whyWrongByChoice.D: When perforators become incompetent, blood refluxes outward into the superficial system, causing superficial venous hypertension and skin damage—not pooling in the deep system. The deep system already has high pressure from the calf muscle pump.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Perforating veins do contain valves (typically 1-2 valves per perforator). These valves are essential for maintaining unidirectional flow and preventing transmission of deep venous pressures to the superficial system during ambulation.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with a 20-year history of varicose veins presents with a shallow, irregularly bordered ulcer measuring 3 x 4 cm located just superior to the medial malleolus of her left leg. The wound base has fibrinous exudate with surrounding brownish hyperpigmentation and induration. Her ankle-brachial index is 0.95. She has palpable pedal pulses bilaterally. Which of the following characteristics most reliably distinguishes this venous ulcer from an arterial ulcer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of severe pain at rest that is relieved by placing the leg in a dependent position

choices.label: B
choices.text: Location above the medial malleolus with irregular borders and surrounding lipodermatosclerosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Deep punched-out appearance with exposed tendon or bone at the wound base

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid onset over days to weeks in the absence of any prior venous disease history

choices.label: E
choices.text: Association with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive antinuclear antibody titers

explanation.summary: Venous ulcers characteristically occur in the gaiter area (medial malleolar region), have irregular borders, a shallow base with granulation tissue or fibrinous exudate, and are surrounded by signs of chronic venous insufficiency (pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis, edema). Arterial ulcers are typically located distally on toes or pressure points, have punched-out borders, and are associated with absent pulses and low ABI.
explanation.whyCorrect: The medial malleolar location, irregular borders, shallow wound bed, and surrounding lipodermatosclerosis with hemosiderin pigmentation are the hallmark features of a venous stasis ulcer. The normal ABI (0.95) and palpable pedal pulses confirm adequate arterial inflow, further supporting a venous etiology. This combination of location and surrounding skin changes is the most reliable way to distinguish venous from arterial ulceration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rest pain relieved by dependency is a characteristic of arterial insufficiency, not venous disease. Venous ulcers tend to be less painful than arterial ulcers, and symptoms are relieved by leg elevation, not dependency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Deep punched-out ulcers with exposed deep structures are characteristic of arterial ulcers, not venous ulcers. Venous ulcers are typically shallow with a granulating or fibrinous base.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Venous ulcers typically develop insidiously in patients with longstanding chronic venous insufficiency. Rapid onset would be more suggestive of an acute process such as trauma, infection, or pyoderma gangrenosum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated ESR and positive ANA suggest an autoimmune or inflammatory etiology (e.g., vasculitis, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus), not chronic venous insufficiency. Venous ulcers are not typically associated with autoimmune serologies.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman undergoes endovenous radiofrequency ablation of the great saphenous vein for symptomatic varicose veins. One week later, she presents with a sensation of pulling along the treated thigh, mild ecchymosis, and a firm palpable cord beneath the skin. She is afebrile and has no leg swelling, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Duplex ultrasonography shows a closed great saphenous vein with no evidence of thrombus extension into the common femoral vein. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassurance that the findings are expected postprocedural changes and continued use of compression stockings

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urgent surgical exploration and excision of the thrombosed great saphenous vein segment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of therapeutic anticoagulation with LMWH for at least 3 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected postprocedural septic thrombophlebitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Repeat radiofrequency ablation of the treated segment to ensure complete venous closure

explanation.summary: After endovenous thermal ablation, patients commonly experience a pulling or tightness sensation along the treated vein, ecchymosis, and a palpable cord from the intentionally thrombosed and fibrosed vein. These are expected postprocedural findings. Management involves reassurance, continued compression stockings, NSAIDs for discomfort, and routine follow-up.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endovenous thermal ablation works by intentionally causing thermal injury to the vein wall, leading to thrombotic occlusion and subsequent fibrosis. The expected postprocedural course includes mild-to-moderate pain, ecchymosis, induration, and a palpable cord along the treated vein, which typically resolves over weeks to months as the vein fibroses and is reabsorbed. The ultrasound confirms successful closure without deep vein extension, which is the main complication to exclude. No additional treatment beyond continued compression and symptomatic care is needed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Surgical excision is not indicated for expected postprocedural changes. The thrombosed vein segment is the intended result of the ablation procedure and will undergo natural fibrosis and resorption.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Therapeutic anticoagulation would be indicated if thrombus extended into the deep venous system (endovenous heat-induced thrombosis, or EHIT class 3-4). The ultrasound shows no deep vein involvement, so therapeutic anticoagulation is not warranted.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Septic thrombophlebitis presents with fever, systemic toxicity, and purulent drainage. This patient is afebrile with no signs of infection. The findings are consistent with expected postprocedural inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Repeat ablation is not necessary. The ultrasound confirms successful venous closure, which is the desired outcome. The palpable cord represents the successfully treated vein.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with longstanding varicose veins presents for evaluation. She describes a recent episode in which she bumped her leg against a table and experienced brisk, dark-red bleeding from a varicosity on her shin that was difficult to stop. She eventually controlled the bleeding by lying down and applying pressure with her leg elevated. On examination, she has prominent varicose veins with several visible superficial venous blebs on the anterior shin. Which of the following is the most important initial counseling point and long-term management recommendation for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bleeding is arterial in origin and requires urgent angiography with possible embolization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leg elevation with direct pressure controls acute hemorrhage; definitive treatment of the incompetent veins should be pursued

choices.label: C
choices.text: Apply a tourniquet proximal to the bleeding site and present to the emergency department for surgical ligation

choices.label: D
choices.text: This complication is benign and self-limited and requires no further evaluation or treatment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initiate long-term anticoagulation therapy to prevent recurrent variceal thrombosis and bleeding

explanation.summary: Hemorrhage from a varicose vein can be dramatic but is venous in nature (dark blood, low pressure). Acute management involves leg elevation above heart level and direct pressure, which reliably controls the bleeding. Long-term, definitive treatment of the underlying venous incompetence (ablation or surgery) is recommended to prevent recurrence.
explanation.whyCorrect: Varicose vein hemorrhage occurs when thin-walled, distended superficial veins are traumatized. The bleeding is venous (dark red, non-pulsatile) and is controlled by elevating the leg above heart level (which reduces venous pressure) and applying direct pressure. While the acute episode is manageable, recurrent hemorrhage is a recognized indication for definitive treatment of the underlying great saphenous vein incompetence through endovenous ablation, sclerotherapy, or surgical intervention. Patients should be counseled on first-aid measures and referred for definitive treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The dark-red, non-pulsatile nature of the bleeding indicates a venous source, not arterial. Arterial bleeding would be bright red and pulsatile. Angiography and embolization are not indicated for varicose vein hemorrhage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A tourniquet is not recommended for venous bleeding from a varicosity. Simple elevation and direct pressure are effective and safe. Tourniquet application could cause ischemic injury and is disproportionate to the clinical scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While each individual episode can be controlled with first aid, varicose vein hemorrhage tends to recur and can occasionally be significant, especially in elderly patients on anticoagulants. Dismissing this without planning definitive treatment would be inappropriate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation would worsen bleeding from varicose veins, not prevent it. The bleeding is a mechanical complication of superficial venous hypertension and thin-walled veins, not a thrombotic event. Treatment addresses the underlying venous incompetence, not the coagulation system.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman of Scandinavian descent presents with a 3-week history of new-onset right-sided headache, jaw claudication, and tenderness over the right temporal region. She also reports malaise, low-grade fevers, and a 5-kg weight loss over the past 2 months. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 105 mm/h and a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 8.2 mg/dL. A temporal artery biopsy is performed. Which of the following histopathologic findings is most characteristic of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall with neutrophilic infiltration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation of the media with multinucleated giant cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Onion-skin concentric intimal thickening with fibrin deposition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transmural necrotizing inflammation with microaneurysm formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intimal proliferation with organized thrombus and recanalization

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis (GCA, temporal arteritis) is a large-vessel vasculitis characterized by granulomatous inflammation of the media with fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina and multinucleated giant cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: GCA classically shows granulomatous inflammation centered on the internal elastic lamina and media of medium-to-large arteries. The infiltrate consists of CD4+ T lymphocytes, macrophages, and multinucleated giant cells. Fragmentation and destruction of the internal elastic lamina is a hallmark feature. The temporal artery is most commonly affected, and biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibrinoid necrosis with neutrophilic infiltration is characteristic of small-vessel vasculitides such as microscopic polyangiitis or ANCA-associated vasculitis, not the granulomatous pattern seen in GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Onion-skin concentric intimal thickening is seen in malignant hypertension (hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis) and in chronic transplant vasculopathy, not in giant cell arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transmural necrotizing inflammation with microaneurysm formation is characteristic of polyarteritis nodosa, a medium-vessel vasculitis, not the granulomatous pattern of GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intimal proliferation with organized thrombus describes a chronic vascular occlusive process or thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease), not the active granulomatous inflammation of GCA.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden, painless loss of vision in his right eye. He has a 4-week history of bilateral temporal headaches, scalp tenderness when combing his hair, and pain in his jaw while chewing. Fundoscopic examination reveals a pale, swollen optic disc with splinter hemorrhages. His ESR is 110 mm/h. High-dose corticosteroids are initiated immediately. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this patient's visual loss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery from carotid plaque

choices.label: B
choices.text: Demyelination of the optic nerve due to autoimmune inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arteritic ischemia of the posterior ciliary arteries supplying the optic nerve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased intraocular pressure from obstruction of aqueous humor outflow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Venous congestion from thrombosis of the central retinal vein

explanation.summary: Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) is the most feared complication of GCA, caused by vasculitis-induced occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries that supply the optic nerve head.
explanation.whyCorrect: In GCA, the inflammatory process affects the posterior ciliary arteries (branches of the ophthalmic artery), causing luminal narrowing and thrombosis. This leads to ischemia of the optic nerve head (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy), presenting as sudden, painless monocular vision loss. The pale, swollen optic disc is classic for arteritic AION. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids are critical to prevent contralateral eye involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Embolic central retinal artery occlusion from carotid atherosclerosis causes sudden monocular vision loss but produces a cherry-red spot on fundoscopy, not a pale swollen disc. The clinical context of temporal headache, jaw claudication, and elevated ESR points to GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Optic neuritis from demyelination (as in multiple sclerosis) typically affects younger patients, causes painful eye movements, and would not present with the systemic symptoms and markedly elevated ESR seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes increased intraocular pressure with a painful red eye, halos around lights, and a mid-dilated pupil, which is not consistent with this painless presentation with systemic inflammatory features.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Central retinal vein occlusion presents with diffuse retinal hemorrhages (blood and thunder fundus), cotton-wool spots, and disc edema, but the clinical scenario with jaw claudication and markedly elevated ESR strongly suggests GCA-related AION.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old Asian woman presents with a 6-month history of fatigue, lightheadedness, and pain in both arms that worsens with activity. Physical examination reveals absent radial pulses bilaterally, a blood pressure discrepancy of 30 mmHg between the arms, and bruits heard over both subclavian arteries. An arteriogram demonstrates irregular narrowing and occlusion of the aortic arch branches. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Giant cell arteritis with aortic arch involvement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibromuscular dysplasia of the subclavian arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atherosclerotic disease of the aortic arch vessels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Takayasu arteritis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Polyarteritis nodosa with large-vessel extension

explanation.summary: Takayasu arteritis is a large-vessel vasculitis that primarily affects the aorta and its major branches in young women, particularly those of Asian descent, leading to stenosis, occlusion, and absent pulses (pulseless disease).
explanation.whyCorrect: Takayasu arteritis typically affects women under age 40, with a predilection for Asian populations. It causes granulomatous inflammation of the aorta and its major branches, leading to vessel wall thickening, stenosis, and occlusion. The absent pulses, blood pressure discrepancy, and arm claudication are classic features that earned it the name 'pulseless disease.' Arteriography shows irregular vessel wall thickening and narrowing.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Giant cell arteritis can involve the aorta and its branches but typically presents in patients over age 50, often with temporal headaches and jaw claudication. The young age and Asian ethnicity strongly favor Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibromuscular dysplasia typically affects the renal and internal carotid arteries, usually in young-to-middle-aged women, and shows a characteristic 'string of beads' appearance on angiography rather than the irregular narrowing seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atherosclerotic disease of the aortic arch vessels would be unusual in a 25-year-old woman without significant cardiovascular risk factors and does not explain the systemic inflammatory symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis that does not typically affect the aorta or its major branches. It characteristically spares the pulmonary circulation and involves visceral arteries with microaneurysm formation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with Takayasu arteritis has been on high-dose prednisone for 3 months. Her inflammatory markers have normalized, but she develops cushingoid facies and hyperglycemia. Her physician decides to add a steroid-sparing agent. Which of the following medications is most commonly used as first-line steroid-sparing therapy in Takayasu arteritis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methotrexate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rituximab

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infliximab

choices.label: E
choices.text: Colchicine

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is the most commonly used first-line steroid-sparing immunosuppressive agent in Takayasu arteritis, allowing for glucocorticoid dose reduction while maintaining disease control.
explanation.whyCorrect: For patients with Takayasu arteritis who cannot tolerate prolonged high-dose glucocorticoids or who have relapsing disease, methotrexate is the most commonly used first-line steroid-sparing agent. It allows for tapering of corticosteroids while maintaining suppression of vessel wall inflammation. Azathioprine and mycophenolate mofetil are also used as alternatives.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide is reserved for severe, refractory cases of vasculitis due to its significant toxicity profile including hemorrhagic cystitis, bone marrow suppression, and increased malignancy risk. It is not a first-line steroid-sparing agent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rituximab (anti-CD20) has shown some promise in refractory Takayasu arteritis but is not established as first-line steroid-sparing therapy. It is more commonly first-line in ANCA-associated vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infliximab and other TNF-alpha inhibitors have been used in refractory Takayasu arteritis, but they are not considered first-line steroid-sparing agents and are typically reserved for cases failing conventional immunosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Colchicine is used in conditions like gout, familial Mediterranean fever, and pericarditis. It does not have a significant role in the management of large-vessel vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman is being evaluated for new-onset bilateral shoulder stiffness, hip girdle pain, and morning stiffness lasting more than one hour. She also reports fatigue and a 3-kg weight loss. Her ESR is 85 mm/h and CRP is 6.4 mg/dL. There are no visual symptoms or temporal headache. Her physician is concerned about an associated vasculitis. Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Polyarteritis nodosa

choices.label: B
choices.text: Takayasu arteritis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Giant cell arteritis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

explanation.summary: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is strongly associated with giant cell arteritis, with approximately 40-60% of GCA patients having PMR symptoms, and 15-20% of PMR patients developing GCA.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient presents with classic polymyalgia rheumatica (bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness, morning stiffness >1 hour, markedly elevated ESR). PMR is strongly associated with giant cell arteritis (GCA), sharing the same demographic (elderly, Northern European descent) and likely representing a spectrum of the same disease. Up to 20% of PMR patients develop GCA, and clinicians must remain vigilant for GCA symptoms (headache, jaw claudication, visual changes) in PMR patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis associated with hepatitis B and does not have a known association with polymyalgia rheumatica. It typically presents with renal, gastrointestinal, and neurologic involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Takayasu arteritis is a large-vessel vasculitis affecting young women. Although it shares the large-vessel category with GCA, it is not associated with PMR and affects a completely different demographic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener) is an ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis that affects the upper and lower respiratory tract and kidneys. It is not associated with PMR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Microscopic polyangiitis is an ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis causing rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage. It has no association with PMR.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with suspected giant cell arteritis undergoes a unilateral temporal artery biopsy. The pathology report shows no evidence of vasculitis. His ESR is 98 mm/h, he has jaw claudication, and a new-onset temporal headache. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discontinue corticosteroids since the biopsy is negative

choices.label: B
choices.text: Start methotrexate as primary therapy without corticosteroids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Order an MRI of the brain to evaluate for stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform a contralateral temporal artery biopsy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reassure the patient that vasculitis has been excluded
