explanation.summary: GCA causes segmental (skip) lesions in the temporal artery, so a negative unilateral biopsy does not exclude the diagnosis. A contralateral biopsy increases diagnostic sensitivity and should be performed when clinical suspicion remains high.
explanation.whyCorrect: Giant cell arteritis produces segmental inflammation with skip lesions, meaning portions of the affected artery may appear histologically normal. A single negative biopsy has a false-negative rate of approximately 10-15%. When clinical suspicion remains high (elevated ESR, jaw claudication, headache), a contralateral temporal artery biopsy is recommended to increase diagnostic sensitivity to approximately 95%. Corticosteroids should be continued during this evaluation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Discontinuing corticosteroids based solely on a negative unilateral biopsy would be dangerous given the high clinical suspicion. Skip lesions are common, and the risk of irreversible vision loss from untreated GCA mandates continued treatment until the diagnosis is adequately excluded.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent in GCA but is not appropriate as primary monotherapy. High-dose corticosteroids remain the first-line treatment for GCA, particularly when vision-threatening symptoms are present.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While GCA can rarely cause stroke, the immediate concern is establishing the diagnosis and preventing vision loss. An MRI of the brain does not address the diagnostic uncertainty regarding GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A single negative biopsy does not exclude GCA due to the segmental nature of the disease. Reassurance would be premature and could lead to discontinuation of treatment with risk of catastrophic visual complications.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old Japanese woman with a 2-year history of Takayasu arteritis develops recurrent episodes of exertional chest pain and syncope. Echocardiography reveals severe aortic regurgitation with a dilated aortic root. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this patient's valvular disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rheumatic inflammation causing commissural fusion of the aortic valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infective endocarditis causing valve perforation and vegetations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration of the aortic valve leaflets

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calcific degeneration of a congenitally bicuspid aortic valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inflammation-induced aortic root dilation causing failure of leaflet coaptation

explanation.summary: Takayasu arteritis can cause aortic root dilation due to chronic inflammation of the aortic wall, leading to aortic regurgitation through failure of leaflet coaptation rather than primary valve disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Takayasu arteritis, granulomatous inflammation of the aorta can weaken the aortic wall, leading to aneurysmal dilation of the ascending aorta and aortic root. This dilation stretches the aortic annulus, preventing proper coaptation of the aortic valve leaflets and resulting in aortic regurgitation. This is a well-recognized complication occurring in up to 20-25% of patients with Takayasu arteritis. The valve leaflets themselves are structurally normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rheumatic heart disease causes commissural fusion and thickening of valve leaflets, typically affecting the mitral valve most commonly. It results from a prior group A streptococcal pharyngitis and is not related to Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Infective endocarditis can cause aortic regurgitation through valve destruction but presents with fever, positive blood cultures, and vegetations. There is no indication of infection in this patient's presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myxomatous degeneration is the hallmark of mitral valve prolapse, not aortic valve disease. It involves accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in the spongiosa layer and is unrelated to vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcific degeneration of a bicuspid aortic valve typically causes aortic stenosis rather than regurgitation and would not be related to Takayasu arteritis. This typically presents at age 40-60.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with recently diagnosed giant cell arteritis has been on prednisone 60 mg daily for 2 weeks. She presents with acute onset of left-sided weakness and dysarthria. CT angiography of the head and neck reveals stenosis of the left vertebral artery. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture with superimposed thrombosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardioembolic event from atrial fibrillation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypertensive lacunar infarction in the basal ganglia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasculitic inflammation causing intimal hyperplasia and luminal narrowing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Corticosteroid-induced cerebral venous sinus thrombosis

explanation.summary: GCA can affect the vertebral arteries and other cranial vessels, causing intimal hyperplasia with progressive luminal narrowing and thrombotic occlusion, leading to posterior circulation stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: GCA can involve the vertebral and other extracranial arteries. The inflammatory process causes intimal hyperplasia, medial destruction, and fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina, leading to progressive luminal narrowing. This can result in thrombotic occlusion and posterior circulation stroke. Stroke complicates GCA in 3-7% of cases, and vertebral artery involvement is a recognized mechanism. This can occur even after initiation of corticosteroids if inflammation has not yet fully resolved.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While atherosclerotic plaque rupture is the most common cause of stroke overall, the clinical context of newly diagnosed GCA with vertebral artery stenosis points to vasculitis-induced narrowing as the mechanism rather than atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardioembolic stroke from atrial fibrillation would not explain the focal vertebral artery stenosis seen on CT angiography. Cardioembolic events typically show abrupt vessel occlusion rather than segmental stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lacunar infarctions are small, deep infarcts caused by lipohyalinosis of penetrating arteries. They would not explain the large-vessel stenosis of the vertebral artery demonstrated on angiography.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Corticosteroids do not characteristically cause cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. Furthermore, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis presents with headache, papilledema, and sometimes seizures, and involves the venous system, not the arterial system.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with Takayasu arteritis undergoes CT angiography, which reveals a long segment of concentric wall thickening in the descending thoracic aorta with mural enhancement. The aortography classification system divides Takayasu arteritis into types based on anatomic distribution. This patient's findings are most consistent with which of the following Takayasu arteritis types?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type I — involvement limited to the aortic arch and its branches

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type IIa — ascending aorta, aortic arch, and arch branches

choices.label: C
choices.text: Type III — descending thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and/or renal arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Type IV — abdominal aorta and/or renal arteries only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Type V — combined features of types IIb and IV

explanation.summary: Takayasu arteritis is classified by anatomic involvement. Type III involves the descending thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and/or renal arteries, without involvement of the aortic arch or its branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Numano classification divides Takayasu arteritis into types based on the distribution of vascular involvement. Type III involves the descending thoracic aorta, abdominal aorta, and/or renal arteries. The CT angiographic finding of concentric wall thickening in the descending thoracic aorta with mural enhancement is consistent with active Type III disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type I involves only the aortic arch and its branches. This patient's involvement is in the descending thoracic aorta, which is distal to the arch and not consistent with Type I.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type IIa involves the ascending aorta, aortic arch, and arch branches. The descending thoracic aorta is not part of this classification, so this does not match the patient's findings.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type IV is limited to the abdominal aorta and/or renal arteries. The patient's involvement is in the descending thoracic aorta, which is above the abdominal aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Type V represents the most extensive form, combining features of Type IIb (ascending aorta, arch, arch branches, and descending thoracic aorta) and Type IV (abdominal aorta/renal arteries). The isolated descending thoracic aorta involvement does not suggest this diffuse pattern.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man with biopsy-proven giant cell arteritis has been in clinical remission on low-dose prednisone for 18 months. He presents with new exertional dyspnea and a widened mediastinum on chest radiograph. CT angiography demonstrates a 5.5-cm aneurysm of the thoracic aorta. Which of the following statements best characterizes the relationship between this patient's vasculitis and his aortic aneurysm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The aortic aneurysm is unrelated to GCA and represents coincidental atherosclerotic disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: GCA patients have a 17-fold increased risk of thoracic aortic aneurysm compared to the general population

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thoracic aortic aneurysm only occurs during the active inflammatory phase of GCA

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic aneurysm in GCA is exclusively caused by corticosteroid-induced connective tissue weakening

choices.label: E
choices.text: GCA-related aortic aneurysms only affect the abdominal aorta, not the thoracic segment

explanation.summary: Patients with GCA have a markedly increased risk (approximately 17-fold) of developing thoracic aortic aneurysm, which can present years after the initial diagnosis even during apparent remission.
explanation.whyCorrect: GCA is associated with a substantially increased risk of thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection. Studies have shown approximately a 17-fold increased risk of thoracic aortic aneurysm in GCA patients compared to the general population. This complication can present years or even decades after the initial diagnosis, even in patients who appear to be in remission, due to prior inflammatory damage weakening the aortic wall. Long-term surveillance with periodic imaging is recommended.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While atherosclerosis is a common cause of aortic aneurysm, the strong epidemiologic association between GCA and thoracic aortic aneurysm (17-fold increased risk) makes it highly likely that this patient's aneurysm is related to his vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thoracic aortic aneurysm in GCA can present years after the active inflammatory phase has resolved. The structural damage to the aortic wall from prior inflammation predisposes to aneurysm formation even during clinical remission.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While corticosteroids can affect connective tissue, the primary mechanism of aortic aneurysm in GCA is inflammatory destruction of the media (elastic fibers and smooth muscle), not corticosteroid effects. Aneurysms can develop in GCA patients regardless of treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GCA-related aortic aneurysms characteristically affect the thoracic aorta more than the abdominal aorta, which is the opposite pattern from atherosclerotic aneurysms. The thoracic segment is the predominant site of GCA-related aneurysmal disease.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman presents with new bilateral temporal headaches, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. Her ESR is 95 mm/h. While awaiting temporal artery biopsy, her physician starts treatment. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Low-dose aspirin alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (approximately 60 mg daily)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide 2 mg/kg/day

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tocilizumab 162 mg subcutaneously weekly without corticosteroids

explanation.summary: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, typically 40-60 mg daily) are the cornerstone of initial treatment for GCA and should be started immediately upon clinical suspicion, even before biopsy confirmation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The initial treatment of GCA is high-dose glucocorticoids, typically prednisone at 1 mg/kg/day (40-60 mg daily). Treatment should be initiated as soon as GCA is suspected, without waiting for biopsy results, because delay risks irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy results remain interpretable for up to 2-4 weeks after starting corticosteroids. If visual symptoms are present, intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy may be considered initially.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Low-dose aspirin may be used as adjunctive therapy to reduce ischemic complications in GCA, but it is insufficient as monotherapy. High-dose corticosteroids are the essential initial treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent in GCA but is not appropriate as initial monotherapy. It takes weeks to achieve therapeutic effect and does not provide the rapid immunosuppression needed to prevent visual loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cyclophosphamide is reserved for severe, refractory vasculitis and carries significant toxicity. It is not first-line treatment for GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor inhibitor) is approved as a steroid-sparing agent in GCA and may be used in combination with a corticosteroid taper. However, it is not recommended as monotherapy without initial corticosteroid use for newly diagnosed GCA.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old Indian woman with Takayasu arteritis presents with severe hypertension refractory to three antihypertensive medications. Physical examination reveals an abdominal bruit. Renal artery duplex ultrasound shows bilateral renal artery stenosis. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of her hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Primary aldosteronism from adrenal adenoma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pheochromocytoma with catecholamine excess

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coarctation of the aorta with upper extremity hypertension

choices.label: D
choices.text: Essential hypertension exacerbated by corticosteroid therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system due to renal hypoperfusion

explanation.summary: Renal artery stenosis in Takayasu arteritis causes renovascular hypertension through activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) due to decreased renal perfusion pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Takayasu arteritis commonly involves the renal arteries, causing stenosis that reduces renal perfusion pressure. The juxtaglomerular cells sense decreased afferent arteriolar pressure and secrete renin, activating the RAAS cascade: angiotensinogen is converted to angiotensin I, then to angiotensin II (via ACE), leading to vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated sodium retention. This renovascular hypertension is often severe and refractory to standard medical therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Primary aldosteronism from adrenal adenoma (Conn syndrome) causes hypertension through autonomous aldosterone production, but this would not explain the bilateral renal artery stenosis and abdominal bruit seen in the clinical context of Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pheochromocytoma causes paroxysmal hypertension with catecholamine excess, presenting with episodic headaches, palpitations, and diaphoresis. It does not cause renal artery stenosis or abdominal bruits.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coarctation of the aorta can occur in Takayasu arteritis, but it would present with upper extremity hypertension and lower extremity hypotension with a blood pressure gradient. The finding of bilateral renal artery stenosis specifically points to renovascular hypertension as the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While corticosteroids can contribute to hypertension, the presence of bilateral renal artery stenosis with an abdominal bruit in a patient with known Takayasu arteritis strongly suggests renovascular hypertension as the primary mechanism.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of fever, weight loss, myalgias, and abdominal pain. He has a history of injection drug use and was found to be hepatitis B surface antigen positive. Physical examination reveals tender subcutaneous nodules along the course of the radial artery, livedo reticularis on the lower extremities, and a left foot drop. Laboratory studies show elevated creatinine at 2.1 mg/dL and an active urinary sediment without red blood cell casts. Mesenteric angiography reveals multiple small aneurysms at arterial branch points. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Polyarteritis nodosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis

explanation.summary: Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) is a medium-vessel necrotizing vasculitis associated with hepatitis B infection, characterized by microaneurysms on angiography, mononeuritis multiplex, renal involvement without glomerulonephritis, and subcutaneous nodules.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN is a necrotizing vasculitis affecting medium-sized muscular arteries. The hepatitis B association, microaneurysms at arterial branch points on angiography, mononeuritis multiplex (foot drop), subcutaneous nodules, livedo reticularis, and renal involvement without glomerulonephritis (no red cell casts) are classic features. PAN characteristically spares the glomeruli and pulmonary vasculature. Approximately 30% of PAN cases are associated with hepatitis B infection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) is a small-vessel vasculitis associated with c-ANCA (PR3-ANCA) that characteristically involves the upper and lower respiratory tract and kidneys with necrotizing glomerulonephritis. It does not cause microaneurysms on angiography.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Microscopic polyangiitis is a small-vessel vasculitis associated with p-ANCA (MPO-ANCA) that causes rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with red blood cell casts and pulmonary capillaritis. The absence of red cell casts and presence of microaneurysms favor PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis is a small-vessel vasculitis associated with hepatitis C (not B) and presents with palpable purpura, arthralgias, and membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. It does not cause microaneurysms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss) is a small-vessel vasculitis associated with asthma, eosinophilia, and p-ANCA. There is no mention of asthma or eosinophilia in this patient.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with 7 days of high fever unresponsive to antibiotics, bilateral nonexudative conjunctival injection, a polymorphous erythematous rash, erythema and edema of the hands and feet, cervical lymphadenopathy (2.5-cm node), and cracked, erythematous lips with a strawberry tongue. Echocardiography performed at the time of diagnosis is normal. Which of the following is the most serious complication that this child is at risk for if left untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic root abscess with acute aortic regurgitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysm with risk of thrombosis and myocardial infarction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary artery hypertension from pulmonary vasculitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal artery stenosis with renovascular hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cerebral aneurysm with subarachnoid hemorrhage

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis of childhood with a predilection for the coronary arteries. Without treatment, approximately 25% of affected children develop coronary artery aneurysms, which can lead to thrombosis, stenosis, and myocardial infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease has a strong predilection for the coronary arteries. Without treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and aspirin within the first 10 days of illness, approximately 25% of children develop coronary artery aneurysms. These aneurysms can lead to thrombosis and myocardial infarction, coronary artery stenosis, or rupture. With timely IVIG treatment, the rate of coronary aneurysm decreases to approximately 4%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic root abscess is a complication of infective endocarditis, particularly in the setting of prosthetic valve infection. It is not a feature of Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary artery hypertension from vasculitis is not a characteristic complication of Kawasaki disease. Pulmonary vasculitis is more commonly associated with ANCA-associated vasculitides.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Renal artery stenosis is a complication of Takayasu arteritis and fibromuscular dysplasia, not Kawasaki disease. While Kawasaki disease is a medium-vessel vasculitis, its predilection is specifically for the coronary arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cerebral aneurysms with subarachnoid hemorrhage are not a recognized complication of Kawasaki disease. Intracranial aneurysms are typically caused by berry aneurysms at the circle of Willis or infectious (mycotic) aneurysms.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with polyarteritis nodosa presents with severe abdominal pain, bloody stools, and peritoneal signs. Emergent mesenteric angiography shows multiple saccular microaneurysms with areas of vessel narrowing in the superior mesenteric artery branches. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his acute abdomen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Appendiceal perforation with fecal peritonitis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mesenteric ischemia from vasculitis-induced arterial thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Small bowel obstruction from adhesive disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perforated peptic ulcer from corticosteroid use

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis from hepatic cirrhosis

explanation.summary: Gastrointestinal involvement in PAN occurs in approximately 30-50% of patients. Vasculitis of mesenteric arteries causes ischemia, infarction, and potentially bowel perforation from arterial thrombosis at sites of inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN causes necrotizing vasculitis of medium-sized mesenteric arteries, leading to thrombosis at sites of vessel wall inflammation and microaneurysm formation. This results in mesenteric ischemia with bowel infarction, manifesting as severe abdominal pain, bloody stools, and peritoneal signs. GI involvement is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in PAN. The angiographic findings of microaneurysms and vessel narrowing are pathognomonic for PAN.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Appendicitis is not a recognized complication of PAN. The mesenteric angiographic findings of microaneurysms specifically point to vasculitis-related mesenteric ischemia as the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Small bowel obstruction from adhesive disease is unrelated to PAN and would present with distension, vomiting, and absent bowel sounds rather than the bloody stools and microaneurysms seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While corticosteroid-induced peptic ulceration is possible, the mesenteric angiographic findings showing microaneurysms and vessel narrowing are diagnostic of PAN-related mesenteric ischemia, which is the most likely cause in this clinical context.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis occurs in patients with ascites from cirrhosis. While PAN can be associated with hepatitis B, the clinical and angiographic findings point to vasculitis-related mesenteric ischemia rather than SBP.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with polyarteritis nodosa undergoes a biopsy of a palpable subcutaneous nodule on his lower extremity. The pathologist describes the findings as transmural necrotizing inflammation of a medium-sized muscular artery with fibrinoid necrosis. Immunofluorescence studies are negative for immune complex deposition. Which of the following additional histologic features would most strongly support the diagnosis of PAN over other vasculitides?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Leukocytoclastic vasculitis with nuclear debris in postcapillary venules

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltration with extravascular granuloma formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Segmental involvement with coexistence of acute, healing, and healed lesions

choices.label: E
choices.text: IgA-dominant immune complex deposits in vessel walls

explanation.summary: A hallmark histologic feature of PAN is the simultaneous presence of lesions at different stages of development (acute, healing, and healed) within the same vessel or adjacent vessels, reflecting the segmental and recurrent nature of the disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN characteristically shows segmental transmural inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis affecting medium-sized muscular arteries. A distinctive feature is the temporal heterogeneity of lesions, with acute (fibrinoid necrosis, neutrophilic infiltrate), healing (mixed inflammatory cells, fibroblast proliferation), and healed (fibrosis with intimal proliferation) lesions coexisting in the same specimen. This pattern reflects the episodic nature of vessel wall injury and distinguishes PAN from other vasculitides.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Granulomatous inflammation with giant cells is characteristic of giant cell arteritis and Takayasu arteritis (large-vessel vasculitides) and granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), not PAN. PAN is a non-granulomatous necrotizing vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Leukocytoclastic vasculitis in postcapillary venules is characteristic of small-vessel vasculitis (such as IgA vasculitis or cutaneous small-vessel vasculitis), not medium-vessel vasculitis like PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eosinophilic infiltration with extravascular granulomas is characteristic of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome), not PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IgA-dominant immune complex deposits are diagnostic of IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura), a small-vessel vasculitis. PAN is pauci-immune, with immunofluorescence characteristically negative for immune complex deposition.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with 6 days of fever, irritability, bilateral conjunctival injection without exudate, a diffuse maculopapular rash, and swollen, erythematous hands. Cervical lymphadenopathy is notably absent. Laboratory studies reveal a platelet count of 450,000/μL, ESR of 60 mm/h, CRP of 5.5 mg/dL, and albumin of 2.8 g/dL. The treating physician suspects Kawasaki disease but notes that the patient does not meet full diagnostic criteria. Which of the following terms best describes this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Typical Kawasaki disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Incomplete Kawasaki disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kawasaki disease shock syndrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macrophage activation syndrome complicating Kawasaki disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recurrent Kawasaki disease

explanation.summary: Incomplete (atypical) Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when a patient has fever for 5 or more days with fewer than 4 of the 5 classic clinical criteria, but has laboratory or echocardiographic findings supportive of Kawasaki disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Classic Kawasaki disease requires fever for at least 5 days plus at least 4 of 5 clinical criteria: bilateral conjunctival injection, oral mucosal changes, extremity changes, polymorphous rash, and cervical lymphadenopathy. This patient has only 3 criteria (conjunctival injection, rash, extremity changes) with absent lymphadenopathy and no mention of oral changes, but has supportive laboratory findings (elevated ESR, CRP, thrombocytosis, hypoalbuminemia). This constitutes incomplete Kawasaki disease, which still warrants treatment with IVIG to prevent coronary artery aneurysms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Typical Kawasaki disease requires fever for at least 5 days plus at least 4 of the 5 principal clinical features. This patient has only 3 clinical criteria, so the full diagnostic criteria are not met.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Kawasaki disease shock syndrome is a severe manifestation characterized by hemodynamic instability with hypotension or signs of poor perfusion. There is no mention of hemodynamic compromise in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) is a life-threatening complication that can occur with Kawasaki disease, characterized by persistent fever, falling ESR (due to hypofibrinogenemia), markedly elevated ferritin, cytopenias, and hepatosplenomegaly. This patient's presentation does not suggest MAS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Recurrent Kawasaki disease refers to a new episode of Kawasaki disease occurring after complete resolution of a prior episode. This patient has no history of a prior episode.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents with asymmetric weakness in both lower extremities that developed over 2 weeks. Neurologic examination reveals a left foot drop and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right thigh. Nerve conduction studies show an axonal neuropathy affecting individual named nerves in a non-contiguous distribution. Sural nerve biopsy reveals necrotizing vasculitis of epineurial arteries. ANCA testing is negative. Which of the following neurologic patterns best describes this patient's findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mononeuritis multiplex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Symmetric distal polyneuropathy in a stocking-glove distribution

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ascending demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Central demyelination with upper motor neuron signs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autonomic neuropathy with orthostatic hypotension

explanation.summary: Mononeuritis multiplex is the asymmetric involvement of two or more individual peripheral nerves in a non-contiguous distribution. It is a hallmark neurologic manifestation of PAN, caused by vasculitis of the vasa nervorum (epineurial arteries).
explanation.whyCorrect: Mononeuritis multiplex describes the pattern of involvement of two or more individual named peripheral nerves (e.g., peroneal nerve causing foot drop, lateral femoral cutaneous nerve causing meralgia paresthetica). This pattern results from vasculitis of the vasa nervorum, the small arteries supplying peripheral nerves. PAN is one of the most common causes of mononeuritis multiplex, occurring in up to 50-70% of patients. The axonal pattern on nerve conduction studies reflects ischemic nerve injury.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Symmetric distal polyneuropathy in a stocking-glove distribution is seen in metabolic neuropathies such as diabetic neuropathy. The asymmetric involvement of individual named nerves in this patient is distinct from this pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ascending demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy describes Guillain-Barré syndrome, which presents with symmetric ascending weakness, areflexia, and demyelinating features on nerve conduction studies, not the asymmetric axonal pattern seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Central demyelination with upper motor neuron signs would suggest a central nervous system disorder such as multiple sclerosis, not a peripheral nerve vasculitis. The foot drop and sensory findings follow peripheral nerve distributions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autonomic neuropathy with orthostatic hypotension can occur in vasculitis but is not the predominant pattern in this patient. The focal motor and sensory deficits following individual nerve territories define mononeuritis multiplex.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with Kawasaki disease is treated with high-dose intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and aspirin on day 7 of illness. His fever persists 36 hours after IVIG infusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Switch to oral corticosteroids as primary therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate cyclophosphamide for refractory disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Discontinue aspirin and start warfarin anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Administer a second dose of IVIG at 2 g/kg

choices.label: E
choices.text: Observe without additional treatment as fever will self-resolve

explanation.summary: Approximately 10-20% of Kawasaki disease patients do not respond to initial IVIG. IVIG-resistant disease is defined as persistent or recurrent fever at least 36 hours after IVIG completion, and a second dose of IVIG (2 g/kg) is the recommended first step.
explanation.whyCorrect: IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease occurs in 10-20% of patients, defined as persistent or recrudescent fever 36 hours or more after completion of the initial IVIG infusion. The recommended approach is to administer a second dose of IVIG at 2 g/kg. These patients are at higher risk for coronary artery aneurysms, and additional anti-inflammatory therapy (corticosteroids, infliximab) may be considered if fever persists after the second IVIG dose.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While corticosteroids are used in IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease, they are typically considered after failure of a second IVIG dose or in combination with the second IVIG dose, not as a primary replacement for IVIG.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide is not part of the standard treatment algorithm for Kawasaki disease. It is used in severe systemic vasculitides such as ANCA-associated vasculitis or refractory PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin is a key component of Kawasaki disease treatment and should not be discontinued. Warfarin may be considered in patients who develop giant coronary aneurysms, but switching from aspirin to warfarin alone is not appropriate at this stage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation without additional treatment in IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease is inappropriate, as these patients are at significantly higher risk for coronary artery aneurysms. Prompt retreatment is essential.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with hepatitis B-associated polyarteritis nodosa is started on treatment. In addition to immunosuppressive therapy, which of the following adjunctive treatments is most important in the management of this patient's underlying etiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interferon-free direct-acting antiviral agents for hepatitis B

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepatitis B vaccination to boost immune clearance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Liver transplantation to eliminate the viral reservoir

choices.label: D
choices.text: Long-term prophylactic antibiotics to prevent secondary infection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antiviral therapy with lamivudine or entecavir and plasma exchange

explanation.summary: Hepatitis B-associated PAN requires antiviral therapy (lamivudine, entecavir, or tenofovir) to achieve HBV seroconversion, often combined with plasma exchange to remove circulating immune complexes. This approach targets the underlying trigger of the vasculitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hepatitis B-associated PAN, the vasculitis is driven by circulating immune complexes containing HBV antigens. Treatment involves a brief course of corticosteroids to control acute inflammation followed by antiviral therapy (lamivudine, entecavir, or tenofovir) to achieve HBV seroconversion. Plasma exchange is used to rapidly remove circulating immune complexes. This approach, pioneered by Guillevin, has dramatically improved outcomes by addressing the underlying viral trigger rather than relying solely on immunosuppression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) are the standard treatment for hepatitis C, not hepatitis B. Hepatitis B is treated with nucleos(t)ide analogues such as entecavir or tenofovir, or pegylated interferon-alpha.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hepatitis B vaccination stimulates immunity against HBsAg in uninfected individuals. It has no therapeutic role in patients already chronically infected with HBV, as they have already been exposed to the antigen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Liver transplantation is reserved for end-stage liver disease or hepatocellular carcinoma complicating chronic hepatitis B. It is not indicated for the treatment of HBV-associated vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prophylactic antibiotics are not part of the standard management of HBV-associated PAN. While immunosuppressive therapy may increase infection risk, the critical adjunctive treatment is antiviral therapy targeting the underlying HBV infection.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with polyarteritis nodosa develops new-onset hypertension and hematuria. Renal angiography shows multiple microaneurysms of the intrarenal arteries. A renal biopsy is being considered. Which of the following is the most important contraindication to percutaneous renal biopsy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of hematuria indicating active glomerulonephritis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immunosuppressive therapy increasing the risk of infection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated creatinine indicating chronic kidney disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Risk of hemorrhage from biopsy of a microaneurysm-bearing kidney

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypertension requiring treatment before any invasive procedure

explanation.summary: Percutaneous renal biopsy is relatively contraindicated in PAN because the presence of intrarenal microaneurysms significantly increases the risk of hemorrhage. The weakened aneurysmal vessel walls can rupture during the biopsy procedure.
explanation.whyCorrect: In PAN, the medium-sized intrarenal arteries develop microaneurysms due to necrotizing vasculitis weakening the vessel wall. Percutaneous renal biopsy in a kidney with microaneurysms carries a significantly elevated risk of life-threatening hemorrhage, as the biopsy needle may traumatize a fragile aneurysm. This is a well-recognized contraindication. The diagnosis of PAN can typically be made by angiography demonstrating microaneurysms or by biopsy of other affected tissues (skin, nerve, muscle).

whyWrongByChoice.A: PAN typically causes renal infarction and ischemia rather than glomerulonephritis. The hematuria is likely from vascular injury rather than glomerular disease. Regardless, hematuria alone is not a contraindication to renal biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While immunosuppressive therapy does increase infection risk in general, this is not the primary contraindication to renal biopsy in PAN. Standard pre-procedure infection precautions would be sufficient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated creatinine alone is not a contraindication to renal biopsy. In fact, elevated creatinine is one of the main indications for performing renal biopsy in many clinical contexts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypertension should be controlled before biopsy, but this is a manageable concern rather than an absolute contraindication. The specific risk of hemorrhage from microaneurysms is the critical concern unique to PAN.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy treated for Kawasaki disease 3 weeks ago now presents for follow-up. Echocardiography reveals a 6-mm fusiform coronary artery aneurysm of the left anterior descending artery. The child is afebrile and otherwise well. In addition to continued low-dose aspirin, which of the following management strategies is most appropriate for long-term follow-up of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No further cardiac surveillance is needed after aneurysm resolution

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediate surgical coronary artery bypass grafting

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin regardless of aneurysm size

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue aspirin as the acute phase has resolved

choices.label: E
choices.text: Serial echocardiographic monitoring with stress testing at appropriate age

explanation.summary: Children with coronary artery aneurysms from Kawasaki disease require long-term serial echocardiographic monitoring and, as they grow older, stress testing to evaluate for coronary stenosis, as aneurysms may regress, persist, or develop stenotic changes.
explanation.whyCorrect: A 6-mm coronary artery aneurysm is classified as a medium-sized aneurysm. These patients require long-term follow-up with serial echocardiography to monitor aneurysm size and morphology. As the child grows older, stress testing (exercise or pharmacologic) and possibly coronary CT angiography are used to evaluate for stenosis or thrombosis. While small aneurysms may regress within 1-2 years, medium and large aneurysms may persist and carry ongoing risks of thrombosis, stenosis, and myocardial ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Even after apparent aneurysm resolution, the vessel wall may remain abnormal with intimal thickening and impaired vasomotor function. Ongoing surveillance is recommended, as late stenosis can develop at sites of resolved aneurysms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Coronary artery bypass grafting is reserved for patients with documented coronary ischemia from severe stenotic lesions, not for moderate-sized aneurysms without evidence of ischemia. Medical management with antiplatelet therapy is the initial approach.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lifelong warfarin anticoagulation is considered for giant coronary artery aneurysms (>8 mm) due to high thrombotic risk, but is not routinely recommended for medium-sized aneurysms. Low-dose aspirin is the standard antiplatelet therapy for this aneurysm size.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Discontinuing aspirin would be inappropriate in a patient with a coronary artery aneurysm. Low-dose aspirin is continued long-term to reduce the risk of thrombotic complications within the aneurysmal segment.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with testicular pain, fever, and weight loss. He has a history of hepatitis B infection. Physical examination reveals a tender, swollen left testicle. ANCA testing is negative. Testicular biopsy reveals necrotizing vasculitis of medium-sized arteries with fibrinoid necrosis. Which of the following organs is characteristically spared in this patient's disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Kidneys

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peripheral nerves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: D
choices.text: Skin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lungs

explanation.summary: Polyarteritis nodosa characteristically spares the pulmonary vasculature. Absence of lung involvement is an important distinguishing feature from ANCA-associated vasculitides and other systemic vasculitides.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN affects medium-sized muscular arteries in virtually any organ but characteristically spares the lungs. This is an important distinguishing feature from microscopic polyangiitis and granulomatosis with polyangiitis, both of which frequently involve the pulmonary vasculature. PAN also characteristically spares the glomeruli (affecting renal arcuate and interlobular arteries but not glomerular capillaries). The absence of pulmonary and glomerular involvement is a key diagnostic criterion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The kidneys are frequently involved in PAN, but through vasculitis of the renal arcuate and interlobular arteries causing renal infarction and ischemia rather than glomerulonephritis. Renal involvement occurs in up to 70% of PAN patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Peripheral nerves are among the most commonly affected organs in PAN, with mononeuritis multiplex occurring in 50-70% of patients due to vasculitis of the vasa nervorum.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The gastrointestinal tract is involved in 30-50% of PAN patients. Mesenteric artery vasculitis causes abdominal pain, bowel ischemia, and potentially perforation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Skin involvement is common in PAN, manifesting as subcutaneous nodules along arteries, livedo reticularis, ulcerations, and digital gangrene.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with biopsy-confirmed polyarteritis nodosa involving the kidneys, peripheral nerves, and gastrointestinal tract is started on induction therapy. Her five-factor score (FFS) is calculated at 2 based on renal insufficiency (creatinine 1.8 mg/dL) and gastrointestinal involvement. Which of the following induction regimens is most appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroids alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Methotrexate monotherapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rituximab plus low-dose prednisone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Azathioprine alone as first-line induction therapy

explanation.summary: The five-factor score (FFS) guides treatment intensity in PAN. An FFS of 1 or greater indicates poor prognosis and warrants induction therapy with high-dose corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide.
explanation.whyCorrect: The five-factor score (FFS) was developed by the French Vasculitis Study Group to guide treatment in PAN. The score includes proteinuria >1 g/day, renal insufficiency (creatinine >1.58 mg/dL), cardiomyopathy, GI involvement, and CNS involvement. An FFS of 0 indicates milder disease manageable with corticosteroids alone, while an FFS of 1 or greater indicates the need for combined corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide as induction therapy. This patient's FFS of 2 clearly warrants aggressive combined therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High-dose corticosteroids alone are appropriate for PAN patients with an FFS of 0, indicating milder disease. With an FFS of 2, the patient has poor prognostic features requiring the addition of cyclophosphamide.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methotrexate is not used for induction therapy in severe PAN. It may have a role in maintenance therapy or in mild, non-life-threatening vasculitis, but is insufficient for this degree of organ involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rituximab is established as induction therapy for ANCA-associated vasculitis but does not have the same evidence base for PAN. Cyclophosphamide remains the standard cytotoxic agent for severe PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Azathioprine is a maintenance therapy agent used after successful induction to maintain remission. It is not potent enough for induction therapy in severe PAN.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of bloody nasal discharge, recurrent sinusitis, and hemoptysis. He now develops rapidly progressive renal failure with an active urinary sediment showing dysmorphic red blood cells and red blood cell casts. Chest CT reveals bilateral pulmonary nodules with cavitation. Serum testing reveals positive c-ANCA with anti-proteinase 3 (PR3) antibodies. Renal biopsy shows segmental necrotizing glomerulonephritis with crescent formation and pauci-immune immunofluorescence. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Polyarteritis nodosa

explanation.summary: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener granulomatosis) is an ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis characterized by the triad of upper respiratory tract involvement, lower respiratory tract involvement (cavitating pulmonary nodules), and pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis, with c-ANCA/PR3-ANCA positivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: GPA classically presents with the triad of upper respiratory tract disease (sinusitis, nasal septal destruction, saddle nose deformity), lower respiratory tract disease (pulmonary nodules, cavitation, hemorrhage), and renal disease (pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis). c-ANCA directed against proteinase 3 (PR3) is highly specific for GPA, found in approximately 90% of patients with active generalized disease. The pauci-immune pattern on immunofluorescence distinguishes ANCA-associated vasculitides from immune complex-mediated diseases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease) causes pulmonary hemorrhage and crescentic glomerulonephritis, but renal biopsy shows linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence, not a pauci-immune pattern. Anti-GBM antibodies would be positive, not c-ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Microscopic polyangiitis is also an ANCA-associated pauci-immune vasculitis but is associated with p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA rather than c-ANCA/PR3-ANCA. It typically lacks the granulomatous upper and lower respiratory tract involvement (sinusitis, cavitating nodules) seen in GPA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgA vasculitis presents with palpable purpura, arthralgias, abdominal pain, and glomerulonephritis with mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence. It primarily affects children and does not cause cavitating pulmonary nodules or positive ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis that is ANCA-negative. It characteristically spares the lungs and glomeruli, which distinguishes it from GPA. PAN does not cause crescentic glomerulonephritis or cavitating pulmonary nodules.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with fatigue, arthralgias, and dark urine. She has a 1-week history of palpable purpura on her lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL (baseline 0.9), urinalysis showing red blood cell casts, and positive p-ANCA with anti-myeloperoxidase (MPO) antibodies. Chest radiograph shows bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates, and she develops hemoptysis. Bronchoalveolar lavage reveals hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease

explanation.summary: Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) is a p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA-associated small-vessel vasculitis that presents with pauci-immune necrotizing glomerulonephritis and pulmonary capillaritis (causing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage), without granulomatous inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: MPA is characterized by necrotizing vasculitis of small vessels (capillaries, venules, arterioles) without granulomatous inflammation. It commonly presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RBC casts indicating glomerular disease) and pulmonary capillaritis causing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (hemoptysis, hemosiderin-laden macrophages). p-ANCA with anti-MPO antibodies is found in approximately 60-70% of MPA cases. Unlike GPA, MPA lacks upper respiratory tract granulomatous disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis presents with late-onset asthma, peripheral eosinophilia (>10%), and extravascular eosinophilic granulomas. There is no mention of asthma or eosinophilia in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: GPA is associated with c-ANCA/PR3-ANCA (not p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA) and characteristically involves the upper respiratory tract with granulomatous inflammation (sinusitis, nasal destruction) and cavitating pulmonary nodules. This patient has diffuse alveolar hemorrhage without upper respiratory tract involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis presents with palpable purpura, arthralgias, and glomerulonephritis, but is associated with cryoglobulins, low complement levels, and hepatitis C, not ANCA positivity. Immunofluorescence shows immune complex deposits, not a pauci-immune pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-GBM disease causes diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and crescentic glomerulonephritis (pulmonary-renal syndrome), but is characterized by anti-GBM antibodies and linear IgG deposits on renal biopsy, not ANCA positivity with a pauci-immune pattern.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 5-day history of palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower extremities, colicky abdominal pain, and bilateral knee and ankle pain. Urinalysis reveals microscopic hematuria and mild proteinuria. Skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis of dermal small vessels. Immunofluorescence microscopy of the biopsy is most likely to reveal which of the following patterns?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Linear IgG deposits along the dermal basement membrane

choices.label: B
choices.text: Granular IgA deposits in the walls of dermal blood vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pauci-immune pattern with no immunoglobulin deposition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Granular IgG and C3 deposits in a membranous pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diffuse IgM deposits in a mesangial distribution

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura) is a small-vessel vasculitis defined by predominant IgA deposits in the walls of affected vessels. The diagnostic hallmark on immunofluorescence is granular IgA deposition in dermal vessel walls.
explanation.whyCorrect: IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schönlein purpura) is the most common systemic vasculitis in children. It presents with the classic tetrad of palpable purpura (lower extremities/buttocks), arthralgias, abdominal pain, and renal involvement (hematuria, proteinuria). The defining diagnostic feature is predominant IgA immune complex deposits in the walls of small vessels on immunofluorescence. Skin biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis with granular IgA deposits, and renal biopsy (if performed) shows mesangial IgA deposits identical to IgA nephropathy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Linear IgG deposits along basement membranes are characteristic of anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome), not IgA vasculitis. This pattern reflects autoantibodies directed against type IV collagen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A pauci-immune pattern (absence of significant immunoglobulin deposition) is characteristic of ANCA-associated vasculitides (GPA, MPA, EGPA), not IgA vasculitis, which is an immune complex-mediated vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granular IgG and C3 deposits in a membranous pattern are seen in membranous nephropathy, not IgA vasculitis. Membranous nephropathy presents with nephrotic syndrome from subepithelial immune complex deposits.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diffuse IgM mesangial deposits are not characteristic of IgA vasculitis. IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin deposited, and the distribution in the skin is along vessel walls, not in a mesangial pattern.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with a 15-year history of asthma presents with worsening asthma control, new-onset paresthesias in the left foot, and palpable purpura on the lower legs. Laboratory studies reveal a peripheral eosinophil count of 4,200/μL (normal <500), positive p-ANCA with anti-MPO antibodies, and an elevated serum IgE level. Nerve conduction studies confirm left peroneal nerve mononeuropathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypereosinophilic syndrome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia

explanation.summary: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss syndrome) is a small-vessel vasculitis characterized by late-onset or worsening asthma, marked peripheral eosinophilia, and systemic vasculitis affecting skin and peripheral nerves.
explanation.whyCorrect: EGPA classically evolves through three phases: an allergic phase (asthma, allergic rhinitis), an eosinophilic phase (peripheral eosinophilia >10% or >1,500/μL, tissue eosinophilia), and a vasculitic phase (small-vessel necrotizing vasculitis with mononeuritis multiplex, palpable purpura, cardiac involvement). The combination of longstanding asthma, marked eosinophilia (4,200/μL), p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA positivity, mononeuropathy, and purpura is classic for EGPA. p-ANCA is found in approximately 40-60% of EGPA patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) causes asthma exacerbation with eosinophilia and elevated IgE, but it is a hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus without systemic vasculitis. It does not cause mononeuropathy or palpable purpura.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypereosinophilic syndrome causes marked eosinophilia with organ damage but is not associated with ANCA positivity, vasculitis, or the classic asthma-eosinophilia-vasculitis progression of EGPA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Microscopic polyangiitis can cause pauci-immune glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage with p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA, but it is not associated with asthma, eosinophilia, or the clinical evolution characteristic of EGPA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia causes pulmonary infiltrates with peripheral eosinophilia but does not cause systemic vasculitis, mononeuropathy, or ANCA positivity.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with hepatitis C infection presents with palpable purpura on the lower extremities, arthralgias, and peripheral neuropathy. Laboratory studies reveal positive rheumatoid factor, low C4 complement level, and positive serum cryoglobulins (type II mixed cryoglobulinemia). Renal biopsy shows membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis with intraluminal hyaline thrombi. Which of the following best describes the composition of the cryoglobulins in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monoclonal IgM only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Polyclonal IgG and polyclonal IgM only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monoclonal IgG only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Monoclonal IgM with rheumatoid factor activity plus polyclonal IgG

choices.label: E
choices.text: Polyclonal IgA with secretory component

explanation.summary: Type II mixed cryoglobulinemia consists of monoclonal IgM (with rheumatoid factor activity, i.e., directed against the Fc portion of IgG) complexed with polyclonal IgG. It is strongly associated with chronic hepatitis C infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type II mixed cryoglobulinemia is the most common type associated with hepatitis C. It contains a monoclonal IgM component (usually with rheumatoid factor activity, meaning it binds the Fc region of IgG) complexed with polyclonal IgG. These immune complexes precipitate in cold temperatures and deposit in small vessels, causing vasculitis. The positive rheumatoid factor reflects the anti-IgG activity of the monoclonal IgM component. Low C4 reflects complement consumption by immune complexes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Monoclonal immunoglobulin only (Type I cryoglobulinemia) consists of a single monoclonal immunoglobulin and is associated with hematologic malignancies such as Waldenström macroglobulinemia or multiple myeloma, not hepatitis C.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Polyclonal IgG and polyclonal IgM (Type III mixed cryoglobulinemia) contains no monoclonal component and is associated with autoimmune diseases and chronic infections. This patient's type II designation specifies a monoclonal IgM component.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Monoclonal IgG alone would constitute Type I cryoglobulinemia, associated with multiple myeloma. It does not have rheumatoid factor activity and is not the type associated with hepatitis C.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Polyclonal IgA with secretory component is not a recognized cryoglobulin type. IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin in IgA vasculitis, not cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with newly diagnosed granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, pulmonary hemorrhage, and constitutional symptoms. His creatinine is 4.5 mg/dL and rising. c-ANCA with anti-PR3 antibodies is strongly positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate induction therapy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroids alone with monitoring

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate plus low-dose prednisone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rituximab or cyclophosphamide plus high-dose corticosteroids

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mycophenolate mofetil plus low-dose corticosteroids

choices.label: E
choices.text: Azathioprine plus high-dose corticosteroids

explanation.summary: Severe, organ-threatening GPA requires aggressive induction therapy with either rituximab or cyclophosphamide combined with high-dose corticosteroids. Both agents have been shown to be equally effective in major clinical trials (RAVE, RITUXVAS).
explanation.whyCorrect: For severe GPA with life-threatening organ involvement (rapidly progressive GN, pulmonary hemorrhage), induction therapy consists of high-dose corticosteroids (including possible pulse IV methylprednisolone) combined with either rituximab (375 mg/m² weekly x 4 doses or two 1-g doses) or cyclophosphamide (oral daily or IV pulse). The RAVE and RITUXVAS trials demonstrated that rituximab is non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for remission induction in ANCA-associated vasculitis. Plasma exchange may be considered for severe renal failure or pulmonary hemorrhage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High-dose corticosteroids alone are insufficient for severe GPA with organ-threatening disease. Without an additional immunosuppressive agent, the risk of progressive renal failure and death is high.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate with low-dose prednisone is appropriate only for limited, non-organ-threatening GPA (e.g., isolated upper respiratory tract disease without renal involvement). It is inadequate for this severe presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mycophenolate mofetil has shown inferior efficacy compared to cyclophosphamide for induction therapy in ANCA-associated vasculitis (MYCYC trial) and is not recommended as first-line induction for severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Azathioprine is used as maintenance therapy after successful induction with cyclophosphamide or rituximab. It is not potent enough for induction in severe GPA with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with granulomatosis with polyangiitis presents with progressive nasal crusting and epistaxis. On examination, he has a saddle nose deformity. Nasal endoscopy reveals septal perforation with granulomatous tissue. Biopsy of the nasal tissue is most likely to show which of the following histopathologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with geographic necrosis and vasculitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Non-necrotizing sarcoid-like granulomas with asteroid bodies

choices.label: D
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltration with Charcot-Leyden crystals

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma

explanation.summary: The histopathologic hallmark of GPA in the upper respiratory tract is necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with geographic (serpiginous) necrosis and necrotizing vasculitis of small vessels.
explanation.whyCorrect: GPA in the upper respiratory tract shows a triad of histopathologic features: necrotizing granulomatous inflammation, geographic (irregular, map-like) areas of necrosis, and necrotizing vasculitis of small and medium vessels. The granulomas are non-caseating but show prominent necrosis in irregular patterns. These features, combined with the clinical context and positive c-ANCA/PR3-ANCA, are diagnostic. The nasal septal destruction (saddle nose) results from this destructive granulomatous process.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells are characteristic of tuberculosis. While GPA granulomas can contain giant cells, they show geographic necrosis rather than the caseous (cheese-like) necrosis of TB. Acid-fast staining and cultures would distinguish these entities.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Non-necrotizing sarcoid-like granulomas are characteristic of sarcoidosis. GPA granulomas are specifically necrotizing, and the presence of vasculitis further distinguishes GPA from sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Eosinophilic infiltration with Charcot-Leyden crystals is characteristic of EGPA (Churg-Strauss syndrome) or other eosinophilic conditions, not the granulomatous necrotizing pattern of GPA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MALT lymphoma can cause nasal masses but is a lymphoproliferative disorder, not a vasculitis. It lacks the granulomatous inflammation and vasculitis characteristic of GPA.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with recurrent oral aphthous ulcers, painful genital ulcers, and a history of anterior uveitis. He also reports tender erythematous nodules on his shins. When a blood draw is performed, a pustular lesion develops at the venipuncture site 48 hours later. Which of the following best describes this skin reactivity phenomenon?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Koebner phenomenon

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pathergy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nikolsky sign

choices.label: D
choices.text: Auspitz sign

choices.label: E
choices.text: Darier sign

explanation.summary: Pathergy is an exaggerated skin response to minor trauma (such as a needle prick) that results in pustule formation at the site. It is a characteristic feature of Behçet disease and is used as a diagnostic criterion.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has Behçet disease (recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, uveitis, erythema nodosum). Pathergy refers to the development of a papule or pustule at the site of minor skin trauma (e.g., needle prick) within 24-48 hours. A pathergy test is performed by pricking the forearm with a sterile needle and observing for pustule formation. It is included in the International Criteria for Behçet Disease and is more common in patients from Middle Eastern and Asian populations. Behçet disease is a variable-vessel vasculitis that can affect vessels of any size.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Koebner phenomenon (isomorphic response) describes the development of skin lesions at sites of trauma in patients with psoriasis, lichen planus, or vitiligo. It produces the same type of lesion as the underlying condition, not a non-specific pustule.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The Nikolsky sign is the detachment of superficial epidermis with lateral pressure on apparently normal skin, seen in pemphigus vulgaris and staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome. It is not related to the pustular response seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The Auspitz sign refers to pinpoint bleeding when psoriatic scales are removed, exposing the dermal papillae. It is specific to psoriasis and unrelated to trauma-induced pustule formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Darier sign describes the urtication (wheal and erythema) that occurs when a mastocytoma or urticaria pigmentosa lesion is rubbed, releasing histamine from mast cells. It is not related to Behçet disease.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with newly diagnosed microscopic polyangiitis achieves remission after 6 months of induction therapy with rituximab and corticosteroids. His creatinine has stabilized at 1.4 mg/dL, and his p-ANCA titer has decreased significantly. Which of the following is the most appropriate maintenance therapy strategy to prevent relapse?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discontinue all immunosuppressive therapy and monitor ANCA titers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Continue high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide maintenance therapy for 5 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole as sole maintenance agent

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rituximab maintenance dosing every 6 months for at least 2 years

explanation.summary: After successful induction with rituximab, maintenance therapy with scheduled rituximab infusions (typically every 6 months for at least 18-24 months) is recommended to prevent relapse in ANCA-associated vasculitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The MAINRITSAN trial demonstrated that rituximab maintenance therapy (500 mg every 6 months) is superior to azathioprine for preventing relapse in ANCA-associated vasculitis after cyclophosphamide induction. Subsequent studies (RITAZAREM) showed similar benefit after rituximab induction. Maintenance rituximab is typically continued for at least 18-24 months, though the optimal duration remains under investigation. This approach significantly reduces relapse rates compared to azathioprine or discontinuation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Discontinuing all immunosuppressive therapy after achieving remission carries a high risk of relapse. ANCA-associated vasculitides have relapse rates of 50% or higher without maintenance therapy, particularly GPA with PR3-ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Indefinite high-dose corticosteroids are not appropriate for maintenance due to serious adverse effects including osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts, adrenal suppression, and increased infection risk. The goal is to taper corticosteroids while maintaining remission with a steroid-sparing agent.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Long-term cyclophosphamide maintenance is not recommended due to cumulative toxicity including bladder cancer, bone marrow suppression, infertility, and increased risk of myelodysplastic syndrome. Cyclophosphamide is limited to induction (typically 3-6 months).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may have a role in preventing upper respiratory tract relapses in GPA, and is used for Pneumocystis prophylaxis during immunosuppression, but it is not sufficient as a sole maintenance agent for systemic ANCA-associated vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl with IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura) presents with worsening proteinuria (2.5 g/day) and microscopic hematuria 4 weeks after the initial episode of palpable purpura and abdominal pain. Her serum creatinine remains normal. Renal biopsy shows mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis. Immunofluorescence microscopy reveals the predominant immunoglobulin deposit. This finding is identical to the immunofluorescence pattern seen in which of the following renal diseases?

choices.label: A
choices.text: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Minimal change disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Membranous nephropathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis

explanation.summary: The renal lesion of IgA vasculitis is histologically and immunopathologically identical to IgA nephropathy (Berger disease), with predominant mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence. Both conditions are considered part of the spectrum of IgA-mediated disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The renal involvement in IgA vasculitis shows mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis with predominant mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence, which is indistinguishable from IgA nephropathy (Berger disease). Both conditions are thought to share a common pathogenesis involving aberrantly glycosylated IgA1 and its deposition in the glomerular mesangium. The distinction between the two relies on clinical context: IgA vasculitis has systemic features (purpura, abdominal pain, arthralgias) while IgA nephropathy presents primarily with hematuria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Minimal change disease shows no significant deposits on immunofluorescence and no changes on light microscopy. Electron microscopy shows podocyte foot process effacement only.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Membranous nephropathy shows granular IgG and C3 deposits along the glomerular basement membrane in a subepithelial pattern, not mesangial IgA deposits. It presents with nephrotic syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis shows segmental sclerosis with hyalinosis on light microscopy, and immunofluorescence typically shows IgM and C3 trapping in sclerotic areas, not mesangial IgA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis shows granular IgG and C3 deposits in a subepithelial 'starry sky' or 'humps' pattern, with diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis, not mesangial IgA deposition.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) presents with progressive dyspnea, lower extremity edema, and an elevated troponin I. Echocardiography reveals reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with global hypokinesis. Endomyocardial biopsy shows eosinophilic infiltration of the myocardium. Which of the following best explains why cardiac involvement is the leading cause of mortality in EGPA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysm formation leading to myocardial infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eosinophilic myocarditis and endomyocardial fibrosis causing heart failure and arrhythmias

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aortic root vasculitis causing acute aortic dissection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension from chronic pulmonary vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pericardial tamponade from hemorrhagic pericarditis

explanation.summary: Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death in EGPA, primarily through eosinophilic myocarditis causing cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure, and fatal arrhythmias, followed by endomyocardial fibrosis (Löffler endocarditis).
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac disease accounts for approximately 50% of deaths in EGPA. The primary mechanisms are eosinophilic infiltration of the myocardium causing acute myocarditis with heart failure, and subsequent endomyocardial fibrosis (Löffler endocarditis) leading to restrictive cardiomyopathy. Eosinophilic granule proteins (major basic protein, eosinophil cationic protein) are directly cardiotoxic, causing myocyte necrosis. Additionally, eosinophilic infiltration of the conduction system can cause fatal arrhythmias. This patient's presentation with reduced ejection fraction and positive troponin reflects active eosinophilic myocarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary artery aneurysms are characteristic of Kawasaki disease and polyarteritis nodosa, not EGPA. The cardiac involvement in EGPA is primarily through direct eosinophilic myocardial infiltration rather than coronary artery disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aortic root vasculitis and dissection are associated with large-vessel vasculitides (Takayasu arteritis, GCA), not EGPA, which is a small-vessel vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While EGPA involves the lungs (asthma, eosinophilic infiltrates), chronic pulmonary hypertension is not the primary mechanism of cardiac death. The direct eosinophilic myocardial damage is far more significant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While pericardial involvement can occur in EGPA, hemorrhagic pericarditis with tamponade is rare and not the leading cause of cardiac mortality. Myocarditis and endomyocardial fibrosis are the predominant mechanisms.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary hemorrhage. Serologic testing reveals both positive anti-GBM antibodies and positive p-ANCA with anti-MPO antibodies. Renal biopsy shows crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane and focal areas of pauci-immune necrotizing vasculitis. Which of the following best characterizes this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pure anti-GBM disease without ANCA-associated features

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pure microscopic polyangiitis without anti-GBM involvement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Double-positive (overlap) disease with features of both anti-GBM disease and ANCA-associated vasculitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: IgA vasculitis with incidental ANCA positivity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lupus nephritis with false-positive anti-GBM and ANCA results

explanation.summary: Approximately 10-30% of patients with anti-GBM disease also have positive ANCA (usually MPO-ANCA). This 'double-positive' overlap syndrome has features of both diseases on biopsy and may have a higher relapse rate than pure anti-GBM disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Double-positive disease (concurrent anti-GBM antibodies and ANCA) occurs in approximately 10-30% of anti-GBM disease patients and 5-10% of ANCA-associated vasculitis patients. The renal biopsy characteristically shows features of both diseases: linear IgG deposits along the GBM (anti-GBM disease) and focal necrotizing pauci-immune vasculitis (ANCA-associated vasculitis). These patients require aggressive treatment addressing both pathologies and may have a higher relapse risk than pure anti-GBM disease (which rarely relapses), likely driven by the ANCA-associated component.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pure anti-GBM disease would show only linear IgG deposits without the focal pauci-immune necrotizing vasculitis seen on this biopsy. The positive p-ANCA/MPO-ANCA and the pauci-immune vasculitic component indicate an overlap syndrome, not pure anti-GBM disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pure MPA would show pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis without linear IgG deposits. The presence of both linear IgG staining and anti-GBM antibodies indicates anti-GBM disease contributing to the pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgA vasculitis shows mesangial IgA deposits, not linear IgG deposits or pauci-immune vasculitis. It is not associated with anti-GBM antibodies or ANCA positivity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lupus nephritis shows 'full house' immunofluorescence (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q) and positive ANA/anti-dsDNA antibodies. The linear IgG pattern and positive anti-GBM/ANCA serology are not features of lupus, and the serologic results are not false positives in this clinical context.
